                                    * stale items;
                                    * pending outputs;
                                    * recommended next action from last time.
Step 3 — Ask whether anything changed
The platform should ask a simple question first:
“Has anything important changed since you were last here?”
Options:
                                    * My finances changed.
                                    * My available time changed.
                                    * My idea changed.
                                    * My target customer changed.
                                    * I spoke to people or gathered evidence.
                                    * I got paying customers or users.
                                    * I lost interest or motivation.
                                    * I found a mentor or partner.
                                    * The market or competitors changed.
                                    * Nothing important changed.
                                    * I am not sure.
Step 4 — Run targeted re-checks
Based on absence length and user responses, the platform asks only the necessary re-check questions. It should not force a full restart unless truly needed.
Step 5 — Update statuses
The platform updates relevant scores, blockers, outputs, and tasks.
Documents may be marked:
                                    * current;
                                    * needs review;
                                    * outdated;
                                    * blocked;
                                    * superseded;
                                    * archived.
Step 6 — Provide re-entry recommendation
The platform gives one recommended next action.
Examples:
                                    * “Continue Phase 3 where you left off.”
                                    * “Redo the target-customer section because your idea changed.”
                                    * “Update your financial runway before proceeding.”
                                    * “Your market research is outdated; refresh competitor analysis.”
                                    * “You gained paying customers; move to operations cleanup.”
                                    * “Your runway dropped; switch to low-capital validation.”
Re-entry output
After the re-entry check, the user should receive a Re-entry Snapshot.
This should include:
                                    1. Where you left off
Current phase, last action, open task.
                                    2. What changed
User-reported and system-detected changes.
                                    3. What still appears valid
Stable assumptions and current outputs.
                                    4. What needs review
Stale documents, expired assumptions, missed tasks.
                                    5. Updated blockers
New, removed, or unchanged blockers.
                                    6. Recommended next action
The highest-priority next step.
                                    7. What not to do yet
Any actions that should remain paused.
Staleness rules
The platform should apply staleness rules to time-sensitive information.
Information type
	Suggested freshness rule
	Financial runway
	Re-check after 28 days of inactivity, or sooner if user reports change.
	Time availability
	Re-check after 28 days.
	Market research
	Light review after 60–90 days; deeper review after 180 days.
	Competitor analysis
	Review after 90 days, sooner in fast-moving sectors.
	Legal/regulatory assumptions
	Review after 90–180 days or whenever venture type/location changes.
	Pricing assumptions
	Review after 60–90 days or after new customer evidence.
	Customer interview evidence
	Remains useful, but context should be age-labeled after 90 days.
	Financial model
	Review after 30–60 days if assumptions depend on current costs or revenue.
	Pitch deck
	Review before external sharing if older than 30–60 days.
	Task roadmap
	Review after any absence longer than 14 days.
	Founder values and skills
	Usually stable, but may be updated voluntarily.
	Personal motivation
	Re-check after 28+ days or after major life events.
	These rules should be configurable by sector. For example, AI software markets may become stale faster than local gardening services.
What should not be re-evaluated unnecessarily
The platform should not annoy users by constantly re-checking stable information.
Usually stable unless the user says otherwise:
                                       * name;
                                       * background history;
                                       * long-term values;
                                       * previous experience;
                                       * completed learning;
                                       * previous validation evidence;
                                       * old decisions and why they were made;
                                       * archived ideas;
                                       * skill history;
                                       * founder type history;
                                       * preferred tone;
                                       * previously approved documents, unless assumptions changed.
The platform should not ask the user to re-answer stable questions merely because time passed.
Different return scenarios
Scenario 1 — User returns after 2 weeks
The platform should simply remind them where they left off and ask whether anything important changed. If not, continue.
Example:
“You were working on customer discovery. Your next task was to contact 10 potential users. Do you want to continue that task or report what happened?”
Scenario 2 — User returns after 2 months
The platform should run a standard re-entry check.
Example:
“Because it has been more than 28 days, I need to quickly check your runway, available time, and whether you gathered any new evidence before we continue.”
Scenario 3 — User returns after 6 months
The platform should treat market and personal assumptions as potentially stale.
Example:
“A lot may have changed in six months. I still have your previous work, but I recommend refreshing financial runway, time availability, competitor assumptions, and your current motivation before using old outputs.”
Scenario 4 — User returns with new paying customers
The platform should not force them back into old tasks. It should update the route.
Example:
“This changes your stage. You now have stronger evidence of demand. I recommend moving from market validation into operations and pricing cleanup.”
Scenario 5 — User returns after financial situation worsened
The platform should reroute to stabilization.
Example:
“Your runway has dropped below the safe threshold. I recommend pausing paid business-development steps and switching to low-capital validation until income is stabilized.”
Scenario 6 — User returns with a changed idea
The platform should preserve the old idea but create a change-impact summary.
Example:
“This is a major idea change. Your previous market research and business pillars may no longer apply. I recommend returning to Phase 2 and preserving the previous work as archived learning.”
Tone for returning users
The platform should not shame users for absence. It should not say:
                                       * “You failed to return.”
                                       * “You missed your deadline.”
                                       * “You are behind.”
                                       * “You lost progress.”
Better tone:
                                       * “Welcome back.”
                                       * “Here is where we left off.”
                                       * “Let’s check what changed.”
                                       * “Some assumptions may need refreshing.”
                                       * “Your previous work is preserved.”
                                       * “The next responsible step is…”
The tone should be calm, professional, direct, and forward-looking.
Relationship to reminders and accountability
If the user has agreed to accountability reminders, the platform may reference missed tasks, but it should do so constructively.
Example:
“Your customer-discovery task was due 5 weeks ago. That is not a problem, but we should decide whether this task is still relevant, should be adjusted, or should be replaced.”
The purpose is to resume responsibility, not to create guilt.
Developer-facing requirements
The system should support:
                                       * last-seen tracking;
                                       * inactivity thresholds;
                                       * stale-data detection;
                                       * output freshness labels;
                                       * re-entry snapshot generation;
                                       * changed-circumstance intake;
                                       * task deadline review;
                                       * evidence update intake;
                                       * blocker recalculation;
                                       * phase rerouting;
                                       * version history;
                                       * user-friendly reactivation flow;
                                       * differentiated re-entry depth based on inactivity duration;
                                       * sector-specific staleness rules;
                                       * optional reminders and accountability preferences.
What the platform should not do
The platform should not:
                                       * force a full restart after absence;
                                       * ignore absence and continue blindly;
                                       * shame the user;
                                       * treat inactivity as failure automatically;
                                       * keep old outputs marked as current if assumptions are stale;
                                       * re-ask stable questions unnecessarily;
                                       * hide that documents may be outdated;
                                       * assume the user did nothing while away;
                                       * assume the venture is still relevant;
                                       * push the old next step if circumstances changed.
Final answer
When a user returns after being away, the platform should remember all stable and structurally useful information: founder profile, venture history, previous answers, evidence, generated documents, tasks, decisions, blockers, and preferences. However, it should re-evaluate time-sensitive and decision-critical information such as financial runway, available time, motivation, market conditions, competitor landscape, validation evidence, legal assumptions, personal constraints, and output freshness. After 28 days of inactivity, the platform should trigger a formal re-entry check; after longer absences, the re-check should become deeper. The user should receive a Re-entry Snapshot showing where they left off, what changed, what remains valid, what needs review, and the most responsible next action.
⭐ Non-negotiable: returning users should not be forced to restart, but the platform must not continue building on stale assumptions.
	 
 
3.7
Is there a point of no return — a moment where the platform locks in a path and the user can no longer change direction?
No. Inside Changepreneurship, there should be no absolute point of no return. The user must always be able to revise answers, change direction, pivot, restart, regenerate outputs, update assumptions, or return to an earlier phase. Venture-building is a learning process, and the platform should not punish users for learning.
However, the platform must make an important distinction between internal reversibility and external consequences.
Inside the platform, almost everything can be changed. Outside the platform, some actions create legal, financial, operational, reputational, or relational consequences that may be difficult or expensive to reverse. The platform should therefore never lock users into a path internally, but it must warn them before they take external actions that create real-world commitment.
The guiding principle is:
The platform should remain flexible, but it must make commitment visible before the user does something that is difficult to undo.
Internal flexibility
Within the platform, the user should be able to change almost anything:
                                       * founder-readiness answers;
                                       * personal goals;
                                       * financial assumptions;
                                       * time availability;
                                       * idea description;
                                       * target customer;
                                       * beneficiary or stakeholder group;
                                       * market segment;
                                       * business model;
                                       * funding model;
                                       * pricing model;
                                       * product concept;
                                       * venture type;
                                       * non-profit vs. for-profit framing;
                                       * pitch materials;
                                       * validation plan;
                                       * mentor needs;
                                       * operational workflows;
                                       * roadmap;
                                       * documents;
                                       * tasks;
                                       * dashboards;
                                       * phase route.
The platform should support this through versioning, change-impact analysis, regeneration, and clear status labels such as current, needs review, outdated, superseded, draft, approved, blocked, or archived.
A user should never feel trapped by an early answer. Early answers are hypotheses, not prison walls.
External irreversibility
Outside the platform, some actions create consequences. These actions are not always impossible to reverse, but they can become costly, slow, legally binding, reputationally sensitive, or emotionally difficult.
Examples include:
                                       * quitting a job;
                                       * incorporating a company;
                                       * signing contracts;
                                       * taking a loan;
                                       * accepting investment;
                                       * borrowing from family or friends;
                                       * hiring employees or contractors;
                                       * signing a lease;
                                       * buying inventory;
                                       * paying for product development;
                                       * making public claims;
                                       * launching publicly;
                                       * collecting customer data;
                                       * entering a regulated market;
                                       * signing partnership agreements;
                                       * accepting donations or grants;
                                       * promising delivery to customers;
                                       * using someone else’s intellectual property;
                                       * making medical, legal, financial, or safety-related claims;
                                       * sending investor materials externally;
                                       * filing legal or tax documents.
The platform should treat these as commitment events.
Commitment events
A commitment event is any user action that increases real-world exposure, cost, legal responsibility, public visibility, or difficulty of reversal.
The platform should detect, warn, and require confirmation before commitment events. It should not necessarily block all of them, but it should ensure the user understands the consequences and has met minimum readiness criteria.
Commitment event
	Why it matters
	Required platform behaviour
	Quitting a job
	Removes income and increases pressure.
	Require financial runway check, survival plan, and validation status review.
	Incorporating a business
	Creates legal/tax obligations.
	Require legal/tax awareness checklist and reason for incorporation.
	Taking a loan
	Creates repayment obligation.
	Require cash-flow model, risk warning, and possibly professional advice.
	Accepting investment
	Creates legal, ownership, and governance consequences.
	Require investor-readiness check and legal review recommendation.
	Hiring people
	Creates cost, management, and legal obligations.
	Require role justification, budget, and workload analysis.
	Signing a lease
	Creates fixed cost and legal obligation.
	Require demand evidence, runway check, and scenario planning.
	Buying inventory
	Creates cash and storage risk.
	Require demand validation, margin check, and small-batch test.
	Paying for product development
	Creates cost before proof.
	Require product validation, MVP scope, and financial runway check.
	Launching publicly
	Creates reputational exposure.
	Require message, claims, compliance, and support-readiness review.
	Collecting personal data
	Creates privacy and compliance duties.
	Require data/privacy checklist.
	Entering regulated sector
	Creates legal/safety obligations.
	Require professional/legal review before proceeding.
	Sending pitch deck externally
	Creates reputational and funding consequences.
	Require output freshness check and investor-readiness review.
	Accepting donations/grants
	Creates accountability and reporting obligations.
	Require funding-use plan, impact metrics, and compliance awareness.
	Commitment warning levels
The platform should use different warning levels based on the seriousness of the commitment.
Warning level
	Meaning
	Example
	Platform behaviour
	Level 1 — Notice
	Low-risk action with minor consequences.
	Changing a document title.
	Inform only.
	Level 2 — Caution
	Reversible action, but may affect later work.
	Changing target customer.
	Show affected outputs and ask for confirmation.
	Level 3 — Strong warning
	Action may cost money, time, or credibility.
	Sending outreach to 100 prospects.
	Require review and confirmation.
	Level 4 — Commitment gate
	Action has legal, financial, operational, or reputational consequences.
	Incorporating, hiring, launching, taking a loan.
	Require readiness checklist and explicit approval.
	Level 5 — Hard block
	Action is unsafe, unlawful, unethical, or unsupported by minimum evidence.
	Taking debt with no repayment path; illegal venture activity.
	Block action or refer to professional help.
	This prevents the platform from treating all changes the same.
Internal point of no return: none
The platform should never say:
                                       * “You cannot change this answer anymore.”
                                       * “You selected this path, so you must continue.”
                                       * “You completed this phase, so earlier phases are locked forever.”
                                       * “You cannot pivot because documents were already generated.”
                                       * “You cannot start a new idea without deleting your previous work.”
Instead, it should say:
                                       * “You can change this, but here is what it affects.”
                                       * “This change will require updates to later outputs.”
                                       * “This document is now outdated.”
                                       * “This pivot creates a new route.”
                                       * “Your previous work will be archived as learning.”
The user remains free to revise. The platform remains responsible for consistency.
External point of no return: commitment gates
The platform should not claim that external decisions are harmless. Instead, it should create commitment gates before high-consequence actions.
A commitment gate should include:
                                       1. Action description
What the user is about to do.
                                       2. Consequence explanation
What may become difficult, costly, or impossible to reverse.
                                       3. Readiness check
Whether the user has enough evidence, runway, legal clarity, operational capacity, or professional support.
                                       4. Risk summary
What could go wrong.
                                       5. Missing evidence
What should ideally be validated first.
                                       6. Alternative safer step
A lower-risk option if the user is not ready.
                                       7. Explicit user confirmation
The user must actively approve proceeding.
                                       8. Professional referral, if needed
Legal, tax, financial, medical, regulatory, or other expert review.
Example:
“You are about to commit to paid product development. This may be difficult to reverse because money will be spent before demand is fully proven. Your current validation evidence is weak, and your runway is only 2 months. I recommend a lower-cost prototype and 10 customer interviews before paying for development. Do you still want to proceed?”
What should be locked, if anything?
The platform should avoid hard locks on answers and phases, but it may lock or protect certain records for traceability.
What may be protected:
                                          * approved external documents already sent;
                                          * signed-off milestone snapshots;
                                          * evidence records;
                                          * decision logs;
                                          * versions used for funding or legal review;
                                          * user approvals;
                                          * consent records;
                                          * exported materials.
These should not be deleted silently because they form part of the audit trail. However, they can be superseded, archived, or marked outdated.
This is not a lock on the user’s future direction. It is protection of historical accuracy.
Handling pivots
A pivot should be treated as a normal part of the journey, not as failure.
The platform should support several pivot types:
Pivot type
	Example
	Platform response
	Customer pivot
	From freelancers to agencies.
	Re-check market research, pricing, outreach, and positioning.
	Problem pivot
	From affordability problem to trust problem.
	Update problem statement and value proposition.
	Solution pivot
	From app to manual service.
	Update product concept, operations, and cost model.
	Business model pivot
	From subscription to licensing.
	Update revenue logic and sales process.
	Venture-type pivot
	From for-profit to non-profit.
	Update funding, governance, language, and metrics.
	Scope pivot
	From broad platform to narrow first use case.
	Update MVP and target segment.
	Founder-role pivot
	From solo founder to co-founder-needed route.
	Update team plan and mentor/co-founder search.
	Market-entry pivot
	From mass market to beachhead niche.
	Update go-to-market and validation plan.
	The platform should preserve old reasoning and show what changed.
Handling complete restarts
If the user wants to start over with a new idea, the platform should allow it.
It should offer:
                                          1. Start a new venture workspace.
                                          2. Preserve the founder profile.
                                          3. Archive the previous idea.
                                          4. Carry over relevant lessons.
                                          5. Reset venture-specific assumptions.
                                          6. Keep previous documents as historical records.
                                          7. Allow comparison between old and new idea if useful.
A restart should not require deleting everything.
Cost and consumption model implications
If the platform uses consumption-based pricing, regeneration and rework must be transparent.
The user should be told when a change may create additional cost.
Example:
“Regenerating the full Business Pillars Blueprint and pitch deck will use additional credits. You can also review the affected sections manually first.”
The platform should not use flexibility as a hidden revenue trap. Users should be free to change, but they should understand cost implications.
What the platform should do before irreversible external actions
Before a user performs a high-consequence external action, the platform should ask:
                                          1. Has the relevant phase been completed?
                                          2. Is the evidence strong enough?
                                          3. Is the user financially safe enough?
                                          4. Are there legal/tax/compliance issues?
                                          5. Is the output current and approved?
                                          6. Has the user considered lower-risk alternatives?
                                          7. Does the user understand the downside?
                                          8. Is professional advice required?
                                          9. Does the user explicitly choose to proceed?
If these checks fail, the platform should either warn, soft-block, hard-block, or refer.
Examples
Example 1 — User wants to change target market
The user changes from “individual consumers” to “municipal governments.”
The platform should not block the change. It should say:
“You can make this change, but it affects your sales cycle, pricing, procurement assumptions, legal requirements, pitch materials, and market research. I recommend re-running Phase 3 and Phase 4 for this new target customer.”
Example 2 — User wants to quit their job
The user says they want to quit their job immediately to work full-time on the idea.
The platform should trigger a commitment gate:
“Quitting your job is a high-consequence decision. Your current runway is 2 months, and your idea has not yet passed market validation. I recommend keeping income while completing customer discovery. I will not mark this as a recommended step.”
Example 3 — User wants to pay a developer
The user wants to spend €8,000 on a prototype.
The platform should check:
                                          * product concept validation;
                                          * financial runway;
                                          * MVP scope;
                                          * cheaper alternatives;
                                          * whether the user has customer evidence.
If evidence is weak, it should recommend a lower-cost test.
Example 4 — User sends an old pitch deck
The user wants to send a pitch deck created three months ago.
The platform should check freshness:
“This pitch deck is based on older assumptions. Since then, your target customer and pricing model changed. I recommend regenerating or reviewing it before sending.”
Example 5 — User wants to accept investment
The user has an interested angel investor.
The platform should not simply celebrate. It should trigger legal and strategic review:
“Accepting investment can affect ownership, control, reporting, and future funding. Before proceeding, you should review valuation, terms, shareholder rights, and legal documentation with a qualified professional.”
What the platform should not do
The platform should not:
                                          * trap users in old answers;
                                          * punish pivots;
                                          * hide consequences of changes;
                                          * silently update major outputs;
                                          * silently use paid credits for regeneration;
                                          * encourage irreversible external commitments prematurely;
                                          * present external commitments as casual next steps;
                                          * allow outdated documents to be sent without warning;
                                          * delete history when a user changes direction;
                                          * confuse internal flexibility with real-world reversibility.
Developer-facing requirements
The system should support:
                                          * internal versioning;
                                          * venture workspaces;
                                          * archive and restart functionality;
                                          * pivot classification;
                                          * commitment-event detection;
                                          * commitment warning levels;
                                          * readiness gates;
                                          * output freshness checks;
                                          * external-sharing warnings;
                                          * explicit approval before major actions;
                                          * professional-referral triggers;
                                          * cost transparency before regeneration;
                                          * audit trail for approved outputs and external commitments;
                                          * historical records that can be superseded but not silently erased.
Final answer
There should be no absolute point of no return inside the platform. Users must always be able to revise answers, redo phases, pivot, regenerate outputs, or start a new venture. However, the platform must distinguish internal flexibility from external consequences. Some real-world actions — such as quitting a job, incorporating, taking loans, hiring, signing contracts, buying inventory, launching publicly, accepting investment, or sending investor materials — create commitments that may be difficult or expensive to reverse. Before those actions, the platform should trigger commitment gates that explain the consequences, check readiness, identify missing evidence, suggest safer alternatives, and require explicit confirmation.
⭐ Non-negotiable: Changepreneurship should never lock users into learning mistakes, but it must warn them before they create real-world consequences that cannot be easily undone.
	 
SECTION 4   THE DECISION ENGINE — HOW SHOULD THE PLATFORM MAKE DECISIONS?
 
The business rules that determine what the platform shows a user at every moment. The most important logic in the product.
4.1
What are all the dimensions the platform should measure about a user?  
The platform should measure the user and the venture through a very large, structured taxonomy. Earlier work expanded the model far beyond the originally mentioned 500 parameters into a much larger post-consolidation taxonomy of 46,958 rows/questions. The uploaded taxonomy dashboard currently organizes this logic across 2 profile phases, 12 assessments, 36 groups, 108 sections, and more than 2,000 clusters, with columns for dependencies, affected outputs, data source, AI extraction logic, danger-zone thresholds, blocking status, compensating dimensions, and explanation logic.
This means Changepreneurship should not rely on a simple founder quiz or a single general readiness score. It should operate as a multi-dimensional decision engine where every answer can affect other parts of the venture model.
However, the full 46,000+ parameter taxonomy should not be shown directly to users. That level of detail is useful for scoring, routing, benchmarking, AI extraction, dependency mapping, and developer logic. Users should experience the taxonomy through clear macro-dimensions, plain-language diagnostics, concrete warnings, and next actions.
The guiding principle is:
The system may measure thousands of micro-signals internally, but the user should see a small number of meaningful dimensions that explain what matters, why it matters, and what to do next.
Taxonomy architecture
The taxonomy should be understood in layers.
Layer
	Purpose
	User visibility
	Micro-parameters
	Individual questions, signals, observations, extracted behaviours, evidence points, thresholds, and dependencies.
	Mostly hidden. Used by scoring and routing logic.
	Clusters
	Groups of related questions around a specific concept, such as resilience, pricing logic, customer validation, technical debt, or market need.
	Sometimes visible as diagnostic sub-areas.
	Sections
	Broader assessment areas within a group.
	Sometimes visible in reports.
	Groups
	Larger themes within an assessment.
	Visible in structured reports and dashboards.
	Assessments
	Major diagnostic modules such as Motivation & Drive, Personality & Work Style, Business Model & Revenue, and Market Opportunity & Demand.
	Visible when useful.
	Macro-dimensions
	The highest-level user-facing dimensions used for dashboards, routing, blockers, and executive summaries.
	Always visible.
	This layered approach allows the platform to be extremely detailed internally without overwhelming the user.
Current taxonomy source structure
The current taxonomy dashboard uses the following high-level fields:
Field
	Meaning for the platform
	Question_ID
	Unique identifier for each question or parameter.
	Phase
	Which major profile phase the question belongs to.
	Assessment
	The diagnostic module the question supports.
	Group
	The thematic group inside the assessment.
	Section
	The narrower category inside the group.
	Cluster
	The detailed concept area being measured.
	Question_Number
	Local sequence number.
	Question_Text
	The user-facing or AI-facing question.
	Inputs (Depends on)
	Which upstream area this question depends on.
	Outputs (Affects)
	Which downstream output this answer influences.
	Data Source
	Where the signal comes from. Current version uses AI chat interaction as the source.
	AI Extraction & Connection Logic
	How the AI extracts meaning and connects it to scores or components.
	Danger Zone Threshold
	The point at which the answer becomes risky.
	Is Blocking?
	Whether a low or dangerous signal blocks progress.
	Compensating Dimensions
	Whether another dimension can offset this weakness, and under what rule.
	Logic / Explanation
	Additional decision logic or reasoning.
	This is valuable because it creates traceability. A developer can see not only the question, but also what it depends on, what it affects, when it becomes dangerous, whether it blocks progress, and whether compensation is possible.
Current major assessment areas
The taxonomy currently contains 12 major assessment areas across the entrepreneur and venture profile layers. These should be treated as the detailed internal assessment modules that feed the larger macro-dimensions.
Assessment area
	What it broadly measures
	Motivation & Drive
	Why the user wants to build, how durable the motivation is, and whether purpose is strong enough to sustain effort.
	Personality & Work Style
	How the user works, decides, communicates, handles structure, and responds to pressure.
	Values, Ethics & Culture
	The user’s ethical boundaries, cultural fit, integrity, and mission alignment.
	Self-Awareness & Growth Mindset
	Whether the user understands their strengths, weaknesses, blind spots, and ability to learn.
	Life Circumstances & Support System
	The user’s time, family, social, geographic, financial, and practical environment.
	Skills, Experience & Leadership
	The user’s relevant capabilities, leadership potential, execution skills, and skill gaps.
	Market Opportunity & Demand
	Whether the market appears to need the idea and whether demand is credible.
	Problem-Solution Fit
	Whether the proposed solution actually addresses a real and meaningful problem.
	Business Model & Revenue
	Whether the venture can capture money, funding, resources, or sustainability.
	Strategic Positioning & Competition
	How the venture compares to alternatives and whether it has a defendable position.
	Market Intelligence & Validation
	The quality of real-world evidence, customer discovery, and validation.
	Technology & Future-Proofing
	Whether the venture’s technology, systems, scalability, and future relevance are strong enough.
	These assessment areas are not separate products. They are inputs into the platform’s decision engine.
User-facing macro-dimensions
For users, the platform should translate the full taxonomy into a smaller set of macro-dimensions. These are the dimensions the user can understand, track, and act on.
1. Personal Readiness
What it measures
Personal Readiness measures whether the person behind the venture is currently stable, disciplined, resilient, and self-aware enough to pursue entrepreneurship responsibly.
Includes
                                          * resilience;
                                          * stress tolerance;
                                          * emotional regulation;
                                          * energy level;
                                          * consistency;
                                          * self-discipline;
                                          * self-reflection;
                                          * openness to feedback;
                                          * patience;
                                          * adaptability;
                                          * confidence without delusion;
                                          * ability to handle uncertainty;
                                          * burnout risk;
                                          * personal stability.
Why it matters
A good idea can still fail if the founder is unstable, exhausted, impulsive, avoidant, or unable to complete difficult tasks. Entrepreneurship requires stamina and reality contact, not only inspiration.
Example user-facing statement
“Your idea has potential, but your current personal capacity appears fragile. Before increasing venture pressure, we need to reduce overload and create a more sustainable work rhythm.”
2. Motivation & Purpose
What it measures
Motivation & Purpose measures why the user wants to build and whether that motivation is strong, healthy, and durable enough to survive difficulty.
Includes
                                          * intrinsic motivation;
                                          * personal mission;
                                          * values alignment;
                                          * purpose clarity;
                                          * impact ambition;
                                          * autonomy desire;
                                          * escape motivation;
                                          * revenge or frustration motivation;
                                          * long-term commitment;
                                          * emotional connection to the problem.
Why it matters
Weak or unhealthy motivation can create impulsive decisions. Strong but unstructured motivation can create overexcitement. The platform must distinguish durable purpose from temporary emotional intensity.
Example user-facing statement
“Your motivation is strong, but it currently appears more connected to escaping your current situation than to a validated market opportunity. We should separate emotional urgency from venture evidence.”
3. Financial Readiness
What it measures
Financial Readiness measures whether the user can safely explore, validate, build, or scale without creating reckless personal risk.
Includes
                                          * income stability;
                                          * personal runway;
                                          * emergency buffer;
                                          * debt pressure;
                                          * monthly obligations;
                                          * dependency responsibilities;
                                          * risk capacity;
                                          * ability to invest;
                                          * spending discipline;
                                          * willingness to use low-capital routes;
                                          * survival plan.
Why it matters
Financial instability can turn a promising idea into a dangerous situation. The platform must prevent users from spending, borrowing, quitting employment, hiring, or building before the financial foundation is safe enough.
Example user-facing statement
“Your financial runway is below the safe threshold for paid product development. You may continue with idea clarification and free validation, but paid development should remain blocked.”
4. Time & Capacity
What it measures
Time & Capacity measures whether the user has enough realistic availability and mental bandwidth to complete the next stage.
Includes
                                          * weekly available hours;
                                          * schedule flexibility;
                                          * work obligations;
                                          * caregiving obligations;
                                          * cognitive load;
                                          * consistency;
                                          * ability to attend calls;
                                          * ability to complete validation tasks;
                                          * energy quality;
                                          * competing priorities.
Why it matters
A user may be highly motivated but unable to act. Without time and energy, the platform should reduce scope, slow the route, or enter preparation mode.
Example user-facing statement
“Your available time is currently too low for active product development. A lower-intensity validation route is more realistic.”
5. Skills, Experience & Execution Capability
What it measures
This dimension measures whether the user has the skills, experience, and execution behaviour required for the current venture stage.
Includes
                                          * domain expertise;
                                          * sales ability;
                                          * communication skill;
                                          * leadership potential;
                                          * technical capability;
                                          * financial literacy;
                                          * operational thinking;
                                          * project management;
                                          * decision-making;
                                          * follow-through;
                                          * ability to complete real-world tasks;
                                          * ability to learn;
                                          * ability to delegate or outsource.
Why it matters
A user does not need to possess every skill personally, but every critical skill must be present, learned, outsourced, automated, or covered by a co-founder, mentor, advisor, or specialist.
Example user-facing statement
“You do not need to become a technical founder yourself, but the current idea requires technical execution capacity. We need either a simpler MVP, a technical partner, or a validated outsourcing plan.”
6. Founder-Idea Fit
What it measures
Founder-Idea Fit measures whether the venture fits the user’s values, personality, risk tolerance, lifestyle, skills, resources, and long-term willingness.
Includes
                                          * values alignment;
                                          * lifestyle fit;
                                          * risk tolerance match;
                                          * motivation durability;
                                          * personal connection to the problem;
                                          * skill fit;
                                          * credibility;
                                          * energy fit;
                                          * willingness to do required activities;
                                          * founder role suitability.
Why it matters
Some ideas are good ideas for the wrong founder. The platform must detect whether the user is suited to this venture or whether they need a co-founder, different role, reduced scope, or different idea.
Example user-facing statement
“The business model requires continuous sales and public outreach, but your answers suggest strong avoidance of those activities. This does not make the idea impossible, but it means the current founder role is mismatched.”
7. Founder-Market Fit
What it measures
Founder-Market Fit measures whether the user has credibility, access, understanding, and trust within the market they want to serve.
Includes
                                          * lived experience;
                                          * professional exposure;
                                          * domain credibility;
                                          * access to customers;
                                          * understanding of customer pain;
                                          * cultural/geographic relevance;
                                          * network access;
                                          * ability to speak the market’s language;
                                          * trust signals;
                                          * distribution access.
Why it matters
A founder can have a promising idea but no credible path into the market. Founder-market fit helps determine whether the user can realistically reach and understand the people they want to serve.
Example user-facing statement
“You understand the problem conceptually, but you currently have no direct access to the people who would buy or use this. The next step is not product design; it is market access.”
8. Idea Clarity
What it measures
Idea Clarity measures whether the user can explain the venture simply, specifically, and coherently.
Includes
                                          * problem definition;
                                          * target user;
                                          * solution description;
                                          * value proposition;
                                          * outcome clarity;
                                          * scope;
                                          * first use case;
                                          * differentiation;
                                          * simple explanation;
                                          * customer/beneficiary/funder distinction.
Why it matters
An unclear idea cannot be tested properly. If the user cannot explain the idea clearly, the platform must route them to clarification before market research, product development, or business planning.
Example user-facing statement
“The idea is still too broad to validate. We need to define one specific problem, one first user group, and one first use case.”
9. Problem-Solution Fit
What it measures
Problem-Solution Fit measures whether the proposed solution actually addresses the problem that matters most to the target audience.
Includes
                                          * pain intensity;
                                          * problem frequency;
                                          * current alternatives;
                                          * solution relevance;
                                          * customer understanding;
                                          * feature-value alignment;
                                          * problem urgency;
                                          * user workflow fit;
                                          * adoption barriers;
                                          * willingness to switch.
Why it matters
Many founders build solutions for problems that are real but not urgent, or solutions that do not match how users actually behave. This dimension prevents product development based only on founder imagination.
Example user-facing statement
“The problem appears real, but your proposed solution may not match how the target user currently tries to solve it. We need to test the solution format before building.”
10. Market Validity & Demand
What it measures
Market Validity & Demand measures whether real people, organizations, beneficiaries, funders, or customers care enough to act.
Includes
                                          * customer discovery;
                                          * demand evidence;
                                          * willingness to pay;
                                          * willingness to fund;
                                          * urgency;
                                          * market size;
                                          * market timing;
                                          * alternatives;
                                          * substitutes;
                                          * competitor strength;
                                          * search behaviour;
                                          * purchase behaviour;
                                          * adoption friction;
                                          * validation quality.
Why it matters
The market must eventually confirm or reject the founder’s assumptions. The platform must not allow internal belief to substitute for external evidence.
Example user-facing statement
“You have a clear idea, but no direct evidence that the target group cares enough to act. Market validation is now the required next step.”
11. Business Model & Financial Logic
What it measures
Business Model & Financial Logic measures whether the venture can sustain itself financially through revenue, funding, donations, grants, sponsorships, licensing, subscriptions, sales, or another credible mechanism.
Includes
                                          * revenue model;
                                          * pricing;
                                          * funding model;
                                          * cost structure;
                                          * gross margin;
                                          * cash-flow timing;
                                          * unit economics;
                                          * break-even logic;
                                          * donor/funder logic;
                                          * grant dependency;
                                          * scalability;
                                          * recurring revenue potential;
                                          * financial sustainability.
Why it matters
A venture can be meaningful and still financially impossible. This dimension ensures that the platform does not help users build something that cannot pay for itself, fund itself, or sustain its mission.
Example user-facing statement
“The mission is strong, but the funding logic is not yet credible. Before building operations, we need to define who pays, why they pay, and how often that funding can repeat.”
12. Strategic Positioning & Competition
What it measures
Strategic Positioning & Competition measures whether the venture has a defendable place in the market or ecosystem.
Includes
                                          * competitor map;
                                          * substitutes;
                                          * differentiation;
                                          * positioning;
                                          * unfair advantage;
                                          * switching costs;
                                          * niche selection;
                                          * beachhead market;
                                          * brand trust;
                                          * market entry strategy;
                                          * defensibility;
                                          * timing.
Why it matters
Users often underestimate alternatives. The real competitor may not be another company; it may be spreadsheets, habits, doing nothing, government services, informal networks, or existing manual workarounds.
Example user-facing statement
“Your stated competition is incomplete. The strongest substitute is not another app; it is the current manual workaround users already trust.”
13. Product / Service Feasibility
What it measures
Product / Service Feasibility measures whether the proposed offer can be built, delivered, tested, maintained, and improved within the user’s capacity and resources.
Includes
                                          * MVP scope;
                                          * technical feasibility;
                                          * operational feasibility;
                                          * delivery complexity;
                                          * quality requirements;
                                          * supplier dependency;
                                          * regulatory requirements;
                                          * development cost;
                                          * maintenance burden;
                                          * customer support needs;
                                          * service consistency;
                                          * product readiness.
Why it matters
A product may be desirable but unrealistic to build or deliver at the current stage. This dimension prevents overbuilding and encourages low-risk testing.
Example user-facing statement
“The full product is too expensive for your current stage. We should test the core value through a manual or low-fidelity version first.”
14. Operational Readiness
What it measures
Operational Readiness measures whether the venture can function without collapsing into chaos.
Includes
                                          * workflows;
                                          * task systems;
                                          * process documentation;
                                          * delivery model;
                                          * scheduling;
                                          * quality control;
                                          * customer support;
                                          * supplier management;
                                          * role clarity;
                                          * automation potential;
                                          * capacity planning;
                                          * reporting;
                                          * system dependencies.
Why it matters
A venture can generate demand and still fail operationally. This is especially important for side-hustlers, local services, consumer products, and users with early traction.
Example user-facing statement
“You already have demand. Your immediate risk is not marketing; it is operational overload and inconsistent delivery.”
15. Network, Mentorship & Support
What it measures
Network, Mentorship & Support measures whether the user has access to people who can help them make better decisions and reduce blind spots.
Includes
                                          * mentor access;
                                          * domain experts;
                                          * founder peers;
                                          * advisors;
                                          * co-founder potential;
                                          * professional network;
                                          * customer access;
                                          * partner access;
                                          * family/partner support;
                                          * board or governance support;
                                          * investor or funder access.
Why it matters
Some gaps cannot be solved by AI alone. A founder may need a domain mentor, commercial co-founder, legal advisor, accountant, board member, or someone with lived market experience.
Example user-facing statement
“This sector is complex enough that you should not proceed without a domain mentor. I recommend finding someone with direct experience in this market before continuing.”
16. Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risk
What it measures
Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risk measures whether the venture can be pursued responsibly and lawfully.
Includes
                                          * regulated-sector exposure;
                                          * licensing needs;
                                          * permits;
                                          * contracts;
                                          * employment restrictions;
                                          * data protection;
                                          * privacy;
                                          * consumer protection;
                                          * vulnerable users;
                                          * health/safety claims;
                                          * financial claims;
                                          * intellectual property;
                                          * discrimination risk;
                                          * ethical harm;
                                          * manipulation or deception risk.
Why it matters
The platform must not help users unknowingly or knowingly build harmful, illegal, misleading, or non-compliant ventures.
Example user-facing statement
“This idea touches a regulated area. Before moving forward, you need professional review. I can help prepare the questions, but I cannot replace legal advice.”
17. Evidence Quality
What it measures
Evidence Quality measures how strong, reliable, current, and relevant the user’s proof is.
Includes
                                          * self-reported belief;
                                          * desk research;
                                          * AI-generated research;
                                          * interviews;
                                          * surveys;
                                          * signups;
                                          * letters of intent;
                                          * pre-orders;
                                          * payments;
                                          * pilots;
                                          * repeat usage;
                                          * referrals;
                                          * third-party validation;
                                          * evidence age;
                                          * evidence bias;
                                          * evidence relevance.
Why it matters
Not all evidence is equal. A friend saying “nice idea” is not the same as a customer paying. The platform must weight evidence properly.
Example user-facing statement
“The current evidence is positive but weak. It comes mainly from people close to you. We need evidence from people who match the target customer and have no reason to be polite.”
18. Momentum, Engagement & Accountability
What it measures
Momentum, Engagement & Accountability measures whether the user is actually progressing, not only thinking or consuming content.
Includes
                                          * task completion;
                                          * return frequency;
                                          * answer depth;
                                          * response consistency;
                                          * validation completion;
                                          * missed deadlines;
                                          * avoidance patterns;
                                          * speed of progress;
                                          * willingness to revise;
                                          * follow-through;
                                          * real-world action;
                                          * accountability acceptance.
Why it matters
The platform must distinguish between users who are building and users who are only imagining. High engagement without real-world action should not be mistaken for progress.
Example user-facing statement
“You are engaging heavily inside the platform, but you have not completed the required external validation task. The next step remains outside the platform.”
19. Communication, Persuasion & Explanation Ability
What it measures
This dimension measures whether the user can explain the venture clearly to customers, partners, funders, team members, mentors, or the public.
Includes
                                          * clarity of explanation;
                                          * storytelling;
                                          * pitch ability;
                                          * customer language;
                                          * ability to simplify technical ideas;
                                          * listening ability;
                                          * objection handling;
                                          * written communication;
                                          * spoken communication;
                                          * credibility;
                                          * persuasion without manipulation.
Why it matters
A venture cannot grow if others cannot understand it. This is especially important for deep-tech founders, impact founders, and users seeking funding or partnerships.
Example user-facing statement
“The technical explanation is strong, but the customer-facing explanation is still too complex. We need to translate the value into plain language.”
20. Technology & Future-Proofing
What it measures
Technology & Future-Proofing measures whether the venture’s technical choices, systems, scalability, and future relevance are strong enough for the intended path.
Includes
                                          * technical architecture;
                                          * AI usage;
                                          * automation potential;
                                          * scalability;
                                          * technical debt;
                                          * integration needs;
                                          * cybersecurity;
                                          * data handling;
                                          * maintainability;
                                          * platform dependency;
                                          * future trends;
                                          * defensibility;
                                          * technology-readiness level.
Why it matters
Technology can create leverage, but also fragility. The platform should help users avoid overbuilding, underbuilding, fragile systems, unnecessary complexity, and future-obsolescence risk.
Example user-facing statement
“The technical path is possible, but the first version is too complex. We should reduce the scope before development begins.”
21. Impact, Values & Cultural Alignment
What it measures
Impact, Values & Cultural Alignment measures whether the venture aligns with the user’s values, intended impact, cultural context, stakeholder expectations, and ethical boundaries.
Includes
                                          * mission integrity;
                                          * values alignment;
                                          * theory of change;
                                          * beneficiary dignity;
                                          * stakeholder trust;
                                          * cultural fit;
                                          * inclusion;
                                          * social impact;
                                          * environmental impact;
                                          * long-term mission protection;
                                          * governance culture;
                                          * internal culture assumptions.
Why it matters
For many users, especially impact-driven users, success is not only financial. The platform must measure whether the venture remains true to its mission and whether the impact logic is real rather than performative.
Example user-facing statement
“Your mission is clear, but the current operating model could create dependency on unpaid labour. We need a sustainability model that protects both the mission and the people delivering it.”
22. User Risk of Misuse, Overreach, or Self-Deception
What it measures
This dimension measures whether the user is likely to misuse the platform’s outputs or misinterpret weak signals as strong evidence.
Includes
                                          * overconfidence;
                                          * skipping difficult steps;
                                          * shallow answers;
                                          * desire for praise;
                                          * refusal to validate;
                                          * treating AI output as proof;
                                          * rushing toward investors;
                                          * premature spending;
                                          * ignoring warnings;
                                          * repeatedly changing direction without learning;
                                          * using generated documents externally before they are ready.
Why it matters
The platform must protect users from polished self-deception. The better the generated outputs look, the more important it becomes to label confidence, evidence strength, and readiness clearly.
Example user-facing statement
“This pitch deck is visually ready, but the evidence behind it is not investor-ready. You should not send it externally yet.”
How dimensions should interact
The platform should never treat these dimensions as isolated scores. They must interact.
Examples:
                                          * Low financial readiness should block high-cost product development even if idea clarity is high.
                                          * Strong market demand can accelerate progress, but not override legal risk.
                                          * High motivation can support persistence, but cannot compensate for zero evidence.
                                          * Strong technical skills can help product feasibility, but not solve poor market access.
                                          * Strong network support can compensate for low experience, but not for harmful venture direction.
                                          * High personal readiness can support a difficult path, but not justify reckless debt.
The decision engine should use conditional logic, not simple averaging.
Blocking versus non-blocking dimensions
Some dimensions should be allowed to reduce the score without stopping progress. Others must block specific actions when critically low.
Examples:
Dimension
	Can be blocking?
	Example blocking condition
	Financial Readiness
	Yes
	User wants to spend money with zero runway.
	Legal, Ethical & Regulatory Risk
	Yes
	Venture enters regulated or harmful territory without review.
	Market Validity
	Yes
	User wants to build product without demand evidence.
	Idea Clarity
	Yes
	User cannot define problem or target user.
	Personal Readiness
	Yes
	Severe burnout or instability makes active building unsafe.
	Execution Capability
	Yes
	User refuses required validation tasks.
	Evidence Quality
	Yes
	User wants external funding materials based on weak evidence.
	Communication Ability
	Usually not fully blocking
	Can be improved, unless external pitching is the next step.
	Network Support
	Sometimes blocking
	Complex venture requires mentor/advisor before proceeding.
	Technology Readiness
	Sometimes blocking
	Product depends on unproven or inaccessible technology.
	This structure allows the platform to be precise. A weak dimension should block only the actions it makes unsafe, not necessarily the entire user journey.
Input/output dependency logic
The taxonomy spreadsheet already includes fields for Inputs (Depends on) and Outputs (Affects). This should become a core system principle.
Each answer should be treated as both:
                                          1. An output of previous context
The answer depends on what the platform already knows about the user, idea, market, financial situation, and prior evidence.
                                          2. An input into future components
The answer may affect the business plan, pitch deck, operations plan, go-to-market strategy, team structure, sales funnel, financial model, risk assessment, or founder profile.
This means the platform should maintain dependency mapping. If an answer changes, affected outputs should be flagged for review or regeneration.
AI extraction logic
The taxonomy should not rely only on direct user scores. The AI should extract signals from natural-language answers.
Examples of extracted signals:
                                             * emotional language;
                                             * uncertainty;
                                             * specificity;
                                             * avoidance;
                                             * overconfidence;
                                             * past experience;
                                             * tools mentioned;
                                             * examples or anecdotes;
                                             * contradictions;
                                             * values;
                                             * stress signals;
                                             * customer knowledge;
                                             * willingness to act;
                                             * depth of answer;
                                             * evidence type;
                                             * repeated themes.
However, AI extraction should not be treated as unquestionable truth. Important inferred signals should be explainable and, where necessary, confirmed with the user.
User-facing display rule
The user should not see thousands of raw questions, dimensions, or internal scores at once. The dashboard should show a clear summary such as:
                                             * Ready to continue;
                                             * Continue with caution;
                                             * Blocked until fixed;
                                             * Needs validation;
                                             * Needs mentor/professional input;
                                             * Strong area;
                                             * Weak area;
                                             * Not enough evidence yet.
For deeper users, drill-down should be available, but the default interface should remain clear.
Final answer
The platform should measure the user and venture through a large multi-layer taxonomy, currently expanded to 46,958 rows/questions across profile phases, assessments, groups, sections, and clusters. Internally, this taxonomy should track micro-signals such as motivation, readiness, values, skills, financial runway, time capacity, market demand, problem-solution fit, business model strength, competitive positioning, validation evidence, technology, ethics, operations, network support, and execution behaviour. Externally, the platform should translate this complexity into a manageable set of macro-dimensions that explain what matters, what is strong, what is weak, what is blocking, what can compensate, and what the user must do next. The purpose of measuring these dimensions is not to create a vanity score, but to power routing, blockers, warnings, benchmarks, generated outputs, and personalized next actions.
⭐ Non-negotiable: the platform must use the full taxonomy internally, but communicate it externally through clear, actionable macro-dimensions. Users should not be overwhelmed by the parameter system; they should benefit from it.
https://manus.im/share/file/fd1daffb-0b17-41a6-b28e-5ce5fd3825d7
4.2
For each dimension, what is the "danger zone"?
WHAT THIS MEANS
At what point should the platform stop a user's normal progress and redirect them? Define the threshold for each dimension where the platform intervenes rather than continues.
A danger zone is the point where the platform should no longer allow normal progress without intervention. It does not always mean the user must stop completely. It means the platform has detected a level of risk, uncertainty, contradiction, or weakness that makes the normal next step irresponsible.
Danger zones should be defined for every major dimension in the taxonomy. Since the platform contains thousands of underlying micro-parameters, each danger zone should be calculated from multiple signals rather than from one isolated answer. For example, financial danger is not only “low savings.” It may be a combination of no income, high debt, high monthly obligations, no support system, and a desire to spend money immediately.
The guiding principle is:
A danger zone is reached when continuing normally would likely increase avoidable risk faster than it increases useful progress.
Danger-zone severity levels
The platform should not treat every weak signal the same. Danger zones should have severity levels.
Severity level
	Meaning
	Platform response
	Level 0 — Healthy / no issue
	The dimension is strong enough for the current phase.
	Continue normally.
	Level 1 — Watch
	Mild weakness or early uncertainty.
	Continue, but monitor and explain.
	Level 2 — Warning
	Meaningful weakness that could affect future progress.
	Show warning and recommend corrective action.
	Level 3 — Soft block
	User should not move into certain next steps until this is improved or validated.
	Pause affected route; assign task or clarification.
	Level 4 — Hard block
	User cannot responsibly proceed into the relevant action or phase.
	Block the action; reroute to stabilization, validation, mentor, or professional review.
	Level 5 — Hard stop
	Venture direction is unsafe, illegal, deceptive, exploitative, or otherwise unacceptable.
	Refuse support for that direction unless redesigned.
	This severity model prevents the platform from being either too permissive or too rigid. A danger zone should block only what it makes unsafe, not necessarily the entire platform experience.
General danger-zone rules
Danger zones should follow these rules:
                                             1. Danger zones must be action-specific.
A weak financial runway may not block idea clarification, but it should block paid product development, hiring, inventory, leases, or quitting employment.
                                             2. Danger zones must be explainable.
The user should always know what was detected, why it matters, what is blocked, and how to unlock progress.
                                             3. Danger zones must be based on multiple signals where possible.
The platform should avoid overreacting to one answer unless the answer itself is critical.
                                             4. Danger zones must trigger a next action.
A danger-zone warning without a concrete next step is not useful.
                                             5. Danger zones must distinguish uncertainty from negative evidence.
“We do not know yet” is different from “the evidence is bad.”
                                             6. Danger zones must be dynamic.
The threshold may differ by venture type, phase, sector, capital intensity, legal risk, and user situation.
                                             7. Danger zones must be recalculated when assumptions change.
If the user updates runway, market evidence, target customer, legal risk, or product scope, the danger state may change.
Danger zones by macro-dimension
1. Personal Readiness
Danger zone
The user enters the danger zone when their personal situation, stress level, energy, emotional stability, consistency, or self-management is too weak to support the demands of the current entrepreneurial step.
Signals
                                                * Severe stress or overwhelm.
                                                * Burnout indicators.
                                                * No consistent follow-through.
                                                * Highly impulsive decision-making.
                                                * Avoidance of difficult questions.
                                                * Extreme urgency without evidence.
                                                * Inability to accept feedback.
                                                * Personal crisis interfering with basic functioning.
                                                * Repeatedly starting over without learning.
                                                * Strong emotional dependence on the idea succeeding.
Platform response
                                                * Level 1–2: warn, slow pace, assign reflection or workload-reduction task.
                                                * Level 3: pause high-pressure actions and route to stabilization or smaller tasks.
                                                * Level 4: block active venture-building if the user’s personal condition makes it unsafe.
                                                * Level 5: refer to professional/emergency support if serious harm or crisis indicators appear.
Example message
“Your current answers suggest that adding venture pressure right now may increase burnout risk. I recommend pausing growth-related tasks and first creating a lower-pressure execution plan.”
________________


2. Motivation & Purpose
Danger zone
The user enters the danger zone when their motivation is mainly escapist, reactive, revenge-driven, fantasy-based, externally pressured, or too unstable to support long-term venture-building.
Signals
                                                * “I just want to quit my job immediately.”
                                                * “I need this to work because I have no other option.”
                                                * Motivation based mainly on proving others wrong.
                                                * No connection to the customer/problem beyond personal excitement.
                                                * Rapid loss of interest when challenged.
                                                * Motivation depends entirely on fast success.
                                                * User cannot explain why the problem matters beyond money or status.
Platform response
