This structure keeps the system organized while allowing deep work.
________________


16. Alerts and notifications
The dashboard should use alerts carefully. Too many alerts will train users to ignore them.
Alert categories:
                                                                                                               * approval required;
                                                                                                               * blocker active;
                                                                                                               * urgent reply received;
                                                                                                               * document outdated;
                                                                                                               * legal/professional risk;
                                                                                                               * runway warning;
                                                                                                               * task overdue;
                                                                                                               * automation completed;
                                                                                                               * external opportunity deadline;
                                                                                                               * re-entry check required;
                                                                                                               * cost threshold approaching.
Each alert should have:
                                                                                                               * severity;
                                                                                                               * reason;
                                                                                                               * action button;
                                                                                                               * dismiss/postpone option where appropriate;
                                                                                                               * link to related record.
Avoid vague alerts like:
“You have updates.”
Use specific alerts like:
“3 customer replies need classification before your market evidence can update.”
________________


17. Dashboard language style
The dashboard should use direct, calm, specific language.
Good:
“Product development is blocked until market evidence improves.”
Bad:
“You’re almost there! Keep going!”
Good:
“Your next action is to approve or edit the 10 customer outreach messages.”
Bad:
“Explore your opportunities.”
Good:
“This document is outdated because pricing changed.”
Bad:
“Document needs attention.”
The dashboard should be warm enough to feel supportive, but direct enough to prevent confusion.
________________


18. Example login dashboard
A returning user might see:
Welcome back, Robert.
You are in Phase 3 — Market Research.
Current status: Continue with caution.
Main blocker: Market evidence is still below the threshold for product development.
Next Best Action: Approve outreach to 12 selected potential interview candidates.
Why: customer evidence is required before product concept testing.
AI prepared: 12 personalized messages and an interview guide.
Estimated cost: 18 credits if sent through connected accounts. Manual option available.
Since last login:
                                                                                                               * 2 mentor candidates replied.
                                                                                                               * 1 document became outdated after you changed the target segment.
                                                                                                               * Your runway check is due.
Do not do yet: paid product development, investor outreach, or public launch.
This is the desired feeling: clear, honest, active, and forward-moving.
________________


19. Dashboard success criteria
The dashboard succeeds if:
                                                                                                               * the user knows what to do next within 30 seconds;
                                                                                                               * the user understands why that action matters;
                                                                                                               * blockers are visible and actionable;
                                                                                                               * AI work is transparent;
                                                                                                               * costs are visible before execution;
                                                                                                               * outdated documents are clearly labelled;
                                                                                                               * external replies and evidence are captured;
                                                                                                               * the user feels continuity after returning;
                                                                                                               * the system reduces wasted time;
                                                                                                               * the user does not need to manually connect scattered tools;
                                                                                                               * the dashboard adapts to the user’s current route;
                                                                                                               * the venture feels like it is being operated, not merely discussed.
20. What the dashboard should not do
The dashboard should not:
                                                                                                               * show every module equally on the first screen;
                                                                                                               * overwhelm users with ERP complexity;
                                                                                                               * hide the next action;
                                                                                                               * hide blockers;
                                                                                                               * hide AI actions;
                                                                                                               * hide costs;
                                                                                                               * hide outdated documents;
                                                                                                               * show vanity scores without action;
                                                                                                               * act like a social feed;
                                                                                                               * act like a generic project-management board;
                                                                                                               * force users to dig through files;
                                                                                                               * use vague motivational language instead of concrete routing;
                                                                                                               * let AI perform external actions without visible approval;
                                                                                                               * bury financial, legal, or personal risk warnings;
                                                                                                               * make the user feel they are managing software instead of building a venture.
Final answer
Every time the user logs back in, they should see the Venture Cockpit: a focused command centre that shows the current venture state, the single next best action, why that action matters, active blockers, pending approvals, recent changes, today’s tasks, AI execution options, cost/credit implications, document freshness, and what not to do yet. The dashboard should be the visible front end of the Changepreneurship Venture Operating System. It should not show everything at once; it should show what matters now and let the user drill deeper into venture model, evidence, people, outreach, opportunities, documents, finance, operations, risks, AI actions, external platforms, metrics, history, and permissions.
The dashboard must adapt to the user’s state: new user, self-discovery, idea discovery, market research, business pillars, concept testing, business development, prototype testing, stabilization route, operations cleanup, or completed venture operating mode. It must be evidence-aware, action-aware, permission-aware, cost-aware, freshness-aware, relationship-aware, and document-aware.
⭐ Non-negotiable: the dashboard must make the next responsible action obvious every time the user logs in. If the user has to search, guess, or remember what to do next, the dashboard has failed
	 
SECTION 6   SCORING & BENCHMARKIN
How the platform measures progress and compares users to peers and standards.
6.1
When a user scores low on a dimension, how should that be communicated?  Options: raw number / descriptive text / colour signal / hidden (only action shown) / your own approach.
When a user scores low on a dimension, the platform should communicate it through a layered feedback system rather than a raw number alone. A low score should not be presented as a grade, judgment, or identity label. It should be presented as a decision-relevant signal: what is weak, why it matters, what it affects, what is blocked, what remains possible, and what action improves the situation.
The recommended approach is:
Use descriptive status labels and action guidance as the default. Use numbers internally and selectively. Use colours only as supporting visual cues. Never show a low score without explaining what the user can do next.
The platform should avoid making users feel like they “failed” as a person. A low score should mean:
“This specific area is not yet strong enough for the next step.”
It should not mean:
“You are bad at entrepreneurship.”
1. Why raw low scores are dangerous
Raw numbers can feel objective, but they can easily mislead users.
A score of 32/100 may look precise, but the user may not understand:
                                                                                                               * what caused it;
                                                                                                               * whether it is serious;
                                                                                                               * whether it blocks progress;
                                                                                                               * whether it can be improved;
                                                                                                               * whether it is temporary;
                                                                                                               * whether another dimension compensates for it;
                                                                                                               * what action should be taken;
                                                                                                               * whether the score is based on evidence or missing information.
Raw numbers can also create emotional problems:
                                                                                                               * users may feel judged;
                                                                                                               * users may compare themselves unfairly;
                                                                                                               * users may obsess over improving the number rather than improving reality;
                                                                                                               * users may become discouraged;
                                                                                                               * users may try to game the system;
                                                                                                               * users may mistake a low number for permanent inability.
For this reason, raw numbers should not be the main communication method for low scores.
2. Preferred communication format
Every low-score message should have six parts:
                                                                                                               1. Status label
A plain-language label such as Warning, Soft Block, Hard Block, Needs Evidence, or Professional Review Required.
                                                                                                               2. Dimension name
The specific area affected, such as Financial Readiness, Market Evidence, Time Capacity, or Founder-Market Fit.
                                                                                                               3. Reason
The main cause of the low status.
                                                                                                               4. Consequence
What this affects or blocks.
                                                                                                               5. Allowed path
What the user can still do safely.
                                                                                                               6. Unlock action
The concrete step required to improve or unlock progress.
Example:
Market Evidence — Soft Block
Your idea is clear, but there is not enough direct evidence from potential customers yet. This blocks product development and investor-ready materials for now. You can continue with customer discovery, outreach, and concept testing. To unlock the next stage, complete at least 10 conversations with people who match the target customer profile or provide equivalent behavioural evidence.
This is much better than:
Market Evidence: 32/100.
3. Recommended user-facing score states
Instead of relying mainly on numerical scores, the platform should use status states.
Status
	Meaning
	User-facing implication
	Strong
	This area supports progress.
	Continue or accelerate if other gates allow.
	Adequate
	Good enough for the current stage.
	Continue normally.
	Watch
	Mild concern.
	Continue, but monitor.
	Warning
	Meaningful weakness.
	Improve before later-stage action.
	Needs Evidence
	The platform cannot score confidently yet.
	Gather proof before decisions.
	Soft Block
	A specific action or phase is paused.
	Complete unlock task first.
	Hard Block
	Progress into the relevant step is not responsible.
	Resolve blocker or reroute.
	Professional Review Required
	Qualified external input is needed.
	Prepare review pack and pause affected route.
	Hard Stop
	Direction is illegal, harmful, or unacceptable.
	Stop or redesign the venture.
	Unknown
	Not enough information.
	Answer more or provide evidence.
	Covered Gap
	Weakness exists but is currently compensated.
	Continue within limits.
	Uncovered Gap
	Weakness exists and no credible compensation exists.
	Create support, mentor, co-founder, or mitigation plan.
	These states should be the main user-facing language.
4. Should raw numbers be shown?
Raw numbers should exist internally. They are useful for:
                                                                                                                  * scoring models;
                                                                                                                  * benchmarking;
                                                                                                                  * change detection;
                                                                                                                  * automation triggers;
                                                                                                                  * cohort analysis;
                                                                                                                  * calibration;
                                                                                                                  * routing;
                                                                                                                  * trend tracking;
                                                                                                                  * measuring improvement over time.
However, raw numbers should be shown to users selectively.
Recommended rule
Situation
	Show raw number?
	Why
	Internal routing
	Yes, internally only.
	Needed for decision engine.
	Simple user dashboard
	Usually no.
	Status and action are clearer.
	Detailed drill-down
	Optional.
	Advanced users may want precision.
	Peer benchmarking
	Maybe, with context.
	Useful if explained carefully.
	Danger-zone warning
	Usually no raw number first.
	The action and risk matter more than the number.
	Progress over time
	Yes, if framed as trend.
	Shows improvement without identity judgment.
	External reports
	Usually no personal raw scores.
	Could be misread by external parties.
	If numbers are shown, they should be secondary to explanation.
Example:
Financial Readiness: Warning
Internal score: 42/100
Reason: runway is below the safe threshold for paid development.
Action: complete runway plan and use low-cost validation route.
The number should never stand alone.
5. Should colours be used?
Colours can be helpful, but they should never be the only communication method. Some users may be colour-blind, some may not understand the meaning, and colours alone can feel crude.
Colours should be supporting signals only.
Example colour logic:
Colour signal
	Meaning
	Green
	Strong / adequate.
	Yellow
	Watch / needs attention.
	Orange
	Warning / soft block.
	Red
	Hard block / hard stop / serious risk.
	Blue
	Needs evidence / unknown / informational.
	Grey
	Not assessed / not applicable / archived.
	Every colour should be paired with text.
Bad:
Red score.
Good:
Hard Block — Financial Readiness
Paid development is blocked because your current runway is below the safe threshold.
6. Should low scores ever be hidden?
Sometimes the raw score should be hidden, but the issue should not be hidden.
The platform should not hide a serious weakness. It should hide or soften the number when the number is not useful or could harm understanding.
For example, instead of saying:
Personal Stability: 21/100.
The platform should say:
Personal Readiness — Stabilization Recommended
Your answers suggest that adding business pressure right now may create more risk than progress. I recommend reducing pressure and using a stabilization route before active venture-building.
This communicates the reality without turning a sensitive area into a harsh score.
7. Sensitive dimensions need extra care
Some low-score areas are emotionally or ethically sensitive and should not be communicated with blunt numbers by default.
Sensitive areas include:
                                                                                                                  * personal stability;
                                                                                                                  * financial vulnerability;
                                                                                                                  * debt pressure;
                                                                                                                  * burnout risk;
                                                                                                                  * mental-health-adjacent indicators;
                                                                                                                  * family or caregiving constraints;
                                                                                                                  * low confidence;
                                                                                                                  * low education level;
                                                                                                                  * low literacy or digital skill;
                                                                                                                  * immigration/work-status constraints;
                                                                                                                  * personal crisis;
                                                                                                                  * history of failure.
For these areas, use:
                                                                                                                  * descriptive language;
                                                                                                                  * careful explanation;
                                                                                                                  * specific next steps;
                                                                                                                  * non-identity wording;
                                                                                                                  * no shame;
                                                                                                                  * no comparison unless clearly helpful;
                                                                                                                  * no visible raw score by default.
Example:
Financial Readiness — Hard Block for Spending
The current issue is not your potential. The issue is that spending money on the venture right now could create avoidable harm. Free validation and idea refinement remain open, but paid development is blocked until your runway improves.
8. Low score communication by severity
The message should change depending on severity.
Level 1 — Watch
Use when the issue is mild.
Example:
Time Capacity — Watch
Your available time is enough for the current phase, but not enough for a fast build route. Keep tasks small and reassess next week.
Level 2 — Warning
Use when the weakness matters but does not yet block the next step.
Example:
Communication Ability — Warning
Your current explanation is too technical for customer conversations. Before outreach, simplify the message into customer language.
Level 3 — Soft Block
Use when a specific action should pause until an unlock condition is met.
Example:
Market Evidence — Soft Block
Product development is paused because there is not enough evidence from target customers. Customer discovery remains open.
Level 4 — Hard Block
Use when the next step would create serious risk.
Example:
Financial Readiness — Hard Block
Paid development, hiring, loans, and inventory purchases are blocked for now because your runway is below the safe threshold.
Level 5 — Hard Stop
Use when the venture direction is not supportable.
Example:
Ethical / Legal Risk — Hard Stop
I cannot support this direction because it depends on misleading users. If you want to redesign the idea into a lawful and ethical version, I can help with that.
9. Low score should always connect to dashboard action
A low score should automatically create at least one dashboard consequence.
Examples:
Low dimension
	Dashboard consequence
	Financial Readiness low
	Create Financial Stabilization Block; block paid actions.
	Market Evidence low
	Create Customer Discovery Block; block product development.
	Time Capacity low
	Create reduced-scope task route.
	Legal Risk high
	Create Professional Review Block.
	Founder-Market Fit low
	Create mentor/insider access task.
	Communication weak
	Create messaging refinement task.
	Evidence Quality weak
	Mark external materials speculative.
	Operations weak
	Pause growth and create operations cleanup tasks.
	Personal Readiness low
	Create stabilization or burnout-prevention route.
	If a low score does not produce an action, the platform is only judging the user, not helping them.
10. Low score should explain what remains allowed
When a dimension is low, users may assume everything is blocked. The platform should avoid that.
Every low-score message should explain what remains possible.
Example:
Paid development is blocked, but idea clarification, free customer discovery, mentor outreach, and low-cost validation remain available.
This keeps the user moving.
11. Low score should include an unlock condition
Every low score that blocks progress must have a clear unlock condition unless it is a hard legal/ethical stop.
Examples:
Dimension
	Unlock condition
	Financial Readiness
	Define safe runway, restore income, or switch to low-capital route.
	Market Evidence
	Complete customer interviews or provide behavioural evidence.
	Time Capacity
	Create recurring work blocks or reduce scope.
	Communication
	Create and test a clear audience-specific explanation.
	Legal Risk
	Obtain professional review or redesign route.
	Founder-Market Fit
	Build access through mentor, insider, partner, or interviews.
	Operations
	Document delivery process and define capacity.
	Business Model
	Define payer/funder and validate pricing or funding logic.
	The platform should make the path out of weakness visible.
12. Low score should not be averaged away
If a dimension is low because it is a hard blocker, the platform should not allow high scores elsewhere to hide it.
Example:
A user may have:
                                                                                                                  * Motivation: Strong;
                                                                                                                  * Idea Clarity: Strong;
                                                                                                                  * Communication: Strong;
                                                                                                                  * Financial Readiness: Hard Block.
The dashboard should say:
Your overall profile has several strengths, but Financial Readiness currently blocks paid venture actions. Strong motivation and clarity do not compensate for unsafe spending risk.
This prevents false confidence.
13. Low score should distinguish weakness from missing data
A low score can mean two different things:
                                                                                                                  1. The user is genuinely weak in the area.
                                                                                                                  2. The platform does not have enough information yet.
These should be communicated differently.
Example genuine weakness:
Market Evidence — Weak
You have spoken with 12 target users, and most did not report this as urgent. A pivot may be needed.
Example missing data:
Market Evidence — Unknown
There is not enough external data yet to assess market demand. The next step is customer discovery.
This distinction is critical. “Unknown” should not be treated as “bad,” but it should still block high-confidence decisions.
14. Low score should distinguish temporary from structural
Some low scores are temporary and can improve quickly. Others are structural and require route changes.
Example temporary:
Your pitch clarity is weak, but this can likely improve with a revised script and practice.
Example structural:
Your current venture model requires weekly in-person delivery, but your location and availability make that unrealistic. The model may need redesign.
This helps the user understand whether to improve, compensate, or pivot.
15. Low score should distinguish uncovered from covered gaps
A low dimension may not be dangerous if it is credibly covered.
Example:
Technical Capability is weak, but currently covered because the recommended MVP is manual and no custom development is planned. Custom development remains blocked until technical support or validation exists.
This is better than simply showing a low technical score.
16. Low score should be phrased as current state, not identity
Avoid identity labels.
Bad:
You are bad at sales.
Better:
Sales capability is currently not strong enough for a direct-sales-heavy route. We can reduce this risk with scripts, practice, partner support, or a different acquisition model.
Bad:
You are financially unprepared.
Better:
Your current financial runway is not strong enough for paid development or quitting work.
Bad:
You are not resilient.
Better:
Your current workload and stress signals suggest that adding venture pressure may not be sustainable right now.
17. Tone guidelines
The tone should be:
                                                                                                                  * direct;
                                                                                                                  * calm;
                                                                                                                  * respectful;
                                                                                                                  * specific;
                                                                                                                  * action-oriented;
                                                                                                                  * non-shaming;
                                                                                                                  * non-flattering;
                                                                                                                  * reality-based;
                                                                                                                  * firm when needed.
The tone should not be:
                                                                                                                  * vague;
                                                                                                                  * motivational without substance;
                                                                                                                  * patronizing;
                                                                                                                  * cruel;
                                                                                                                  * overly clinical;
                                                                                                                  * gamified;
                                                                                                                  * school-like;
                                                                                                                  * identity-based.
The platform should sound like a serious co-founder who is honest because they are trying to protect the user.
18. Example low-score messages
Financial readiness low
Financial Readiness — Hard Block for Spending
Your current runway is below the safe threshold for paid venture actions. Paid development, hiring, loans, and inventory purchases are blocked for now. You can still continue with idea clarification, free customer discovery, and mentor outreach. To unlock paid actions, complete a runway plan and define how basic costs will be covered.
Market evidence low
Market Evidence — Needs Evidence
The idea is clear, but we do not yet have enough evidence from target customers. Product development and investor-ready documents are paused. The next action is customer discovery with at least 10 relevant people.
Time capacity low
Time Capacity — Warning
Your available time is too low for the current build plan. The venture does not need to stop, but the route must be smaller. I recommend a low-intensity validation path with one task per week.
Communication low
Customer Explanation — Needs Work
The current explanation is too abstract for customer outreach. Before contacting people, we should turn it into a simple problem-benefit message.
Legal risk high
Legal / Professional Review Required
This venture touches a regulated area. I cannot responsibly route you into launch or public claims until the legal questions are reviewed. I can prepare a professional review pack.
Personal readiness low
Personal Readiness — Stabilization Recommended
Your answers suggest that active business-building may add pressure faster than it creates progress. I recommend a stabilization route first. This does not mean the venture is impossible; it means the current pace is not safe.
Evidence quality low
Evidence Quality — Weak
The current evidence comes mainly from people close to you. That feedback may be useful, but it is not strong enough to confirm demand. We need evidence from people who match the target customer and have no reason to be polite.
19. Dashboard display example
A dashboard card for a low dimension could show:
Market Evidence
Status: Soft Block
Reason: No direct customer interviews yet.
Blocks: product development, investor-ready pitch deck, paid advertising.
Still allowed: free outreach, interviews, concept testing, mentor review.
Unlock: 10 customer interviews or equivalent behavioural evidence.
AI can help: draft outreach and identify contacts.
Cost: 14 credits if AI prepares the outreach list and messages.
This combines score, explanation, action, automation, and cost.
20. Should users see an overall score?
For low dimensions, the platform should prioritize dimension-level communication over an overall score. An overall score can hide critical weaknesses.
Example:
A user might have an overall 78/100 but still be blocked because financial readiness is dangerously low.
The dashboard should say:
Your general readiness is promising, but one hard blocker prevents paid progress: Financial Readiness.
An overall score should never be allowed to override dimension-specific blockers.
21. Peer comparison for low scores
If peer comparison is used, it must be careful.
Good:
Compared with similar users at this stage, your market evidence is below the recommended threshold for product development. This means validation should come before building.
Bad:
You are behind other founders.
Peer comparison should benchmark readiness for action, not personal worth.
22. User disagreement and correction
If the user disagrees with a low score, the platform should allow correction, but not remove the warning without evidence.
Example:
You can correct this assessment if important information is missing. What part is inaccurate? You can update the answer, provide evidence, or request reassessment.
The user should be able to:
                                                                                                                  * correct wrong information;
                                                                                                                  * provide evidence;
                                                                                                                  * add context;
                                                                                                                  * request reassessment;
                                                                                                                  * challenge assumptions;
                                                                                                                  * choose a lower-risk path.
But they should not be able to bypass a hard block by simply insisting.
23. Developer-facing requirements
The system should support:
                                                                                                                  * internal numerical scores;
                                                                                                                  * user-facing status labels;
                                                                                                                  * severity levels;
                                                                                                                  * dimension-specific explanations;
                                                                                                                  * blocker mapping;
                                                                                                                  * unlock conditions;
                                                                                                                  * allowed-path logic;
                                                                                                                  * sensitivity-aware communication;
                                                                                                                  * compensation logic;
                                                                                                                  * missing-data distinction;
                                                                                                                  * evidence quality distinction;
                                                                                                                  * trend tracking;
                                                                                                                  * peer benchmark mapping;
                                                                                                                  * dashboard action generation;
                                                                                                                  * user correction flow;
                                                                                                                  * score recalculation after new evidence;
                                                                                                                  * audit trail for major score changes.
Final answer
When a user scores low on a dimension, Changepreneurship should not show only a raw number. The default should be descriptive, action-oriented feedback using status labels such as Watch, Warning, Needs Evidence, Soft Block, Hard Block, Professional Review Required, or Hard Stop. Colours can be used as supporting visual cues, but never alone. Raw numbers should exist internally and may be shown in detailed views, but they should not be the main message, especially for sensitive areas such as financial vulnerability, personal readiness, burnout risk, debt, or confidence.
Every low-score message should explain what was detected, why it matters, what is blocked, what remains allowed, and what unlocks progress. The platform should distinguish between genuine weakness, missing data, temporary weakness, structural mismatch, covered gaps, and uncovered gaps. A low score should always produce a dashboard consequence: a task, warning, blocker, reroute, mentor/professional referral, or automation option.
⭐ Non-negotiable: a low score must never be communicated as a personal judgment. It must be communicated as a current decision signal with a concrete route forward.
	 
 
6.2
Should scores change automatically over time even if the user has done nothing new?  For example: if market conditions change, or if a user has been inactive for 6 months.
Yes, scores should be able to change over time even if the user has not actively entered new information. However, this must be handled carefully. The platform should not silently punish users for inactivity, and it should not pretend to know that something changed when it only knows that information may be stale.
The correct approach is not simple automatic score decay. The correct approach is a dynamic score freshness and confidence system.
This means that some scores may remain stable, some may become stale, some may lose confidence, some may trigger revalidation, and some may update automatically if reliable external data changes. The platform should distinguish between:
                                                                                                                  * the score itself;
                                                                                                                  * the confidence level of the score;
                                                                                                                  * the freshness of the underlying information;
                                                                                                                  * the risk created by relying on old information;
                                                                                                                  * whether the user must re-confirm or revalidate before proceeding.
The guiding principle is:
Scores should not decay merely because time passed. They should change, lose confidence, or require revalidation when the underlying reality may have changed enough to affect decisions.
1. Why scores cannot be permanently static
If scores never change unless the user updates answers, the platform will become unreliable. Entrepreneurship is affected by time, market movement, life changes, regulation, technology, competition, financial runway, customer behaviour, and user momentum. A score that was accurate three months ago may be misleading today.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * A user’s financial runway may be lower after months of inactivity.
                                                                                                                  * Competitors may have launched new products.
                                                                                                                  * Customer demand may have changed.
                                                                                                                  * AI tools or technology may have made a product easier or harder to build.
                                                                                                                  * Laws or platform rules may have changed.
                                                                                                                  * A pitch deck may become outdated.
                                                                                                                  * A market report may no longer be reliable.
                                                                                                                  * A user’s motivation may have changed.
                                                                                                                  * A user’s task momentum may have dropped.
                                                                                                                  * A pricing assumption may become stale.
                                                                                                                  * A co-founder candidate or mentor lead may no longer be relevant.
A system that ignores time will keep recommending actions based on old reality.
2. Why scores should not simply decay automatically
A naive system might reduce scores automatically after a certain period of inactivity. That would be too crude.
For example:
                                                                                                                  * Domain expertise does not disappear after 6 months.
                                                                                                                  * Founder values do not usually decay quickly.
                                                                                                                  * Previous customer interviews do not become worthless overnight.
                                                                                                                  * A completed legal review may remain useful for a while.
                                                                                                                  * A previous failure lesson may remain highly relevant.
                                                                                                                  * Strong operational systems may still exist.
Automatic decay can be unfair and inaccurate. It can make users feel punished for absence.
Therefore, the platform should not simply say:
“You were inactive, so your score went down.”
It should say:
“Some information may now be stale. Before using it for decisions, we need to confirm what is still true.”
3. Score, confidence, and freshness must be separate
The platform should separate three concepts.
Concept
	Meaning
	Example
	Score
	The platform’s assessment of the dimension.
	Market Evidence: Promising.
	Confidence
	How sure the platform is that the score is reliable.
	Confidence: Moderate.
	Freshness
	How current the underlying data is.
	Last validated: 112 days ago.
	This is critical.
A score may remain high, but confidence may fall because the evidence is old.
Example:
Market Evidence: Promising
Confidence: Low due to age of evidence
Action: refresh customer discovery before product development
The platform should not necessarily lower the market score immediately. It may instead mark the score as stale or lower confidence until refreshed.
4. Recommended scoring states over time
The dashboard should use time-aware score states.
State
	Meaning
	Platform behaviour
	Current
	Data is fresh enough for the current decision.
	Continue normally.
	Aging
	Data is still usable but approaching review threshold.
	Show light warning.
	Stale
	Data may no longer be reliable for decisions.
	Require confirmation before major action.
	Expired
	Data is too old for the intended use.
	Block relevant actions until refreshed.
	Unknown
	Not enough data exists.
	Require input/evidence.
	Changed externally
	Reliable external signal changed.
	Recalculate or request review.
	User re-confirmation needed
	The platform cannot know if the information is still true.
	Ask targeted re-entry questions.
	Revalidation required
	Old evidence is not enough for the next step.
	Assign validation task.
	This avoids false precision.
5. Which scores should remain mostly stable?
Some dimensions should not change much just because time passes. They may update only if the user provides new information or new behaviour appears.
Mostly stable dimensions:
                                                                                                                  * long-term values;
                                                                                                                  * personal background;
                                                                                                                  * education history;
                                                                                                                  * previous professional experience;
                                                                                                                  * domain experience already gained;
                                                                                                                  * previous venture lessons;
                                                                                                                  * broad personality tendencies;
                                                                                                                  * past skill acquisition;
                                                                                                                  * previous validation evidence as historical evidence;
                                                                                                                  * decision history;
                                                                                                                  * founder story;
                                                                                                                  * prior completed tasks.
These do not automatically become false with time. However, their relevance may change depending on the new venture, market, or phase.
Example:
Your engineering background remains a strength. However, because the venture has shifted from engineering product to consumer marketplace, the relevance of this strength has changed.
6. Which scores should become stale over time?
Some dimensions are time-sensitive and should lose freshness if not updated.
Time-sensitive dimensions:
                                                                                                                  * financial runway;
                                                                                                                  * available time;
                                                                                                                  * current employment situation;
                                                                                                                  * debt pressure;
                                                                                                                  * personal workload;
                                                                                                                  * burnout risk;
                                                                                                                  * motivation intensity;
                                                                                                                  * market demand;
                                                                                                                  * competitor landscape;
                                                                                                                  * pricing assumptions;
                                                                                                                  * customer behaviour;
                                                                                                                  * technology feasibility;
                                                                                                                  * legal/regulatory assumptions;
                                                                                                                  * funding availability;
                                                                                                                  * grant deadlines;
                                                                                                                  * partner opportunities;
                                                                                                                  * mentor/co-founder availability;
                                                                                                                  * task relevance;
                                                                                                                  * external document readiness;
                                                                                                                  * pitch deck freshness;
                                                                                                                  * financial model accuracy;
                                                                                                                  * operational capacity.
These should be rechecked at defined intervals or when triggering events occur.
7. Suggested freshness rules by dimension
The platform should define freshness rules. These should be configurable by sector, venture type, and stage.
Dimension / data type
	Suggested freshness rule
	Result when stale
	Financial runway
	Re-check after 28 days or after major spending/income change.
	Spending decisions blocked until updated.
	Time capacity
	Re-check after 28 days or after life/work change.
	Active route may be reduced.
	Market evidence
	Review after 60–90 days; deeper review after 180 days.
	Product/funding decisions may require refresh.
	Competitor analysis
	Review after 90 days; faster in AI/software/fast-moving sectors.
	Positioning and pitch materials need review.
	Pricing assumptions
	Review after 60–90 days or after new customer evidence.
	Financial model and offer may need update.
	Legal/regulatory assumptions
	Review after 90–180 days or when venture/location/sector changes.
	Professional-review warning may appear.
	Financial model
	Review after 30–60 days or when costs/prices change.
	Bank/funder/investor materials need review.
	Pitch deck
	Review before external use if older than 30–60 days.
	External-ready status may be removed.
	Grant/funding opportunities
	Review continuously or by deadline.
	Expired opportunities archived.
	Mentor/co-founder leads
	Review after 30–60 days of no contact.
	Follow-up or archive recommended.
	Customer outreach list
	Review after 30–60 days.
	Contact relevance may need update.
	Task roadmap
	Review after 14–28 days of inactivity.
	Task list needs re-planning.
	Operations capacity
	Review after new customers, workload increase, or 30–60 days.
	Growth may be paused.
	Founder motivation
	Re-check after 28+ days of inactivity or major life event.
	Re-entry check triggered.
	Evidence from interviews
	Keep as historical evidence, but age-label after 90 days.
	May require fresh validation before major decision.
	8. Inactivity should trigger re-entry, not automatic punishment
If a user has been inactive for 6 months, the platform should not simply lower all scores. It should trigger a Deep Re-entry Check.
The platform should say:
“You have been away for 6 months. Your previous work is preserved, but some assumptions may now be stale. Before continuing, we need to confirm what is still true.”
The re-entry check should review:
                                                                                                                  * financial runway;
                                                                                                                  * available time;
                                                                                                                  * motivation;
                                                                                                                  * current life situation;
                                                                                                                  * employment/income;
                                                                                                                  * whether the idea still matters;
                                                                                                                  * whether the target market changed;
                                                                                                                  * whether the user gathered evidence while away;
                                                                                                                  * whether competitors changed;
                                                                                                                  * whether old documents are still safe;
                                                                                                                  * whether previous tasks still matter;
                                                                                                                  * whether the route should continue, pivot, pause, or restart.
The platform should preserve the user’s work, but not rely on it blindly.
9. Market-condition changes
Some scores may change because the outside world changed, even if the user did nothing.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * new competitor enters the market;
                                                                                                                  * regulation changes;
                                                                                                                  * platform policy changes;
                                                                                                                  * AI technology makes the product obsolete or easier to build;
                                                                                                                  * customer behaviour shifts;
                                                                                                                  * economic conditions affect willingness to pay;
                                                                                                                  * grant program closes;
                                                                                                                  * funding environment changes;
                                                                                                                  * supplier prices increase;
                                                                                                                  * a major industry event changes market urgency.
When this happens, the platform should not automatically rewrite the venture model without explanation. It should show an external-change notice.
Example:
“External market conditions may have changed. A new competitor appears to address a similar problem. Your competitor map and positioning may need review before using investor or partner materials.”
10. External data updates: automatic versus review-required
The platform should classify external changes by reliability and severity.
External signal
	Platform response
	Reliable and factual
	Update relevant record and notify user.
	Reliable but interpretation needed
	Flag for review and explain possible impact.
	Weak or uncertain
	Add as possible signal, do not recalculate major scores.
	High-impact risk
	Trigger warning or professional review.
	Time-sensitive opportunity
	Create opportunity/task and deadline.
	Contradicts current assumptions
	Lower confidence and request validation.
	Example:
If a grant deadline is found, the platform can create an opportunity record.
If a competitor launches, the platform should not immediately declare the idea invalid. It should update the competitor map and ask whether differentiation still holds.
11. Score-change categories
When scores change over time, the platform should categorize the reason.
Score-change reason
	Example
	User-provided update
	User says runway changed.
	User behaviour
	User missed tasks repeatedly.
	External evidence
	Customer replies show low demand.
	External market change
	New regulation or competitor.
	Time-based staleness
	Evidence is old.
	Inactivity
	User away for months.
	Document dependency change
	Business model changed, affecting pitch deck readiness.
	Professional feedback
	Accountant or lawyer flags issue.
	Automation result
	Outreach generated replies and evidence.
	Manual correction
	User fixes wrong answer.
	The user should be able to see why a score changed.
12. Score-change transparency
If a score changes automatically, the platform must explain it.
A score-change message should include:
                                                                                                                  1. what changed;
                                                                                                                  2. why it changed;
                                                                                                                  3. whether the score itself changed or only confidence/freshness changed;
                                                                                                                  4. what this affects;
                                                                                                                  5. what is blocked or still allowed;
                                                                                                                  6. what action restores confidence or improves the score;
                                                                                                                  7. whether user confirmation is needed.
Example:
Market Evidence changed from Current to Stale.
Reason: the last customer validation evidence is 184 days old.
Your previous evidence is preserved, but it is no longer strong enough for product development or investor materials without refresh.
Next action: complete 5 updated customer conversations or confirm that market conditions have not changed.
13. Scores should have history
Scores should be versioned over time.
The user should be able to see:
                                                                                                                  * current score/status;
                                                                                                                  * previous score/status;
                                                                                                                  * date of change;
                                                                                                                  * reason for change;
                                                                                                                  * evidence used;
                                                                                                                  * confidence level;
                                                                                                                  * affected outputs;
                                                                                                                  * action taken;
                                                                                                                  * whether user confirmed or disputed it.
This prevents the platform from feeling arbitrary.
14. Trend matters more than one score
The platform should show trends, not only current status.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * improving;
                                                                                                                  * declining;
                                                                                                                  * stable;
                                                                                                                  * stale;
                                                                                                                  * volatile;
                                                                                                                  * unknown;
                                                                                                                  * blocked but improving;
                                                                                                                  * strong but aging;
                                                                                                                  * weak but now covered.
Example:
Time Capacity: Warning, but improving. You completed 3 weekly work blocks in a row.
or:
Market Evidence: Previously promising, now stale. Revalidation required before external use.
Trend helps users understand movement.
15. Scoring should affect documents and external readiness
When scores become stale or change, the platform should update document statuses.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * If market evidence becomes stale, investor deck becomes needs review.
                                                                                                                  * If financial runway drops, paid development tasks become blocked.
                                                                                                                  * If legal risk increases, public claims become blocked.
                                                                                                                  * If customer evidence improves, pitch deck may move from speculative to advisor-ready.
                                                                                                                  * If operations weaken, growth plan becomes paused.
                                                                                                                  * If the user is inactive for 6 months, external-ready materials become needs freshness review.
Scores should not live in isolation. They must affect the operating system.
16. Scores should affect automation permissions
If scores change, automation permissions may need to change.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * If a document becomes outdated, AI should not send it externally.
                                                                                                                  * If financial readiness becomes hard block, AI should not execute paid marketplace actions.
                                                                                                                  * If legal risk appears, AI should not send public claims.
                                                                                                                  * If market evidence is weak, AI may assist with discovery but not investor outreach.
                                                                                                                  * If user is inactive, AI may pause proactive outreach until re-entry confirmation.
This is essential because Changepreneurship can act on behalf of the user.
17. Sector-specific score freshness
Scores should not age at the same speed in every sector.
Examples:
Sector type
	Freshness behaviour
	AI/software
	Market, competitor, and technology assumptions become stale faster.
	Local service business
	Market assumptions may remain stable longer, but pricing and capacity still need review.
	Regulated sectors
	Legal and compliance assumptions require periodic review.
	Deep-tech
	Technology readiness may remain stable, but commercialization evidence needs updating.
	Non-profit/social impact
	Funding opportunities and stakeholder priorities may change frequently.
	E-commerce/product
	Supplier costs, platform rules, competition, and demand can shift quickly.
	Government/municipal ventures
	Procurement cycles and stakeholder priorities may change by budget/political cycle.
	The freshness engine should be configurable.
18. User-controlled freshness confirmation
The user should be able to confirm that some information remains valid.
Example:
“Is your weekly availability still 8 hours?”
Options:
                                                                                                                  * Yes, still accurate.
                                                                                                                  * No, update it.
                                                                                                                  * Not sure.
                                                                                                                  * Temporarily changed.
For more serious areas, confirmation alone may not be enough. Evidence may be required.
Example:
A user can confirm their time availability. But they cannot confirm market demand without evidence if the evidence is stale.
19. Score decay versus confidence decay
The platform should prefer confidence decay over score decay for many dimensions.
Example:
Original:
Market Evidence: Strong, confidence high.
After 6 months inactivity:
Market Evidence: Previously strong, confidence reduced due to age. Revalidation required before high-commitment action.
This is better than automatically changing:
Market Evidence: Strong → Weak.
The evidence did not become weak. It became old.
20. When actual score should change automatically
There are cases where the actual score should change, not just confidence.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * runway decreases mathematically over time if expenses are known;
                                                                                                                  * grant deadline passes;
                                                                                                                  * user misses repeated tasks;
                                                                                                                  * external replies show low demand;
                                                                                                                  * pricing test results are negative;
                                                                                                                  * competitor analysis reveals strong substitutes;
                                                                                                                  * legal risk is confirmed;
                                                                                                                  * platform-connected financial data shows spending increased;
                                                                                                                  * outreach response rates are poor;
                                                                                                                  * operational capacity is exceeded by new customers;
                                                                                                                  * documents become outdated because core assumptions changed.
In these cases, the score/status may update automatically, but the platform must explain why.
21. When the platform should ask instead of changing
If the platform does not have enough reliable data, it should ask instead of changing the score.
Examples:
                                                                                                                  * “Has your income changed?”
                                                                                                                  * “Are you still pursuing the same idea?”
                                                                                                                  * “Is this target customer still correct?”
                                                                                                                  * “Did you complete any customer conversations while away?”
                                                                                                                  * “Is this pricing still accurate?”
                                                                                                                  * “Have any competitors changed?”
                                                                                                                  * “Do you still want to continue?”
Uncertainty should trigger questions, not fake precision.
22. Inactivity examples
User inactive for 30 days
Platform response:
                                                                                                                  * light re-entry check;
                                                                                                                  * update task roadmap;
                                                                                                                  * check runway/time if needed;
                                                                                                                  * flag stale tasks;
                                                                                                                  * no broad score changes unless data changed.
User inactive for 90 days
Platform response:
                                                                                                                  * standard re-entry check;
                                                                                                                  * mark market and financial assumptions as needing review;
                                                                                                                  * reduce confidence in time-sensitive scores;
                                                                                                                  * refresh task board;
                                                                                                                  * check document external-readiness.
User inactive for 180 days
Platform response:
                                                                                                                  * deep re-entry check;
                                                                                                                  * many time-sensitive scores marked stale;
                                                                                                                  * external-ready documents lose status until reviewed;
                                                                                                                  * market, competitor, financial, runway, and motivation checks required;
                                                                                                                  * old work preserved but not blindly trusted.
User inactive for 1 year
Platform response:
                                                                                                                  * major revalidation;
                                                                                                                  * preserve full history;
                                                                                                                  * treat venture as potentially stale;
                                                                                                                  * ask whether to continue, pivot, archive, or restart;
                                                                                                                  * require updated market and personal readiness data before active building.
23. Dashboard display
The dashboard should show time-aware score information like this:
Dimension
	Current status
	Freshness
	Confidence
	Action
	Financial Readiness
	Warning
	41 days old
	Low
	Update runway.
	Market Evidence
	Promising
	122 days old
	Moderate-low
	Refresh interviews.
	Idea Clarity
	Strong
	Current
	High
	Continue.
	Competitor Map
	Aging
	87 days old
	Moderate
	Review before pitch.
	Pitch Deck Readiness
	Needs review
	Outdated
	Low
	Regenerate.
	This is clearer than silently changing a score.
24. User-facing examples
Example: inactive for 6 months
“Welcome back. Your previous work is preserved. Because you have been inactive for 6 months, several time-sensitive areas now require review: runway, availability, market evidence, competitor map, and external-ready documents. I have not deleted or downgraded your work, but I will not rely on old assumptions for high-commitment actions until we refresh them.”
Example: market changed
“A new competitor appears to address part of the same problem. Your idea is not automatically invalid, but your positioning score is now marked ‘Needs review.’ I recommend updating the competitor map before using partner or investor materials.”
Example: financial runway aging
“Your financial runway was last updated 39 days ago. Paid development remains paused until you confirm your current income, savings, and monthly obligations.”
Example: old strong evidence
“Your previous customer interviews were positive, but they are now 7 months old. I am keeping the evidence as historical proof, but before launch or funding materials, we need fresh validation.”
25. Developer-facing requirements
The system should support:
                                                                                                                  * score timestamps;
                                                                                                                  * evidence timestamps;
                                                                                                                  * freshness rules;
                                                                                                                  * confidence decay;
                                                                                                                  * actual score recalculation;
                                                                                                                  * sector-specific freshness intervals;
                                                                                                                  * inactivity thresholds;
                                                                                                                  * re-entry checks;
                                                                                                                  * external-signal ingestion;
                                                                                                                  * score-change reason logging;
                                                                                                                  * document-readiness updates;
