                                                                                                                     * whether benchmarks need recalibration.
At 3 years, the platform should begin to understand which routes create sustainable progress and which patterns create hidden failure.
________________


13. What to collect at 5 years
Although the question mentions 1, 3, and 10 years, a 5-year checkpoint is very useful.
Collect:
                                                                                                                     * active/closed/pivoted status;
                                                                                                                     * business/non-profit survival;
                                                                                                                     * revenue/funding stability;
                                                                                                                     * founder income;
                                                                                                                     * employment created;
                                                                                                                     * impact achieved where relevant;
                                                                                                                     * customer/user retention;
                                                                                                                     * debt and bankruptcy status;
                                                                                                                     * major growth or contraction;
                                                                                                                     * whether original venture still exists or evolved;
                                                                                                                     * whether founder started another venture;
                                                                                                                     * whether Changepreneurship influenced long-term decision-making;
                                                                                                                     * whether the user avoided or recovered from major risk.
5 years is a strong mid-point before the 10-year survival KPI.
________________


14. What to collect at 10 years
The 10-year checkpoint is the key long-term outcome measure.
A. Core 10-year venture outcome
Collect:
                                                                                                                     * is the original venture still active;
                                                                                                                     * if active, in what form;
                                                                                                                     * if not active, when and why did it stop;
                                                                                                                     * was there formal bankruptcy or insolvency;
                                                                                                                     * did it close without bankruptcy;
                                                                                                                     * did it pivot into another venture;
                                                                                                                     * was it sold, merged, acquired, handed over, or transformed;
                                                                                                                     * did it become a stable self-employment income source;
                                                                                                                     * did it become a growth company;
                                                                                                                     * did it become a sustainable non-profit/social enterprise;
                                                                                                                     * did it create lasting impact even if not still active.
B. Financial outcome
Collect:
                                                                                                                     * cumulative revenue/funding range;
                                                                                                                     * current annual revenue/funding range;
                                                                                                                     * profitability/sustainability category;
                                                                                                                     * founder income category;
                                                                                                                     * debt created and resolved;
                                                                                                                     * bankruptcy status;
                                                                                                                     * personal financial improvement/worsening;
                                                                                                                     * external funding received;
                                                                                                                     * employees/contractors created;
                                                                                                                     * taxes/contributions where available and appropriate.
C. Founder outcome
Collect:
                                                                                                                     * is the user still entrepreneurially active;
                                                                                                                     * did they start another venture;
                                                                                                                     * did they return to employment;
                                                                                                                     * did platform learning help their career even if venture closed;
                                                                                                                     * long-term skill gains;
                                                                                                                     * network gains;
                                                                                                                     * confidence/self-efficacy change;
                                                                                                                     * burnout or wellbeing outcome;
                                                                                                                     * overall life impact.
D. Platform attribution data
Collect:
                                                                                                                     * which parts of Changepreneurship were most influential;
                                                                                                                     * whether the user followed the recommended route;
                                                                                                                     * which warnings mattered;
                                                                                                                     * which documents were used;
                                                                                                                     * which AI-executed tasks mattered;
                                                                                                                     * which human support was obtained through the platform;
                                                                                                                     * whether the user believes the platform prevented harm;
                                                                                                                     * whether the user believes the platform increased chance of success.
At 10 years, the platform can measure survival and bankruptcy outcomes, but also long-term user value.
________________


15. Data collection methods
The platform should not rely only on long surveys. It should use multiple data sources, with consent.
A. In-platform data
Automatically collected while user uses the platform:
                                                                                                                     * phases completed;
                                                                                                                     * tasks completed;
                                                                                                                     * blockers triggered/resolved;
                                                                                                                     * documents generated/shared;
                                                                                                                     * evidence logged;
                                                                                                                     * AI actions performed;
                                                                                                                     * outreach sent;
                                                                                                                     * replies received;
                                                                                                                     * dashboard activity;
                                                                                                                     * credit usage;
                                                                                                                     * external integrations used.
B. User self-report follow-ups
Collected through short structured check-ins:
                                                                                                                     * venture status;
                                                                                                                     * revenue/funding range;
                                                                                                                     * registration status;
                                                                                                                     * bankruptcy/closure status;
                                                                                                                     * debt range;
                                                                                                                     * customer/funder count range;
                                                                                                                     * founder wellbeing;
                                                                                                                     * qualitative lessons;
                                                                                                                     * platform usefulness.
Keep these short. Long follow-ups will have low response rates.
C. Connected-account data, optional
With explicit permission, the platform may collect or verify:
                                                                                                                     * accounting/bookkeeping summaries;
                                                                                                                     * payment/revenue categories;
                                                                                                                     * CRM/pipeline status;
                                                                                                                     * calendar meetings;
                                                                                                                     * email outreach outcomes;
                                                                                                                     * external platform applications;
                                                                                                                     * website/landing page metrics;
                                                                                                                     * task board progress;
                                                                                                                     * marketplace activity.
This should always be optional and permission-based.
D. Public or official data, where lawful and appropriate
Where legally allowed and practically available, the platform may use public records or external sources to verify:
                                                                                                                     * business registration status;
                                                                                                                     * company closure;
                                                                                                                     * bankruptcy/insolvency events;
                                                                                                                     * public funding announcements;
                                                                                                                     * public websites;
                                                                                                                     * public social/business pages;
                                                                                                                     * public company information.
This should be used carefully and transparently.
E. Manual confirmation
For sensitive outcomes, the platform should ask the user rather than assume.
Examples:
                                                                                                                     * personal debt;
                                                                                                                     * stress/burnout;
                                                                                                                     * family impact;
                                                                                                                     * private closure reasons;
                                                                                                                     * legal disputes;
                                                                                                                     * personal financial harm.
F. Cohort analysis
The platform should compare groups over time:
                                                                                                                     * users who completed all phases vs partial users;
                                                                                                                     * users who followed warnings vs ignored warnings;
                                                                                                                     * AI-execution users vs manual-only users;
                                                                                                                     * mentor-supported users vs unsupported users;
                                                                                                                     * low-resource founders vs stable founders;
                                                                                                                     * sector families;
                                                                                                                     * founder types;
                                                                                                                     * business models;
                                                                                                                     * users who were blocked then stabilized vs users who proceeded immediately.
This is how Changepreneurship learns what works.
________________


16. Survey design principles
Long-term follow-up questions should be short, structured, and respectful.
The platform should use:
                                                                                                                     * ranges instead of exact numbers where possible;
                                                                                                                     * multiple choice where possible;
                                                                                                                     * optional open-text fields;
                                                                                                                     * sensitive-data warnings;
                                                                                                                     * clear explanation of why data is collected;
                                                                                                                     * ability to skip sensitive questions;
                                                                                                                     * ability to update previous answers;
                                                                                                                     * consent renewal for long-term follow-up.
Example revenue question:
What was your venture’s approximate annual revenue or funding in the last 12 months?
☐ €0
☐ €1–€1,000
☐ €1,001–€10,000
☐ €10,001–€50,000
☐ €50,001–€250,000
☐ €250,001+
☐ Prefer not to say
Example status question:
What is the current status of the venture?
☐ Active
☐ Paused
☐ Pivoted into a new version
☐ Closed responsibly
☐ Closed with financial loss
☐ Formal bankruptcy/insolvency
☐ Sold/merged/transferred
☐ I am not sure how to classify it
This creates useful data without demanding exact disclosure.
________________


17. Attribution problem: how to know whether Changepreneurship caused the outcome
The platform should be honest: it cannot perfectly prove causation for every user. A venture outcome depends on many factors outside the platform.
However, it can strengthen attribution by collecting:
                                                                                                                     * baseline readiness;
                                                                                                                     * phase completion;
                                                                                                                     * route followed;
                                                                                                                     * warnings triggered;
                                                                                                                     * warnings followed or ignored;
                                                                                                                     * AI tasks used;
                                                                                                                     * documents used externally;
                                                                                                                     * mentor/co-founder/funder/customer contacts generated by platform;
                                                                                                                     * user-reported usefulness;
                                                                                                                     * comparison cohorts;
                                                                                                                     * outcomes by engagement level;
                                                                                                                     * outcomes by intervention type.
Possible impact analysis:
                                                                                                                     * users who completed all phases vs users who dropped early;
                                                                                                                     * users who resolved blockers vs users who bypassed them;
                                                                                                                     * users who validated before spending vs users who did not;
                                                                                                                     * users who received mentor support vs users who did not;
                                                                                                                     * users who used AI outreach vs users who only planned;
                                                                                                                     * users with similar starting profiles but different platform engagement.
This does not create perfect proof, but it creates credible evidence.
Counterfactual data
The platform can ask:
                                                                                                                     * “Would you have started this venture without Changepreneurship?”
                                                                                                                     * “Did Changepreneurship make you start sooner, later, or not at all?”
                                                                                                                     * “Did Changepreneurship prevent you from taking a risk you would otherwise have taken?”
                                                                                                                     * “Did Changepreneurship help you stop, pivot, or stabilize?”
                                                                                                                     * “Did Changepreneurship help you get customers, mentors, funding, or co-founders?”
These questions help estimate added value.
________________


18. Measure responsible non-starts
This is critical.
If the platform works well, some users should not start a venture immediately. That may be a good outcome.
The platform should track:
                                                                                                                     * users routed to stabilization;
                                                                                                                     * users who accepted not-ready recommendation;
                                                                                                                     * users who avoided flagged spending;
                                                                                                                     * users who avoided quitting employment prematurely;
                                                                                                                     * users who paid down debt first;
                                                                                                                     * users who completed job/income tasks;
                                                                                                                     * users who returned later stronger;
                                                                                                                     * users who chose a safer idea;
                                                                                                                     * users who abandoned a bad idea before financial harm.
A user who does not start because the platform prevented a reckless start should not be counted as failure. They should be counted under harm prevention or responsible delay.
Responsible non-start outcomes
Outcome
	Interpretation
	Stabilized income before starting
	Positive preparation outcome.
	Avoided unsafe loan
	Prevented harm.
	Stopped non-viable idea before spending
	Positive evidence-based decision.
	Pivoted to safer model
	Positive adaptation.
	Returned to employment with improved skills
	Positive user outcome, even if no venture.
	Returned later and launched responsibly
	Strong delayed success outcome.
	This protects the mission from becoming “start at all costs.”
________________


19. Data privacy and ethics
Long-term data collection must be ethical and consent-based.
The platform should:
                                                                                                                     * explain what data is collected and why;
                                                                                                                     * get consent for long-term follow-up;
                                                                                                                     * allow users to opt out;
                                                                                                                     * allow users to delete personal data where legally required/applicable;
                                                                                                                     * separate identifiable user data from anonymized benchmark data;
                                                                                                                     * aggregate data before benchmarking;
                                                                                                                     * protect sensitive financial and personal information;
                                                                                                                     * avoid exposing individual users through small cohort benchmarks;
                                                                                                                     * use ranges instead of exact amounts where possible;
                                                                                                                     * minimize collection of unnecessary sensitive data;
                                                                                                                     * allow “prefer not to say” for sensitive questions;
                                                                                                                     * keep audit trails for consent.
Long-term outcome tracking is valuable, but it must not become surveillance.
Sensitive data categories
Extra care is needed for:
                                                                                                                     * personal debt;
                                                                                                                     * income;
                                                                                                                     * savings/runway;
                                                                                                                     * bankruptcy;
                                                                                                                     * mental health/burnout indicators;
                                                                                                                     * family obligations;
                                                                                                                     * legal disputes;
                                                                                                                     * immigration/work status;
                                                                                                                     * failure reasons;
                                                                                                                     * business closure;
                                                                                                                     * personal crisis;
                                                                                                                     * vulnerable-user work;
                                                                                                                     * regulated-sector issues.
These should be collected only with clear purpose and protection.
________________


20. Data retention and deletion
The platform should define what happens to long-term data.
Recommended rules:
                                                                                                                     * personal identifiable data should be deleteable where required and appropriate;
                                                                                                                     * anonymized aggregate benchmark data may be retained if properly anonymized and allowed by consent/legal basis;
                                                                                                                     * users should be told how their data contributes to benchmarks;
                                                                                                                     * users should be able to opt out of future follow-ups;
                                                                                                                     * long-term follow-up consent should be renewable;
                                                                                                                     * sensitive data should have stricter retention limits;
                                                                                                                     * records used for audit/legal purposes may require separate retention logic.
This overlaps with data ethics, but must be designed from the beginning because the KPIs require long-term tracking.
________________


21. Dashboard and product implications
To measure long-term outcomes, the Venture Operating Dashboard should include an Outcome Tracking & Alumni Block.
This block should show:
                                                                                                                     * user’s current venture status;
                                                                                                                     * last known outcome;
                                                                                                                     * follow-up due date;
                                                                                                                     * optional update form;
                                                                                                                     * data-sharing consent status;
                                                                                                                     * benchmark contribution status;
                                                                                                                     * alumni milestones;
                                                                                                                     * re-entry option;
                                                                                                                     * new venture option;
                                                                                                                     * archive/close/pivot status.
For users who leave, the platform should still offer lightweight follow-up:
                                                                                                                     * email survey;
                                                                                                                     * SMS/WhatsApp survey where appropriate and consented;
                                                                                                                     * one-click status update;
                                                                                                                     * annual update link;
                                                                                                                     * incentive for responding;
                                                                                                                     * option to reopen dashboard.
The goal is to keep outcome data flowing without requiring the user to stay subscribed forever.
________________


22. Incentives for users to provide long-term data
Long-term data collection will fail if users see no benefit.
The platform should provide value in exchange for updates.
Possible incentives:
                                                                                                                     * free annual venture health snapshot;
                                                                                                                     * updated benchmark comparison;
                                                                                                                     * renewed risk check;
                                                                                                                     * document freshness scan;
                                                                                                                     * alumni report;
                                                                                                                     * discount credits for completing follow-up;
                                                                                                                     * access to anonymized benchmark insights;
                                                                                                                     * “where are you now?” dashboard update;
                                                                                                                     * opportunity to re-enter with updated recommendations;
                                                                                                                     * founder progress timeline.
Example message:
“Complete your 1-year update and receive a free Venture Health Snapshot comparing your current status with similar founders.”
This makes data collection mutually useful.
________________


23. What to compare against
To measure the KPIs, the platform needs comparison points.
Possible comparison groups:
                                                                                                                     1. Internal cohorts
Different levels of platform engagement and route completion.
                                                                                                                     2. Pre-launch baseline
Early users before major platform improvements.
                                                                                                                     3. External public statistics
Used carefully and contextually.
                                                                                                                     4. Control-like groups
Users who started but did not complete phases, or users who only used free tools.
                                                                                                                     5. Sector-specific cohorts
Compare similar sectors and business models.
                                                                                                                     6. Founder-type cohorts
Compare users with similar starting constraints.
The platform should avoid broad claims like “we reduced bankruptcy by X%” unless the comparison is methodologically credible.
Better:
“Among users who completed the full readiness and validation route, the 3-year closure-with-debt rate was lower than among comparable users who did not complete the route.”
This is more credible than vague marketing claims.
________________


24. Leading indicators before 10-year data exists
The platform cannot wait 10 years before learning whether it works. It needs leading indicators.
Useful leading indicators:
                                                                                                                        * user completes validation before spending;
                                                                                                                        * hard blockers are resolved before launch;
                                                                                                                        * debt avoided;
                                                                                                                        * unsafe spending avoided;
                                                                                                                        * customer evidence obtained;
                                                                                                                        * first external commitment reached;
                                                                                                                        * mentor/co-founder support obtained;
                                                                                                                        * legal review completed where needed;
                                                                                                                        * responsible pivot/stop decisions;
                                                                                                                        * first revenue/funding;
                                                                                                                        * operational systems created;
                                                                                                                        * 1-year active status;
                                                                                                                        * 3-year active or responsible closure status.
These are not substitutes for 10-year outcomes, but they help the platform improve earlier.
________________


25. Metrics by platform intervention
Changepreneurship should measure which interventions create results.
Examples:
Intervention
	Outcome to track
	Financial hard block
	Reduced unsafe spending, fewer debt outcomes.
	Customer discovery route
	Higher validation quality, better product decisions.
	Mentor search automation
	More mentor conversations, better route completion.
	Co-founder search
	Better capability coverage, improved launch quality.
	Document external-readiness gates
	Fewer premature investor/partner/customer contacts.
	Marketplace integrations
	More useful external opportunities.
	AI outreach execution
	Higher response rate, faster evidence collection.
	Operations cleanup route
	Lower burnout, improved retention, better margins.
	Professional review gates
	Fewer legal/regulatory problems.
	Not-ready rerouting
	More responsible delays, lower harm rate.
	This tells the team what to improve.
________________


26. Outcome categories for reporting
For product analytics and investor reporting, the platform should report outcomes in nuanced categories.
Suggested outcome categories:
                                                                                                                        1. Started and active.
                                                                                                                        2. Started and growing.
                                                                                                                        3. Started and stable side income.
                                                                                                                        4. Started and impact-active.
                                                                                                                        5. Started but struggling.
                                                                                                                        6. Paused responsibly.
                                                                                                                        7. Pivoted based on evidence.
                                                                                                                        8. Closed responsibly without major harm.
                                                                                                                        9. Closed with financial harm.
                                                                                                                        10. Formal bankruptcy/insolvency.
                                                                                                                        11. Did not start because stabilization required.
                                                                                                                        12. Did not start because idea invalidated.
                                                                                                                        13. Returned later and started responsibly.
                                                                                                                        14. Still exploring.
                                                                                                                        15. Unknown / lost to follow-up.
The “unknown” category is important. The platform should not treat missing follow-up data as failure or success.
________________


27. Handling lost-to-follow-up users
Not all users will respond after leaving. The platform should plan for this.
Rules:
                                                                                                                        * do not assume non-response equals failure;
                                                                                                                        * track response rates by cohort;
                                                                                                                        * use optional public data where allowed;
                                                                                                                        * use statistical caution;
                                                                                                                        * report “known outcomes” separately from “all users”;
                                                                                                                        * maintain lost-to-follow-up category;
                                                                                                                        * design follow-ups to be short and valuable;
                                                                                                                        * avoid overclaiming long-term impact if follow-up rates are low.
Example reporting:
“Among users with confirmed 3-year outcomes…”
not:
“All users achieved…”
unless the data truly supports it.
________________


28. Product analytics versus user-facing benchmarking
Some long-term data should be used for product analytics but not shown directly to users.
For example, the platform may internally learn that founders with less than 1 month runway and no stable income have much higher failure or debt-risk rates. User-facing communication should translate that into:
“Your current runway is below the safe threshold for paid venture actions.”
not:
“People like you usually fail.”
Internal analytics can be blunt. User-facing communication must be precise, respectful, and actionable.
________________


29. Developer-facing data model
The system should support records for:
                                                                                                                        * User Baseline Record;
                                                                                                                        * Venture Baseline Record;
                                                                                                                        * Exit Snapshot Record;
                                                                                                                        * Follow-Up Record;
                                                                                                                        * Outcome Record;
                                                                                                                        * Venture Status History;
                                                                                                                        * Financial Outcome Record;
                                                                                                                        * Harm Prevention Record;
                                                                                                                        * Bankruptcy/Insolvency Record;
                                                                                                                        * Closure Reason Record;
                                                                                                                        * Pivot Record;
                                                                                                                        * Revenue/Funding Range Record;
                                                                                                                        * Employment/Income Impact Record;
                                                                                                                        * User Wellbeing Outcome Record;
                                                                                                                        * Intervention Exposure Record;
                                                                                                                        * AI Execution Usage Record;
                                                                                                                        * External Support Record;
                                                                                                                        * Consent Record;
                                                                                                                        * Data Deletion/Anonymization Record;
                                                                                                                        * Benchmark Contribution Record.
Each outcome record should include:
                                                                                                                        * date;
                                                                                                                        * source;
                                                                                                                        * confidence level;
                                                                                                                        * whether self-reported or verified;
                                                                                                                        * sensitivity level;
                                                                                                                        * consent status;
                                                                                                                        * benchmark eligibility;
                                                                                                                        * anonymization status.
________________


30. What the platform should not do
The platform should not:
                                                                                                                        * measure only venture starts;
                                                                                                                        * count reckless starts as success;
                                                                                                                        * treat every non-start as failure;
                                                                                                                        * treat every closure as failure;
                                                                                                                        * ignore responsible stops and pivots;
                                                                                                                        * ignore debt, burnout, or personal harm;
                                                                                                                        * claim bankruptcy reduction before enough long-term data exists;
                                                                                                                        * compare unlike users and sectors;
                                                                                                                        * collect sensitive long-term data without consent;
                                                                                                                        * make follow-up surveys too long;
                                                                                                                        * assume missing data means failure;
                                                                                                                        * use user data for benchmarks without anonymization safeguards;
                                                                                                                        * hide how outcome data is used;
                                                                                                                        * optimize for vanity KPIs instead of responsible entrepreneurship.
________________


Final answer
To measure the two long-term KPIs — increasing the percentage of people who become entrepreneurs and decreasing the percentage of ventures that go bankrupt over 10 years — Changepreneurship should create a Longitudinal Venture Outcome System. It should collect baseline data when the user starts, an exit snapshot when the user completes, leaves, launches, pauses, or becomes inactive, and follow-up data at 30 days, 90 days, 6 months, 1 year, 2 years, 3 years, 5 years, 7 years, and 10 years.
The platform should collect data on venture status, launch status, revenue/funding, customers/users/beneficiaries, registration, debt, financial harm, closure reason, bankruptcy/insolvency, pivots, responsible stops, founder income, employment status, mentor/co-founder support, legal/professional issues, documents used, AI actions used, warnings followed or ignored, and whether the platform prevented unsafe decisions. It should also measure responsible non-starts, because preventing a user from quitting work, taking debt, or spending money on an unvalidated idea can be a successful outcome.
The platform should not measure success only by “more people started companies.” It should measure whether users started responsibly, avoided preventable harm, survived longer, closed more cleanly when necessary, and made better evidence-based decisions. Long-term data should be consent-based, privacy-protective, aggregated for benchmarks, and carefully separated into known outcomes and lost-to-follow-up users.
⭐ Non-negotiable: Changepreneurship must not optimize for reckless entrepreneurship. A user who starts responsibly, delays responsibly, pivots responsibly, or stops before preventable harm may all represent successful platform outcomes.
	 
SECTION 7   THE AI ROLE — WHAT SHOULD AI DO VS. FIXED RULES?
 
AI is powerful but expensive and unpredictable. Fixed rules are consistent but rigid.
7.1
For each current AI function, should it stay as AI or become a fixed rule? 
The current AI functions should not simply “stay AI” or “become fixed rules.” Most of them should become hybrid functions. This means the structure, thresholds, safety limits, and required fields should be rule-based, while AI should handle interpretation, summarization, writing, explanation, personalization, and edge cases.


The platform should avoid using AI as the sole decision-maker for anything that blocks or unlocks major user progress. At the same time, it should avoid reducing deeply human or contextual work to rigid formulas.


The recommended answer is:


Every current AI function should be redesigned as a hybrid system: fixed-rule backbone, AI interpretation layer, user-facing explanation layer, and approval/audit layer where needed.


1. Summary table
Current AI function        Keep as AI? Or fixed rule?        Recommended approach        Why
Generating the founder profile summary        Hybrid        Fixed Founder Operating Matrix + AI-written summary        The profile needs consistent dimensions, but the explanation must be personal and nuanced.
Calculating the success probability score        Mostly fixed rules + AI explanation        Replace with readiness/risk forecast; fixed scoring model, AI explains and contextualizes        A “success probability” must not be a black-box AI guess. It needs auditable logic and confidence labels.
Deciding the next step / routing the user        Hybrid, with fixed hard gates        Fixed blockers and routing rules + AI scenario interpretation        Hard blockers must be consistent; AI helps with ambiguous mixed cases and user-friendly explanation.
Writing the business plan        AI, governed by fixed structure        AI-generated document from structured records and templates        Writing needs AI, but content must come from approved venture records and evidence labels.
Identifying strengths and growth areas        Hybrid        Fixed taxonomy and status logic + AI synthesis        Strengths/gaps should be consistent, but AI can explain combinations, compensations, and edge cases.


The most important architecture decision is that AI should not invent the underlying truth. It should interpret, structure, explain, and generate from the venture records.


2. Function 1 — Generating the founder profile summary
Recommendation


This should be a hybrid function.


Fixed rules should define the underlying profile structure.
AI should generate the human-readable summary.
The dashboard should show a modular Founder Operating Matrix rather than a rigid archetype.
What should be fixed-rule based


The platform should use fixed structures for:


profile dimensions;
required inputs;
scoring/status labels;
hard blockers;
sensitive-dimension handling;
evidence requirements;
confidence labels;
compensation logic;
founder-idea fit categories;
founder-market fit categories;
support-needs categories;
update triggers;
version history.


For example, the platform should consistently evaluate:


financial readiness;
time capacity;
personal readiness;
skills and experience;
execution behaviour;
evidence discipline;
communication ability;
support network;
founder-idea fit;
founder-market fit;
risk awareness;
operational discipline;
automation leverage.


These dimensions should not change randomly depending on how the AI feels.


What AI should do


AI should:


summarize the user’s profile in clear language;
explain combinations of strengths and risks;
identify contradictions;
explain compensating factors;
personalize the operating recommendation;
adapt tone to user type;
generate mentor/advisor summary versions;
translate the matrix into practical next actions;
explain why the platform recommends a route.


Example AI-generated summary:


“Your strongest current assets are domain knowledge, persistence, and analytical thinking. The main risk is not idea quality; it is that your current weekly capacity is too limited for the build route you selected. Your technical gap is acceptable for now because the recommended next step is manual validation, not software development. Your operating route should be low-capital validation, one customer-discovery task per week, and mentor support before any paid development.”


What AI must not do


AI should not:


invent a founder identity;
lock the user into an archetype;
label the user as permanently weak;
ignore hard blockers because the written summary sounds positive;
generate a flattering profile that hides risk;
expose sensitive personal profile data externally without approval;
change profile status without evidence or rule-based trigger.
Why hybrid is best


A founder profile needs consistency and nuance. Fixed rules provide consistency. AI provides nuance. Without fixed rules, the profile becomes too subjective. Without AI, the profile becomes too mechanical and hard to understand.


Final decision


Keep AI for the narrative profile summary, but use fixed-rule profile dimensions, status labels, blocker logic, evidence rules, and update triggers underneath.


3. Function 2 — Calculating the success probability score
Recommendation


This should not remain as a black-box AI-generated “success probability score.” It should be redesigned as a mostly fixed-rule scoring and forecasting system, with AI used for explanation, not for inventing the score.


The term “success probability” is risky because it suggests mathematical certainty that the platform cannot honestly provide. A user may interpret “72% success probability” as if the platform knows their future. That is dangerous.


Recommended replacement


Replace “success probability score” with a set of clearer metrics:


Venture Readiness Status
Risk Level
Evidence Confidence
Next-Step Eligibility
External-Readiness Status
Survival-Risk Indicators
Founder-Venture Fit Status
Route Confidence


If a numerical score is used, it should be described as:


“Readiness score for the current next step”


not:


“Probability of success.”


What should be fixed-rule based


The score model should be based on auditable inputs such as:


financial runway;
time capacity;
evidence quality;
market validation;
business model clarity;
legal/professional-review risk;
product feasibility;
founder-idea fit;
founder-market fit;
execution behaviour;
operations readiness;
support network;
technical capability;
stage-specific requirements;
sector risk;
capital intensity;
current blockers.


The scoring system should use fixed formulas, thresholds, weighting ranges, or rules that developers and product owners can inspect.


What AI should do


AI should:


explain the score in plain language;
identify which factors drove the score;
explain what would improve it;
distinguish score from confidence;
detect whether the user is overperforming or under-evidenced;
generate scenario explanations;
explain why a high overall score still does not override a hard blocker;
summarize score changes over time.


Example:


“Your readiness for product development is currently blocked, not because the idea is weak, but because market evidence is insufficient. Improving financial runway will help, but it will not unlock product development unless customer evidence also improves.”


What AI must not do


AI should not:


invent a probability of business success;
say “you have an 83% chance of succeeding” without rigorous statistical basis;
override fixed hard blockers;
treat weak evidence as strong because the user wrote well;
generate a precise-looking number from vague inputs;
hide uncertainty;
let an overall score average away legal, financial, or ethical danger.
Score types that may be acceptable


Acceptable:


“Readiness for next step: Warning”;
“Evidence confidence: Low”;
“External-readiness: Not ready”;
“Financial safety: Hard block”;
“Route confidence: Moderate”;
“Market validation: Promising but incomplete.”


Use numeric values cautiously and contextually:


internal score: 68/100;
user-facing status: Promising but incomplete;
confidence: Moderate;
next action: complete pricing validation.
Why mostly fixed-rule is best


Scoring must be consistent, explainable, auditable, and defensible. AI can help communicate the score, but it should not be the source of the score.


Final decision


Do not keep “success probability score” as free-form AI. Replace it with a rule-based readiness/risk/eligibility scoring system. Use AI to explain, contextualize, and translate the score into action.


4. Function 3 — Deciding the next step / routing the user
Recommendation


This should be a hybrid function with fixed hard gates.


Routing is one of the most important functions in the platform. It determines whether the user continues, pauses, validates, stabilizes, seeks a mentor, gets professional review, creates documents, launches, pivots, or stops. This cannot be left entirely to AI.


What should be fixed-rule based


Fixed rules should control:


hard blockers;
legal/ethical hard stops;
professional-review triggers;
financial hard blocks;
no-spend rules;
minimum evidence gates;
external-ready document gates;
user permission requirements;
automation permission limits;
phase prerequisites;
required outputs per phase;
re-entry checks after inactivity;
document update rules;
data privacy and sensitive-action rules.


Examples:


If financial readiness is hard block, paid development is blocked.
If legal/professional review is required, launch and external claims are blocked.
If market evidence is missing, investor-ready materials cannot be marked investor-ready.
If document is outdated, AI cannot send it externally.
If user has been inactive for 6 months, re-entry check is required.


These rules should not depend on AI discretion.


What AI should do


AI should:


interpret mixed cases;
explain why the route was chosen;
propose the most useful next action;
adapt the route to user context;
identify edge cases;
suggest compensating routes;
generate task plans;
handle nuance between similar routes;
ask clarifying questions when needed;
prioritize among multiple possible next actions;
translate rules into respectful user-facing language.


Example:


Fixed rule says:


Product development blocked because market evidence is below threshold.


AI explains:


“Your idea is clear enough to test, but not yet strong enough to build. The safest next step is customer discovery. I recommend sending interview requests to 10 target users before spending money on product development.”


Routing should have layers


The routing engine should use layers:


Safety layer
Legal, ethical, personal, financial, and professional-review gates.
Phase layer
Determines which phase the user is in and which prerequisites are missing.
Evidence layer
Determines whether enough proof exists for the next commitment.
Fit layer
Founder-idea fit, founder-market fit, support needs.
Execution layer
Time, tasks, capacity, automation, outreach, operations.
AI optimization layer
Chooses the most useful next action inside the allowed route.


AI should operate mainly in layer 6 and explain layers 1–5. It should not override layers 1–3.


What AI must not do


AI should not:


route around hard blockers;
let the user skip validation because they sound confident;
allow high-risk actions because the user insists;
downgrade professional-review requirements;
recommend spending when runway is unsafe;
send external materials without approval;
choose a route based only on persuasive writing;
hide the fact that a rule caused the route.
Why hybrid is best


Routing needs safety and flexibility. Fixed rules provide safety. AI provides flexibility.


Final decision


Routing should be hybrid. Fixed rules determine what is allowed, blocked, required, or unsafe. AI determines the best next action inside those boundaries and explains the route clearly.


5. Function 4 — Writing the business plan
Recommendation


This should remain strongly AI-assisted, but it must be governed by fixed structure, source records, evidence labels, and document-readiness rules.


Writing a business plan is exactly the kind of work AI can do well: structuring information, drafting, summarizing, adjusting tone, creating versions for different audiences, and updating sections. However, AI should not invent facts, evidence, financials, traction, legal conclusions, or market validation.


What should be fixed-rule based


Fixed rules should define:


business plan structure;
required sections;
required fields by venture type;
evidence labels;
missing-data warnings;
professional-review warnings;
external-readiness gates;
document status;
versioning;
dependency mapping;
branding rules;
audience-specific variants;
user approval requirements;
update triggers;
what claims are prohibited or require review.


For example, a bank business plan and investor business plan should not be the same document. The fixed rules should determine which sections are needed for which audience.


What AI should do


AI should:


write the plan clearly;
adapt it to the audience;
summarize the venture;
turn structured records into coherent sections;
identify missing sections;
create alternative versions;
simplify language;
improve professionalism;
regenerate affected sections after changes;
produce mentor, bank, investor, grant, partner, or internal versions;
preserve user voice where appropriate.
Source-of-truth rule


AI must write from structured venture records, not free imagination.


The business plan should pull from:


venture concept;
founder profile;
market evidence;
business model;
financial model;
risk register;
product/service scope;
operations plan;
validation results;
prototype results;
decision log;
user-approved assumptions.


If information is missing, AI should not fill it with fake confidence. It should mark the gap.


Example:


“Pricing is currently an assumption. The business plan can include it as a draft assumption, but it cannot be treated as validated until pricing evidence is collected.”


What AI must not do


AI should not:


invent customers, traction, revenue, market data, testimonials, partnerships, funding, or team members;
turn assumptions into facts;
remove risk warnings to make the plan look better;
create investor-ready plans before validation;
write legal, tax, or regulatory conclusions;
overwrite user-approved versions without approval;
generate external-ready documents from outdated records;
use generic filler that makes every plan sound the same.
Why AI is appropriate here


Business-plan writing is high-context, language-heavy, and version-heavy. AI is useful. But because the plan may be shared externally, fixed rules must protect truth, evidence, status, and version control.


Final decision


Keep business-plan writing as AI-assisted, but make it record-driven, template-governed, evidence-labelled, versioned, and blocked from external-ready status until readiness gates are passed.


6. Function 5 — Identifying strengths and growth areas
Recommendation


This should be a hybrid function.


Strengths and growth areas should be derived from a consistent taxonomy and scoring logic, but AI should synthesize them into useful, context-aware language.


What should be fixed-rule based


Fixed rules should define:


dimensions assessed;
what counts as strength;
what counts as warning;
what counts as blocker;
what counts as unknown;
compensation rules;
stage relevance;
severity levels;
evidence requirements;
trend labels;
support categories;
dashboard consequences.


For example:


low technical skill is not automatically a blocker;
low technical skill becomes a blocker if the user wants to build a technical product without support;
low market evidence blocks product development;
low communication ability blocks outreach only until scripts and clarity are improved;
low financial runway blocks paid actions but not free validation.


These rules must be consistent.


What AI should do


AI should:


explain the strengths in context;
identify how strengths can be used;
explain growth areas without shaming;
connect weaknesses to actions;
explain compensation;
detect edge-case combinations;
adapt the message to user psychology;
create mentor/co-founder/freelancer support recommendations;
update the Founder Operating Matrix summary;
identify which growth areas matter now versus later.


Example:


“Your communication ability is not currently the highest-risk issue because you are not yet pitching externally. The more urgent growth area is market access: you do not yet have a reliable way to reach target customers. Communication will become more important once outreach begins.”


This is the kind of nuance AI can provide.


What AI must not do


AI should not:


flatter the user by turning every weakness into a strength;
identify growth areas disconnected from the current route;
shame the user;
hide hard blockers under positive language;
generate generic personal-development advice;
treat missing information as weakness without saying it is unknown;
confuse personality with current operating fit.
Why hybrid is best


Strengths and growth areas require both structured assessment and nuanced explanation. Fixed rules identify the signals. AI turns them into practical guidance.


Final decision


Use fixed taxonomy, thresholds, blocker logic, and compensation rules to identify strengths and growth areas. Use AI to explain them, prioritize them, and connect them to the user’s route and dashboard actions.


7. General rule: AI should interpret, not govern hard truth


Across all current functions, AI should be used primarily for:


summarizing;
explaining;
writing;
translating complexity;
generating options;
adapting tone;
detecting patterns;
preparing tasks;
drafting documents;
suggesting next actions within rules;
handling edge cases;
assisting with real-world execution.


AI should not be the sole authority for:


hard blockers;
legal/ethical stops;
financial spending gates;
professional-review requirements;
user permissions;
billing/cost controls;
external-sharing eligibility;
document status changes after external sharing;
deletion/retention rules;
compliance-sensitive decisions;
success probability calculation;
safety-critical recommendations.


This keeps the system useful without becoming unpredictable.


8. Fixed rules should control non-negotiables


Fixed rules should control anything that must be consistent, auditable, and enforceable.


Examples:


Safety rules
do not support illegal or harmful venture directions;
trigger professional review for regulated sectors;
block unsafe external claims;
prevent unsupported medical, financial, legal, or safety claims.
Financial rules
block paid development when runway is below threshold;
require budget approval before paid AI execution;
show cost estimates before credit consumption;
warn before risky loans or inventory purchases.
Evidence rules
do not mark investor-ready without sufficient evidence;
do not treat friends/family feedback as strong validation;
require behavioural evidence for high-commitment decisions;
mark old evidence as stale.
Document rules
preserve shared versions;
mark affected documents outdated when source records change;
require approval before external sharing;
use status labels.
Permission rules
do not send messages without approval;
do not create accounts without consent;
do not share sensitive data without explicit permission;
log external actions.


These rules should be predictable and not left to AI judgment.


9. AI should handle ambiguity and edge cases


Fixed rules alone cannot handle all combinations of user and venture context. AI is especially valuable for edge cases.


Examples:


strong founder but wrong idea;
weak founder today but high future potential;
high traction but severe burnout;
low runway but no-cost validation route available;
deep-tech founder with strong invention but weak market language;
impact founder with strong mission but no funding logic;
accidental entrepreneur with real demand but operational collapse;
user who appears overperforming but has weak evidence;
user with unusual constraints not captured by standard forms.


AI should analyze these situations and propose the best route inside fixed boundaries.


10. AI cost control


Because AI is expensive, not every function should use AI every time.


The platform should use fixed rules first where possible, then call AI when:


explanation is needed;
synthesis is needed;
user-facing language must be generated;
edge cases appear;
documents are generated;
multiple signals conflict;
personalization matters;
real-world task execution requires drafting or reasoning.


Examples:


Task        Use fixed rules first?        Use AI when?
Checking if runway below threshold        Yes        Explain implications and next route.
Updating dashboard status        Yes        Summarize changes for user.
Marking document outdated        Yes        Draft updated section if user requests.
Detecting missing market evidence        Yes        Generate outreach strategy.
Routing to customer discovery        Yes        Personalize task plan.
Writing pitch deck        No, structure fixed        AI writes from records.
Explaining mixed profile        Partly        AI synthesizes nuance.


This keeps costs lower and reliability higher.


11. AI confidence and auditability


Whenever AI contributes to an important result, the platform should store:


what AI generated;
what source data it used;
what rules constrained it;
what confidence level applies;
whether the user approved it;
whether it affected routing;
whether it was shared externally;
what version was used.


This is important for traceability.


AI outputs should be auditable, especially when they influence:


routing;
external materials;
user warnings;
professional-review recommendations;
automation actions;
document regeneration;
mentor/co-founder/funder/customer outreach.
12. Human/user approval layer


Some decisions should not be made by either AI alone or fixed rules alone. They require user confirmation or professional/human involvement.


User approval required
sending outreach;
posting publicly;
sharing documents externally;
spending credits above threshold;
creating external accounts;
applying to programs;
contacting investors, banks, funders, government bodies, or legal professionals;
changing official venture direction;
approving final external documents;
accepting risk disclaimers.
Professional input required
legal conclusions;
tax/accounting conclusions;
regulated financial services;
medical/health claims;
safety-critical products;
employment law;
IP/patent matters;
insurance needs;
regulated education/certification;
public-sector procurement where required.


The AI can prepare the user for these decisions, but not replace the responsible party.


13. Function classification framework for future features


For future AI features, the platform should classify each function using four questions:


Does this require consistency and auditability?
If yes, use fixed rules.
Does this require nuance, synthesis, or communication?
If yes, use AI.
Can this create financial, legal, reputational, or personal harm?
If yes, require fixed gates and user approval.
Can the user reasonably understand and override the decision?
If no, make it more transparent and conservative.


This framework lets developers decide where AI belongs.


Rule of thumb
Function type        Best approach
Hard safety gate        Fixed rule.
Legal/ethical/professional trigger        Fixed rule + professional referral.
Numerical calculation        Fixed formula.
User-facing explanation        AI.
Document writing        AI with templates and source records.
Routing        Hybrid.
External action        AI preparation + user approval + fixed permission rules.
Scoring        Fixed model + AI explanation.
Edge-case interpretation        AI within fixed constraints.
Billing/cost control        Fixed rule.
Personalization        AI.
Audit/version control        Fixed rule.
14. Practical developer architecture


A developer-ready architecture could use four layers.


Layer 1 — Rule Engine


Responsible for:


thresholds;
blockers;
permissions;
scoring formulas;
document statuses;
update policies;
professional-review triggers;
external-readiness gates;
billing limits;
compliance constraints.
Layer 2 — Venture Record System


Responsible for:


founder records;
venture records;
assumptions;
evidence;
contacts;
tasks;
documents;
risks;
decisions;
financials;
integrations;
approvals.
Layer 3 — AI Reasoning and Generation Layer


Responsible for:


synthesis;
summaries;
document generation;
outreach drafting;
explanation;
personalized coaching;
edge-case interpretation;
recommendations within allowed routes.
Layer 4 — Approval and Audit Layer


Responsible for:


user approvals;
version history;
external-sharing logs;
automation logs;
consent;
rollback;
cost history;
traceability.


This architecture lets AI be powerful while keeping the platform safe and controllable.


15. Example: how hybrid logic works in practice
Scenario


A non-technical founder with 2 months runway wants to build an AI SaaS product immediately.


Fixed rules detect
runway below paid-development threshold;
no technical capability;
no market evidence;
technical product requires support;
product development blocked;
investor-ready materials blocked.
AI explains


“Your idea may be promising, but paid product development is not the responsible next step. You currently have limited runway, no validated customer evidence, and no technical execution support. The safest route is a manual or no-code validation test, supported by customer discovery. I can help draft outreach and create a no-code validation plan.”


Dashboard actions created
Financial Stabilization Block;
Customer Discovery Block;
Technical Support / Co-Founder Block;
