                                                                                                                                    * Slack/Teams summarization;
                                                                                                                                    * Notion workspace generation;
                                                                                                                                    * Upwork freelancer management;
                                                                                                                                    * payment/billing integrations;
                                                                                                                                    * form submission;
                                                                                                                                    * grant portal monitoring.
The connection itself is not the paid value. The processing and execution are.
________________


16. Paid actions should be approved before execution
Every paid action should show:
                                                                                                                                    * action name;
                                                                                                                                    * short purpose;
                                                                                                                                    * estimated credit cost;
                                                                                                                                    * possible range if uncertain;
                                                                                                                                    * whether external data is used;
                                                                                                                                    * whether external action will occur;
                                                                                                                                    * whether manual alternative exists;
                                                                                                                                    * what the user will receive;
                                                                                                                                    * approval button.
Example:
“Generate full competitor scan — estimated cost: 18 Venture Credits. This will search external sources, summarize competitors, and produce a comparison table. Manual alternative: use the free competitor checklist.”
This protects the user from surprise costs.
________________


17. Paid actions should have caps
Users should be able to set spending limits.
Useful caps:
                                                                                                                                    * daily credit cap;
                                                                                                                                    * monthly credit cap;
                                                                                                                                    * per-action approval threshold;
                                                                                                                                    * auto-stop when credits are low;
                                                                                                                                    * maximum cost per research task;
                                                                                                                                    * maximum cost per automation;
                                                                                                                                    * no external action without approval;
                                                                                                                                    * no recurring monitoring without explicit consent.
This is especially important because the platform may serve financially cautious founders.
________________


18. Paid should not mean exploitative
The platform should never make users pay because they are confused, desperate, or vulnerable.
It should not charge for:
                                                                                                                                    * basic safety warnings;
                                                                                                                                    * explaining why something is risky;
                                                                                                                                    * basic pricing clarity;
                                                                                                                                    * consent management;
                                                                                                                                    * disconnecting accounts;
                                                                                                                                    * deleting data;
                                                                                                                                    * billing support;
                                                                                                                                    * access to already-paid outputs;
                                                                                                                                    * manual alternatives;
                                                                                                                                    * basic account access.
The paid layer must be tied to productive work, not dependency.
________________


19. Recommended paid categories
A practical paid structure could look like this:
Category
	Example
	Pricing logic
	AI analysis
	Founder Operating Matrix
	Venture Credits
	Documents
	Business plan, pitch deck
	Venture Credits
	Research
	Market scan, competitor scan

	Connected data

	________________


20. Final answer for section 8.3
Paid or metered features should include deep AI analysis, major document generation, market research, competitor scans, connected-account processing, outreach, automation, monitoring, advanced workflows, integrations, data rooms, large storage, professional-review packages, and regeneration of major outputs.
The platform should charge when it performs meaningful work, consumes real resources, creates valuable business assets, saves substantial time, or acts externally on behalf of the user.
The user should always see the estimated cost before the action starts, should be able to approve or reject the action, should have access to manual alternatives where possible, and should be protected by spending caps.
⭐ Non-negotiable: paid usage must be transparent, consent-based, and tied to real value — never to confusion, safety, basic control, or hidden dependency.
	 
 
8.4
Are there organizations that would buy bulk access (universities, incubators, corporate outplacement, innovation programs)? List any you already have in mind.
Changepreneurship should use a simple and transparent pricing structure based on the actual cost of delivering the service, with a standard markup applied on top.
The preferred model is:
Cost price × standard markup = customer price
This keeps pricing understandable, flexible, and commercially sustainable without forcing users into complicated subscriptions, credit systems, or artificial feature limitations.
The goal is not to make the pricing structure overly technical. Users should understand what they are paying for, and the business should be able to explain prices in a straightforward way.
________________


8.4.1 Standard pricing
For regular commercial users, Changepreneurship should apply its normal markup to the underlying delivery cost.
This delivery cost may include:
                                                                                                                                    * AI usage;
                                                                                                                                    * software infrastructure;
                                                                                                                                    * data processing;
                                                                                                                                    * document generation;
                                                                                                                                    * research time;
                                                                                                                                    * human review;
                                                                                                                                    * administrative handling;
                                                                                                                                    * support;
                                                                                                                                    * external tools or services;
                                                                                                                                    * operational risk;
                                                                                                                                    * platform maintenance.
The standard markup should ensure that Changepreneurship remains financially healthy and can continue improving the platform.
The principle is:
Regular customers pay the normal commercial rate so the platform can remain sustainable.
This avoids underpricing the service while still keeping the pricing logic easy to understand.
________________


8.4.2 Reduced markup for special organizations
For special organizations, Changepreneurship may apply a reduced markup.
This group may include:
                                                                                                                                    * universities;
                                                                                                                                    * incubators;
                                                                                                                                    * innovation programs;
                                                                                                                                    * foundations;
                                                                                                                                    * social-impact organizations;
                                                                                                                                    * public-interest initiatives;
                                                                                                                                    * selected non-profit partners.
For these organizations, the markup may be reduced to approximately:
Cost price × 1.75
This represents roughly a 50% reduction compared with the normal commercial markup, assuming the normal markup is approximately double that level.
The purpose is not merely to offer a discount. It is a deliberate social contribution.
Changepreneurship can position this as:
“Our way of giving back to society is to make our support more accessible to organizations that help founders, students, innovators, and public-interest initiatives.”
________________


8.4.3 Why this model is better
This model is better because it is simple.
It avoids:
                                                                                                                                    * complicated credit systems;
                                                                                                                                    * confusing subscription tiers;
                                                                                                                                    * hidden usage limits;
                                                                                                                                    * artificial restrictions;
                                                                                                                                    * users having to calculate what each action costs;
                                                                                                                                    * internal complexity in billing;
                                                                                                                                    * unnecessary pricing bureaucracy.
Instead, the model is based on a clear question:
“What does it cost us to deliver this properly, and what markup is fair for this type of customer?”
This makes pricing easier to explain, easier to negotiate, and easier to adapt to different types of clients.
________________


8.4.4 Recommended pricing principle
The recommended principle is:
Changepreneurship should charge regular commercial clients at the normal commercial markup, while offering a reduced markup of approximately 1.75× cost price to selected organizations that create wider social value.
This gives the business two important advantages:
                                                                                                                                    1. Commercial sustainability
Regular customers pay enough to keep the platform profitable and scalable.
                                                                                                                                    2. Social contribution
Special organizations receive meaningful price relief, making the service more accessible for projects with educational, entrepreneurial, social, or public-interest value.
________________


8.4.5 Boundaries for reduced markup
The reduced markup should not apply automatically to everyone.
It should be reserved for organizations whose work contributes to a broader public or social purpose.
Examples include:
                                                                                                                                       * universities supporting students or researchers;
                                                                                                                                       * incubators supporting early-stage founders;
                                                                                                                                       * innovation programs helping entrepreneurs or communities;
                                                                                                                                       * foundations funding public-interest initiatives;
                                                                                                                                       * non-profits supporting social, educational, environmental, or civic goals.
Changepreneurship should retain discretion to decide whether an organization qualifies.
This is important because the reduced markup is not a normal sales discount. It is a strategic contribution.
________________


8.4.6 Suggested wording for external communication
Externally, this could be explained as follows:
Changepreneurship uses a transparent cost-plus pricing model. For regular commercial clients, we apply our standard markup to the cost of delivery. For selected universities, incubators, innovation programs, foundations, and other public-interest organizations, we offer a reduced markup of approximately 1.75× cost price. This is part of our commitment to making entrepreneurial support more accessible and is our way of giving back to society.
________________


8.4.7 Final version for the document
Changepreneurship should use a simple cost-plus pricing structure. Instead of relying on complicated credit systems, subscription tiers, or artificial usage restrictions, the platform should calculate pricing based on the actual cost of delivering the service plus a markup.
For regular commercial users, Changepreneurship should apply its normal commercial markup. This ensures the company remains financially sustainable and has enough margin to maintain, improve, and scale the platform.
For special organizations, such as universities, incubators, innovation programs, foundations, and selected public-interest organizations, Changepreneurship may apply a reduced markup of approximately 1.75× cost price. This represents a meaningful reduction compared with the normal commercial markup and reflects Changepreneurship’s intention to give back to society by making its services more accessible to organizations that support founders, students, innovators, and social-impact initiatives.
This model keeps pricing transparent, flexible, and easy to explain. It allows Changepreneurship to remain commercially viable while still supporting organizations that create broader societal value.
	 
SECTION 9   EDGE CASES & SPECIAL SITUATIONS
 
Every product breaks in unexpected ways. These need intentional decisions — not accidental ones.
9.1
A user gets routed to "fix your finances first," then disappears for 6 months. What does the platform do when they return?
When a user returns after being routed to “fix your finances first”, the platform should not simply restart them from zero or assume that nothing has changed. It should treat the return as a re-entry moment and perform a fresh but lightweight reassessment.
The platform should first acknowledge the user’s previous status:
“Last time, the recommendation was to stabilise your personal finances before continuing with the entrepreneurship track. A lot can change in six months, so let’s quickly review your current situation.”
The platform should then check whether the user’s financial situation has improved, stayed the same, or worsened. This should not be done through a long onboarding process again, but through a short update flow covering the essentials:
                                                                                                                                       1. current income situation;
                                                                                                                                       2. debt or payment pressure;
                                                                                                                                       3. savings or emergency buffer;
                                                                                                                                       4. monthly financial stability;
                                                                                                                                       5. available time and mental capacity;
                                                                                                                                       6. whether the user still wants to continue with the same idea.
Based on this reassessment, the platform should choose one of three routes.
Route 1: The user is now stable enough
If the user has improved their financial situation sufficiently, the platform can allow them back into the entrepreneurship track. It should not make them repeat everything. Instead, it should reopen their previous idea, show what was already saved, and ask whether they want to continue, update, or start fresh.
The message could be:
“Your situation appears more stable than before. You can now continue developing your idea. Would you like to resume your previous concept, update it, or start with a new one?”
Route 2: The user is still financially vulnerable
If the user is still under financial pressure, the platform should again advise caution. However, it should not simply reject them. It can offer a safer path, such as low-risk preparation, learning, research, or planning that does not require financial investment.
The message could be:
“Your financial situation still looks fragile. Starting a business with financial pressure can increase risk. You can continue with preparation tasks, but the platform does not recommend investing money, quitting work, taking loans, or making major commitments yet.”
This keeps the user engaged without pushing them into unsafe decisions.
Route 3: The situation is worse or urgent
If the user’s financial situation has worsened, the platform should pause the entrepreneurship route again and guide them toward financial stabilisation resources. Depending on the country and context, this may include budgeting support, debt advice, benefit checks, or local support services.
The message could be:
“Based on your answers, the safest next step is still to focus on financial stability first. The entrepreneurship track will remain available, but the platform recommends dealing with the financial pressure before taking business risks.”
What the platform should remember
The platform should remember the user’s previous state, but it should not permanently label them as unsuitable. A six-month absence should trigger a reassessment, not a punishment.
It should remember:
                                                                                                                                       * the previous recommendation;
                                                                                                                                       * the user’s original idea;
                                                                                                                                       * the reason they were routed to “fix your finances first”;
                                                                                                                                       * any saved notes, plans, or goals;
                                                                                                                                       * the date of the last assessment;
                                                                                                                                       * what changed since then.
Core principle
The platform should behave like a responsible advisor:
“You are welcome back. Let’s check what changed before deciding whether entrepreneurship is now responsible.”
The user should feel that the platform remembers them, respects their progress, and protects them from unnecessary risk. The platform should neither blindly restart the entrepreneurship process nor permanently block the user because of a previous financial warning.
	 
 
9.2
Two users have nearly identical profiles and nearly identical business ideas. Should the platform treat them differently? Should it warn them?
Yes. The platform may treat them differently, but only if the differences are based on relevant personal context, risk factors, execution capacity, timing, constraints, and user goals — not simply because the ideas are similar.
The platform should not automatically warn both users that someone else has a similar idea, especially if doing so would reveal confidential user information. However, it can warn each user in a general way that similar ideas may already exist and that differentiation, execution, validation, and timing matter more than uniqueness alone.
9.2 Answer
If two users have nearly identical profiles and nearly identical business ideas, the platform should not assume that both users should receive the exact same advice. Similar profiles and similar ideas do not necessarily mean the same outcome, the same level of risk, or the same recommended next step.
The platform should treat them similarly where the facts are the same, but differently where relevant circumstances differ.
For example, the business idea may be almost identical, but one user may have:
                                                                                                                                       * more available savings;
                                                                                                                                       * better emotional resilience;
                                                                                                                                       * stronger domain experience;
                                                                                                                                       * more time available;
                                                                                                                                       * better access to potential customers;
                                                                                                                                       * stronger technical skills;
                                                                                                                                       * fewer family or debt pressures;
                                                                                                                                       * a clearer route to validation;
                                                                                                                                       * a safer fallback plan.
In that case, the platform can reasonably guide one user toward active testing, while advising the other user to stay in preparation mode, reduce personal risk, or strengthen their foundation first.
The important point is this:
The platform should not judge only the business idea. It should judge the combination of the idea, the person, the timing, the risk, and the user’s ability to execute.
Should the platform warn them?
Yes, but carefully.
The platform should not say:
“Another user has almost the same idea as you.”
That would create privacy problems and could damage trust. It may also discourage users unnecessarily, even though many similar businesses can exist successfully at the same time.
Instead, the platform should give a general market reality warning:
“This idea may not be unique. Many successful businesses are built around similar ideas, but success usually depends on execution, positioning, customer access, trust, pricing, and speed of validation. Before investing serious time or money, you should test whether your specific target group wants your specific version of this solution.”
This warns the user without exposing another user’s information.
Should identical ideas be treated as a conflict?
Not automatically.
Two users can have the same broad idea and still create very different businesses. For example, two users may both want to create a gardening service, a tutoring platform, an AI assistant, or a local cleaning business. That does not mean one should be blocked or discouraged.
The platform should only treat it as a serious issue if there is a real conflict, such as:
                                                                                                                                       * one user is copying another user’s confidential material;
                                                                                                                                       * the idea is based on protected intellectual property;
                                                                                                                                       * the platform itself is facilitating unfair access to another user’s private work;
                                                                                                                                       * two users are being placed into the same closed opportunity where only one can reasonably proceed;
                                                                                                                                       * the platform is about to share sensitive insights from one user with another.
In normal cases, similarity should trigger differentiation guidance, not rejection.
What the platform should do
The platform should compare the users only internally and responsibly. It can use similarity detection to improve the quality of advice, but not to reveal other users.
A good response from the platform could be:
“Your idea falls into a category where similar concepts may already exist. That is not automatically a problem. The next step is to clarify what makes your version different: your target customer, your specific pain point, your delivery method, your pricing, your trust advantage, and your ability to reach customers.”
The platform can then ask questions such as:
“Who exactly is this for?”
“Why would they choose your version?”
“What proof do you have that they want this?”
“Can you test demand without spending much money?”
“What unfair advantage do you personally have?”
“What would make this hard for others to copy?”
Should the platform give both users the same score?
Not necessarily.
The idea score may be similar, but the user-readiness score may differ. The platform should separate these two things.
For example:
Assessment area
	User A
	User B
	Idea quality
	Similar
	Similar
	Market risk
	Similar
	Similar
	Financial risk
	Low
	High
	Execution ability
	Strong
	Weak
	Available time
	High
	Low
	Recommended next step
	Test with customers
	Stabilise first / prepare safely
	This distinction is important because a good idea can still be a bad next step for a particular person.
Core principle
The platform should not treat entrepreneurship as a race where only the first person with an idea deserves to continue. Similar ideas are normal. What matters is whether the user can responsibly test and execute the idea.
A strong principle for the platform would be:
“Similar ideas may receive similar market analysis, but users should receive personalised guidance based on their own risk, readiness, resources, and execution context.”
So the answer is:
Yes, the platform may treat them differently, but only for relevant and explainable reasons. It should warn them that similar ideas may exist and that differentiation matters, but it must not reveal or imply the existence of another specific user with the same idea.
	 
 
9.3
A user's answers are internally contradictory — for example, they say they have a very low risk tolerance but describe an extremely high-risk idea. What should the platform do?
When a user provides answers that appear internally contradictory, the platform should treat this as an important diagnostic signal rather than as a simple error. For example, a user may state that they have a very low risk tolerance, but then describe a business idea that is highly speculative, capital-intensive, legally uncertain, or dependent on unproven technology. In such cases, the platform should not simply accept the answers at face value and continue as if there is no issue.
The platform should first identify and clearly flag the contradiction in neutral language. It should explain to the user that their stated preference and their proposed idea appear to point in different directions. The purpose is not to judge the user, but to help them understand the implications of their own answers.
For example, the platform could say:
“You indicated that you have a low tolerance for risk. However, the idea you described appears to involve a high level of uncertainty, financial exposure, or execution risk. Before continuing, we need to clarify whether you want to adjust the idea, revise your risk profile, or proceed while accepting that the idea may not match your stated preferences.”
The platform should then guide the user through a clarification step. This may include asking the user to choose between several options:
                                                                                                                                       1. Adjust the idea so it better matches their stated low risk tolerance.
                                                                                                                                       2. Revise their risk tolerance if they realise they are actually willing to accept more uncertainty.
                                                                                                                                       3. Keep both answers unchanged, while acknowledging that there is a mismatch.
                                                                                                                                       4. Request a safer version of the idea, where the same concept is explored in a smaller, cheaper, legally safer, or more reversible way.
The platform should not automatically overwrite the user’s answers. However, it should also not ignore the contradiction. The safest approach is to preserve the original answers, record the contradiction, and ask the user to clarify their intention before giving strong recommendations.
If the contradiction remains unresolved, the platform should continue with caution. Any output should clearly mention the mismatch and avoid presenting the idea as fully aligned with the user’s stated preferences. For example, instead of saying:
“This idea is suitable for your profile,”
the platform should say:
“This idea may not fully match your stated low risk tolerance. The following analysis assumes that you are willing to explore a higher-risk path despite your earlier preference.”
The platform should also distinguish between different types of contradiction. Some contradictions are mild and can be handled through clarification. Others are serious enough to require stronger intervention. For example, contradictions involving legal compliance, financial exposure, safety, vulnerable users, or unrealistic expectations should trigger a more careful response. In those cases, the platform should recommend professional advice, additional due diligence, or a lower-risk alternative before the user proceeds.
The key principle is that contradiction should lead to reflection, clarification, and risk adjustment, not automatic rejection. Many early-stage entrepreneurs are still discovering what they actually want. A user may believe they want a safe path, while emotionally being attracted to a high-risk idea. The platform’s role is to make that tension visible and help the user make a more conscious decision.
A suitable rule for the platform would be:
If a user’s answers are internally contradictory, the platform must flag the inconsistency, explain the practical consequences, ask for clarification, and adjust the guidance accordingly. It should not silently proceed as if the contradiction does not exist.
	 
 
9.4
A user finishes all 7 phases. What happens next? Does the platform stay engaged? Is there a Phase 8?
When a user completes all 7 phases, the platform should not simply produce a final report and disengage. The first 7 phases are designed to clarify the user’s business idea, assess its realism, identify risks, understand the user’s personal context, and define a structured direction.
However, completing the first 7 phases does not mean the business is now operational. It means the foundation has been created.
After Phase 7, the platform should transition into Phase 8: Personalized Business Operations.
Phase 8 is the implementation and operations phase. Its purpose is to build the practical business components the user needs and to help run the business as much as possible through structured workflows, personalized tools, and permission-based automation.
In Phase 8, the platform should start functioning like a personalized, lightweight ERP system. It should help the user manage tasks, clients, leads, documents, invoices, follow-ups, communication, internal processes, deadlines, and recurring routines. Unlike a traditional ERP system, this operational layer should be built around the user’s specific business idea, personal constraints, skills, goals, budget, preferred working style, and risk tolerance.
Phase 8 may include the creation of business documents, client records, task boards, workflows, financial overviews, proposal templates, intake forms, standard operating procedures, email templates, dashboards, and automation rules. The platform should also help identify repetitive manual work and suggest ways to automate it.
Where the user gives permission, the platform may automate certain tasks. However, automation must be permission-based and traceable. The platform should not send emails, issue invoices, make legal commitments, spend money, submit forms, or take sensitive actions without the appropriate level of user consent. The user should be able to decide whether the platform may only suggest actions, prepare drafts, execute after approval, or automatically perform certain predefined low-risk workflows.
Phase 8 should therefore not be treated as an optional afterthought. It is the bridge between business planning and business execution. Without Phase 8, the platform may help the user create a good idea, but still leave them with the burden of organizing, administrating, following up, and executing everything manually.
The purpose of Phase 8 is to reduce that burden.
The platform should preserve the result of the first 7 phases as the strategic foundation, then use that foundation to build and operate the user’s personalized business system. Over time, Phase 8 should continue to adapt as the business changes, new information becomes available, and the user’s needs evolve.
In short:
The first 7 phases help define the business. Phase 8 helps build and run it.
	 
 
9.5
A user wants to completely start over with a new idea. What happens to their history?
When a user wants to completely start over with a new business idea, the platform should allow this without forcing the user to delete everything or lose their previous progress.
A new idea should be treated as a new business track, not as a full reset of the user’s entire account.
This means the platform should separate two types of history:
                                                                                                                                       1. User history
                                                                                                                                       2. Business idea history
These should not be treated as the same thing.
User History Should Be Preserved
The user’s personal history should remain available because it is still relevant, even if the business idea changes.
This includes information such as:
                                                                                                                                       * The user’s skills
                                                                                                                                       * Personality traits
                                                                                                                                       * Working style
                                                                                                                                       * Risk tolerance
                                                                                                                                       * Financial limits
                                                                                                                                       * Available time
                                                                                                                                       * Preferred communication style
                                                                                                                                       * Strengths and weaknesses
                                                                                                                                       * Personal goals
                                                                                                                                       * Constraints
                                                                                                                                       * Decision patterns
                                                                                                                                       * Previous lessons learned
This information belongs to the user, not to one specific business idea.
For example, if the user previously showed that they struggle with administration, dislike repetitive manual work, or prefer visual explanations, that knowledge remains useful for the next business idea. The platform should not force the user to repeat all of this unless the user wants to update it.
The platform should be able to say:
“You are starting with a new business idea. I will keep your personal profile and previous lessons, but the business-specific work from the old idea will be archived unless you choose to reuse parts of it.”
Business Idea History Should Be Archived, Not Deleted by Default
The old business idea should not simply disappear. It should be archived as a previous business track.
This archive may include:
                                                                                                                                       * The original idea
                                                                                                                                       * Phase answers
                                                                                                                                       * Reports
                                                                                                                                       * assumptions
                                                                                                                                       * risk assessments
                                                                                                                                       * business model work
                                                                                                                                       * generated documents
                                                                                                                                       * decisions
                                                                                                                                       * rejected options
                                                                                                                                       * financial estimates
                                                                                                                                       * validation results
                                                                                                                                       * automation rules
                                                                                                                                       * operational components from Phase 8
By archiving the old idea, the platform preserves traceability. This is important because the user may later want to understand why they abandoned an idea, reuse part of it, compare it with a new idea, or return to it.
The default behavior should therefore be:
Starting over creates a new business track while preserving the old business track in archive form.
The User Should Choose What Carries Over
When starting over, the platform should ask the user what should be reused and what should stay behind.
There should be three practical options:
Option 1: Clean Start
The user starts a completely new business idea without carrying over any business-specific content from the previous idea.
However, the platform still keeps the user profile unless the user explicitly chooses to reset that too.
This is best when the new idea is completely unrelated.
Example:
“I previously worked on a gardening service, but now I want to build a software product.”
Option 2: Reuse Useful Parts
The user starts a new idea but reuses selected parts of the previous work.
This could include documents, workflows, customer profiles, market research, pricing logic, automation ideas, or lessons learned.
This is best when the new idea is different, but still related in some way.
Example:
“The old idea was a local home service business. The new idea is a platform for managing home service providers.”
Option 3: Fork the Previous Idea
The user creates a new version of the old idea while keeping the original intact.
This is useful when the user is not fully abandoning the previous idea, but wants to explore a different direction.
Example:
“Instead of offering the service myself, what if I turn it into a marketplace?”
In this case, the platform should create a separate branch of the idea. The original remains preserved, and the new version can evolve independently.
The Platform Should Avoid Contaminating the New Idea
When a user starts over, the platform must be careful not to force the old idea into the new one.
The old idea may contain assumptions that are no longer valid. If those assumptions are automatically carried forward, the platform may make bad recommendations.
For example, the old idea may have assumed:
                                                                                                                                       * A local Dutch market
                                                                                                                                       * A low starting budget
                                                                                                                                       * A service-based business model
                                                                                                                                       * Direct client contact
                                                                                                                                       * No inventory
                                                                                                                                       * No employees
                                                                                                                                       * No technical product
If the new idea is an international software product, those assumptions may be wrong.
Therefore, business-specific assumptions should not carry over automatically. They should either be archived or explicitly selected by the user.
The platform should distinguish between:
“This is true about the user.”
and:
“This was only true for the previous business idea.”
This distinction is critical.
The User Should Be Able to Delete History, But Deletion Should Be Deliberate
Archiving should be the default, but deletion should still be possible.
The user should be able to delete:
                                                                                                                                       * A previous idea
                                                                                                                                       * A specific document
                                                                                                                                       * A specific phase answer
                                                                                                                                       * A generated file
                                                                                                                                       * A business track
                                                                                                                                       * Their entire account history
However, deletion should be deliberate because it may remove valuable context permanently.
For example, the platform could warn:
“Deleting this business track will permanently remove the phase answers, reports, decisions, documents, and operational setup connected to this idea. Your general user profile will remain unless you also choose to reset it.”
This protects the user from accidentally destroying useful information.
Phase 8 History Needs Special Treatment
Phase 8 may contain operational components, automations, client records, documents, invoices, task boards, workflows, and permission settings. This history is more sensitive than earlier planning phases.
When starting over, Phase 8 components should be reviewed carefully.
The platform should ask what should happen to:
                                                                                                                                       * Active automations
                                                                                                                                       * Client or lead records
                                                                                                                                       * Draft emails
                                                                                                                                       * Invoice templates
                                                                                                                                       * Recurring reminders
                                                                                                                                       * Task boards
                                                                                                                                       * External integrations
                                                                                                                                       * Permission settings
                                                                                                                                       * Business dashboards
                                                                                                                                       * Documents linked to the old idea
The platform should not automatically keep running automations from an abandoned business idea.
For example, if the user starts over, the platform should not continue sending follow-up emails, payment reminders, or task updates related to the old business unless the user explicitly keeps them active.
A safe rule would be:
When a business idea is archived, all Phase 8 automations linked only to that idea should be paused by default, unless the user chooses to keep them active.
The Platform Should Use Old History as Learning, Not as a Cage
The previous idea should not be treated as a failure. It should become useful learning material.
The platform can use the old history to help the user improve the new idea by identifying patterns such as:
                                                                                                                                       * Why the previous idea was abandoned
                                                                                                                                       * Which assumptions were wrong
                                                                                                                                       * Which risks appeared repeatedly
                                                                                                                                       * Which parts of the user found difficult
                                                                                                                                       * Which business models matched the user poorly
                                                                                                                                       * Which strengths were underused
                                                                                                                                       * Which tasks caused friction
                                                                                                                                       * Which parts of the process worked well
For example, the platform might say:
“In your previous idea, the biggest difficulty was the amount of manual follow-up required. For this new idea, I recommend designing automation and delegation earlier.”
This allows the platform to become smarter over time without trapping the user in their old plan.
	 
 
9.6
Should the platform ever refer a user to external professional help (financial advisor, legal counsel, therapist)? If yes, when and how?
Yes. The platform should refer users to external professional help when a matter requires certified, licensed, regulated, or specialist expertise. However, the preferred design should go beyond a simple referral. The platform should integrate with existing expert platforms and professional directories so that users can access qualified external support directly from within the workflow.
The platform should function as an agnostic expert-routing layer. This means it should not be tied to one provider, one professional network, one school of thought, one commercial partner, or one ideological approach. Instead, it should help the user find the most suitable external expert or platform based on the user’s actual need, location, budget, urgency, language, legal jurisdiction, and risk level.
Examples of relevant external platforms or directories could include:
                                                                                                                                       * BetterHelp or similar platforms for online mental health support. BetterHelp describes itself as an online therapy service that connects users with licensed mental health professionals through video, audio, live chat, and messaging. However, it should be treated with extra privacy due diligence because the FTC previously settled charges against BetterHelp concerning the sharing of sensitive health data for advertising purposes.
                                                                                                                                       * MicroMentor for business mentoring. MicroMentor presents itself as a global, free platform connecting entrepreneurs with volunteer business mentors.
                                                                                                                                       * Professional lawyer directories, such as the Dutch NOvA “Zoek een advocaat” search tool, which lists lawyers registered in the Netherlands and allows users to search by legal area, location, and subsidised legal aid availability.
                                                                                                                                       * Financial advisor directories, such as NAPFA for fee-only fiduciary financial planners in the United States, or the CFP Board’s “Find a CFP® Professional” tool. NAPFA states that its members work on a fee-only basis and do not receive commissions from selling financial products.
                                                                                                                                       * Accountant or tax advisor networks, such as NOAB in the Netherlands for administration and tax advisory offices, Register Belastingadviseurs for Dutch tax advisors, or CPAverify in the United States for checking licensed CPAs.
                                                                                                                                       * Debt counselling and financial help networks, such as NVVK in the Netherlands, whose members include municipalities, credit banks, and organisations involved in debt counselling, budget management, and financial assistance.
                                                                                                                                       * Healthcare credential checks, such as the Dutch BIG-register, where users can search for registered healthcare professionals by name, speciality, or BIG number.
These examples should be treated as possible integration categories, not as permanent endorsements. The platform should maintain a neutral and regularly updated catalogue of external options.
Core Principle: The Platform Does Not Replace Experts
The platform should not present itself as a lawyer, therapist, accountant, financial advisor, doctor, or debt counsellor. Its role is to:
                                                                                                                                       1. Detect when external expertise is needed.
                                                                                                                                       2. Help the user understand what type of expert is appropriate.
                                                                                                                                       3. Compare suitable external platforms or professionals.
                                                                                                                                       4. Help the user prepare the necessary information.
                                                                                                                                       5. With permission, assist the user in creating or managing the external account.
                                                                                                                                       6. Help the user follow up, track decisions, and understand next steps.
The external professional remains responsible for professional advice. The platform remains responsible for structure, preparation, coordination, traceability, and user support.
Account Handling on Behalf of the User
A valuable feature would be the ability for the platform to help the user operate accounts on external expert platforms. However, this must be handled with strict consent and control.
The platform may assist with:
                                                                                                                                       * Creating an account on an external expert platform.
                                                                                                                                       * Filling out intake forms.
                                                                                                                                       * Uploading documents.
                                                                                                                                       * Drafting a message to an advisor, mentor, lawyer, therapist, accountant, or debt counsellor.
                                                                                                                                       * Scheduling an appointment.
                                                                                                                                       * Summarising the user’s situation.
                                                                                                                                       * Preparing questions.
                                                                                                                                       * Tracking responses.
                                                                                                                                       * Reminding the user of follow-up actions.
                                                                                                                                       * Keeping a decision log.
However, the platform should not independently:
                                                                                                                                       * Sign contracts.
                                                                                                                                       * Accept legal terms on behalf of the user without explicit consent.
                                                                                                                                       * Submit legally binding declarations without approval.
                                                                                                                                       * Make payments without approval.
                                                                                                                                       * Choose a professional without showing the user the reasoning.
                                                                                                                                       * Give final legal, tax, medical, psychological, or investment advice.
                                                                                                                                       * Hide commercial relationships or rankings from the user.
The user should remain the account owner. The platform may act as a delegated assistant only within clearly defined permissions.
Permission Model
The platform should use a layered permission model.
Level 1: Research Only
The platform searches for relevant platforms or professionals and presents options.
Example:
“I found three suitable legal support options. Here are the differences, likely costs, and reasons each may or may not fit your case.”
Level 2: Prepare Only
The platform prepares documents, summaries, intake answers, and questions, but does not submit anything.
Example:
“I prepared your intake message for the debt counsellor. Please review it before I submit it.”
Level 3: Submit With Approval
The platform can submit forms or messages only after the user approves the final version.
Example:
“This is the final text that will be sent to the accountant. Please approve before submission.”
Level 4: Managed Assistance
The platform can handle routine interaction within strict limits, such as scheduling, uploading approved documents, or sending approved follow-up messages.
Example:
“I can upload the approved documents and send the prepared message to the selected advisor.”
Level 5: Restricted or Not Allowed
Certain actions should always require explicit human approval or may be completely blocked, such as signing contracts, accepting legal settlements, making payments, changing legal filings, or sending sensitive medical or legal information.
Agnostic Expert Matching
The platform should not simply push users toward one partner platform. It should match users to expert sources based on transparent criteria.
Possible matching criteria:
                                                                                                                                       * Country and jurisdiction.
                                                                                                                                       * Language.
                                                                                                                                       * Budget.
                                                                                                                                       * Urgency.
                                                                                                                                       * Certification or licensing status.
                                                                                                                                       * Type of problem.
                                                                                                                                       * Whether the user needs advice, representation, coaching, therapy, bookkeeping, tax filing, or debt restructuring.
                                                                                                                                       * Whether the issue is personal or business-related.
                                                                                                                                       * Whether public, nonprofit, commercial, or professional-body options are available.
                                                                                                                                       * Whether the provider is independent, commission-based, fee-only, volunteer-based, or commercially incentivised.
                                                                                                                                       * Privacy and data protection standards.
                                                                                                                                       * User reviews, where reliable.
                                                                                                                                       * Conflict-of-interest risks.
                                                                                                                                       * Availability and response time.
The platform should clearly explain why it recommends a particular option.
Example:
“This is a legal dispute in the Netherlands. A lawyer search through the Dutch Bar’s official directory is more appropriate than a generic legal marketplace because the title ‘advocaat’ is protected and the directory focuses on registered lawyers.”
Professional Categories the Platform Should Support
The platform should eventually support expert routing for at least these categories:
1. Mental Health and Wellbeing
For stress, burnout, anxiety, depression, trauma, crisis situations, emotional instability, or personal problems affecting business functioning.
Possible routes:
                                                                                                                                       * Online therapy platforms.
                                                                                                                                       * Local therapists.
                                                                                                                                       * Crisis services.
                                                                                                                                       * Doctors or general practitioners.
                                                                                                                                       * Licensed psychologists or psychiatrists.
                                                                                                                                       * Professional registers or credential checks.
For mental health, the platform must be extra careful with privacy, urgency, and crisis detection.
2. Legal Support
For disputes, contracts, government conflict, permits, liability, intellectual property, employment matters, immigration, housing, insurance disputes, or court deadlines.
Possible routes:
                                                                                                                                       * Official lawyer directories.
                                                                                                                                       * Legal aid organisations.
                                                                                                                                       * Public legal information services.
                                                                                                                                       * Specialist law firms.
                                                                                                                                       * Mediators.
                                                                                                                                       * Consumer dispute bodies.
The platform should help prepare a legal brief, timeline, evidence bundle, and questions for the lawyer.
3. Financial Advice
For investment decisions, major debt, financial planning, pension choices, insurance questions, business financing, risk exposure, or high-value decisions.
Possible routes:
                                                                                                                                       * Fee-only financial planners.
                                                                                                                                       * Certified financial planners.
                                                                                                                                       * Debt counsellors.
                                                                                                                                       * Insurance advisors.
                                                                                                                                       * Public financial help services.
The platform should clearly distinguish between general financial education and regulated financial advice.
4. Accounting and Tax
For bookkeeping, VAT, payroll, income tax, corporate tax, cross-border income, tax filings, legal structure, deductions, and audit risk.
Possible routes:
                                                                                                                                       * Accountant directories.
                                                                                                                                       * Tax advisor registers.
                                                                                                                                       * Bookkeeping platforms.
                                                                                                                                       * Administration offices.
                                                                                                                                       * Certified public accountant verification tools.
The platform can prepare the financial records, but the professional should validate tax positions.
5. Business Mentoring
For strategy, marketing, operations, entrepreneurship, leadership, pricing, customer acquisition, scaling, or personal business development.
Possible routes:
                                                                                                                                       * MicroMentor.
                                                                                                                                       * Startup mentor networks.
                                                                                                                                       * Chamber of commerce support.
                                                                                                                                       * Accelerator mentors.
                                                                                                                                       * Industry-specific expert communities.
This category is lower risk than legal, tax, medical, or financial advice, but the platform should still distinguish between informal mentoring and regulated professional advice.
External Expert Workflow
A good workflow could look like this:
                                                                                                                                       1. Risk detection
 The platform detects that the user’s issue requires external expertise.
                                                                                                                                       2. Expert category selection
 The platform classifies the need: legal, financial, tax, therapy, mentoring, debt counselling, etc.
                                                                                                                                       3. Jurisdiction and context check
 The platform identifies country, language, urgency, budget, and whether the issue is personal or business-related.
                                                                                                                                       4. Provider comparison
 The platform presents several suitable platforms, directories, or professionals.
                                                                                                                                       5. User chooses a route
 The user selects the preferred provider or asks the platform to recommend a best option.
                                                                                                                                       6. Account creation or connection
 The platform helps the user create an account or connect an existing account.
                                                                                                                                       7. Brief preparation
 The platform prepares a clean summary, timeline, documents, questions, and desired outcome.
                                                                                                                                       8. Controlled submission
 The platform submits only approved information.
                                                                                                                                       9. Follow-up management
 The platform tracks messages, appointments, documents, deadlines, and advice received.
                                                                                                                                       10. Decision logging
 The platform records what was advised, what the user decided, and what the next action is.
Professional Brief Generator
This should become a core feature.
When external expertise is needed, the platform should generate a professional brief containing:
                                                                                                                                          * User identity and contact details.
                                                                                                                                          * Short description of the issue.
                                                                                                                                          * Relevant timeline.
                                                                                                                                          * Key facts.
                                                                                                                                          * Documents available.
                                                                                                                                          * Questions to be answered.
                                                                                                                                          * Desired outcome.
                                                                                                                                          * Deadlines.
                                                                                                                                          * Risk level.
                                                                                                                                          * Previous steps already taken.
                                                                                                                                          * What the platform has already helped prepare.
                                                                                                                                          * What the professional is being asked to decide or review.
This makes the external expert’s work faster, cheaper, and more accurate.
Example:
“I recommend involving a Dutch lawyer. I can prepare a one-page legal brief with the facts, timeline, evidence list, and exact questions, so the lawyer does not need to reconstruct the situation from scattered documents.”
Data Protection and Confidentiality
Because the platform would handle sensitive information, especially with legal, financial, and mental health matters, privacy must be built into the design.
The platform should include:
                                                                                                                                          * Explicit consent before sharing data externally.
                                                                                                                                          * Clear visibility into what will be shared.
                                                                                                                                          * Data minimisation: only share what is necessary.
                                                                                                                                          * Separate handling for mental health, legal, financial, and identity documents.
                                                                                                                                          * User-controlled revocation of access.
                                                                                                                                          * Audit logs.
                                                                                                                                          * Encryption.
                                                                                                                                          * No hidden advertising-based data sharing.
                                                                                                                                          * No selling of sensitive user data.
