                                                                                                                                          * Clear disclosure of affiliate relationships, if any.
                                                                                                                                          * Option to use official public directories instead of commercial marketplaces.
This is especially important for sensitive categories such as therapy, legal disputes, debt, taxes, and medical care.
Neutrality and Anti-Bias Rules
Because the platform values agnostic expert access, it should avoid becoming a biased recommendation engine.
The platform should not prefer experts because of:
                                                                                                                                          * Affiliate commissions.
                                                                                                                                          * Ownership relationships.
                                                                                                                                          * Political ideology.
                                                                                                                                          * Religious affiliation.
                                                                                                                                          * One therapeutic school.
                                                                                                                                          * One financial philosophy.
                                                                                                                                          * One legal strategy.
                                                                                                                                          * One country-specific provider when better local alternatives exist.
When a provider is promoted, sponsored, partnered, or commission-based, the platform should clearly disclose that.
The ideal rule is:
The user’s interest comes first. Commercial relationships must never silently determine the expert recommendation.


	 
SECTION 10   REFERENCE POINTS — WHAT HAS WORKED AND WHAT HASN'T
 
Learning from what already exists in the world.
10.1
Name 2–3 products, experiences, books, or processes that gave you the feeling you want users to have here. What specifically worked?  Does not have to be software.
I have no answer to this.
	 
 
10.2
Name 2–3 products or experiences this platform should be the OPPOSITE of. What specifically was wrong or harmful about them?
Robert, a strong answer could be:
________________


Name 2–3 products or experiences this platform should be the opposite of. What specifically was wrong or harmful about them?
1. Traditional business planning tools
Changepreneurship should be the opposite of traditional business planning tools.
The problem with most business planning tools is that they usually solve only one isolated part of the business-building process. One tool helps with a business plan. Another helps with financial projections. Another helps with customer research. Another helps with task management. Another helps with outreach. Another helps with branding. Each tool may be good at its own narrow function, but the total experience becomes fragmented.
The user is forced to constantly switch between tools, formats, browser tabs, documents, platforms, templates, and mental models. That context switching is exhausting and harmful because the user loses momentum. Instead of being guided through the full journey, the user becomes the project manager of the tools themselves.
Many of these tools also assume that the user already knows what they are doing. They are useful when the user knows exactly what section they need, what question they need answered, what terminology to use, and what the next step should be. But that is often not the reality for early-stage entrepreneurs, activists, project starters, or people trying to turn an idea into something real.
It is similar to Google: Google is powerful when you know what you are looking for. But when you do not know the name of the thing, the right category, the right search term, or the right path forward, the system fails you. Traditional business planning tools often have the same weakness. They help you execute a known task, but they do not help enough when the user is confused, uncertain, overwhelmed, or missing context.
Changepreneurship should not be another isolated planning tool. It should be an integrated thinking-and-doing system that helps the user understand the situation, identify what is missing, make decisions, and move the project forward.
________________


2. The current internet-based business development experience
Changepreneurship should also be the opposite of the current “figure it out yourself on the internet” experience.
At the moment, building a business or project often means being pushed into the wider internet ecosystem, with all of its flaws. The user has to search endlessly, compare conflicting advice, register for different platforms, send many messages, chase people, deal with paywalls, and manually judge whether a source is reliable.
A major harmful part of this experience is wasted outreach. Users are often encouraged to contact many people, potential partners, advisors, investors, suppliers, mentors, or customers. But in practice, many people never reply. The user spends time writing messages, filling out forms, joining platforms, and chasing responses, only to receive silence. That creates frustration, loss of motivation, and wasted energy.
Paywalls are another problem. A user may find what appears to be useful information, only to discover that the important part is locked behind a subscription, consultation fee, course, premium community, or hidden sales funnel. This creates a situation where the internet looks abundant, but the useful path is fragmented, expensive, and unclear.
The harm is not only inconvenience. It creates the illusion that progress is being made while the user is actually stuck in research, outreach, and administration. The user becomes busy, but the project does not truly move forward.
Changepreneurship should be different. It should reduce unnecessary searching, reduce dead-end outreach, help the user identify the right next step, and where possible, help perform the work instead of only suggesting that the user go somewhere else.
________________


3. Business development books, courses, and passive learning material
Changepreneurship should also be the opposite of business books, courses, and other passive business education products.
Business development books can be informative. They can explain concepts, frameworks, strategies, and inspiring examples. However, the problem is that reading about business development does not automatically make business development happen.
Books and courses often leave the user with more knowledge, but not necessarily more execution. The user still has to translate the theory into their own situation. They still have to decide what applies, what does not apply, what to do first, who to contact, what to write, what to build, how to test it, and how to follow up. That translation from theory to action is where many people get stuck.
The harmful part is that these products can make the user feel temporarily inspired while still leaving them alone with the real work. The user may understand the idea intellectually, but the system does not help enough with implementation, prioritisation, accountability, evidence gathering, outreach, iteration, or decision-making.
Changepreneurship should not be a passive library of advice. It should be an active partner in execution. It should help the user think, but also help the user do.
The difference should be:
Most business tools, books, and platforms give the user information or isolated functionality. Changepreneurship should help the user move from confusion to clarity, and from clarity to action.
In other words, Changepreneurship should not merely explain entrepreneurship. It should help operationalise it.
	 
 
10.3
Is there a specific methodology, framework, or school of thought this platform should be grounded in?  E.g. lean startup, design thinking, a specific psychological framework, a known business canvas, etc.
10.3 Is there a specific methodology, framework, or school of thought this platform should be grounded in?
Yes. Changepreneurship should be strongly grounded in Maslow’s hierarchy of needs, especially in the early phases of the platform.
I personally see entrepreneurship as something that is only truly feasible when the first layers of a person’s life are sufficiently stable. In Maslow’s model, the lower layers are about basic survival, safety, and belonging. These are not abstract psychological ideas; they directly affect whether someone has the emotional, financial, social, and practical capacity to build something new.
For Changepreneurship, this matters because entrepreneurship requires more than a good idea. It requires energy, resilience, mental space, risk tolerance, and the ability to recover when things do not go as expected. If someone is still fighting for basic stability, then asking them to “be entrepreneurial” can easily become unrealistic or even harmful.
The first layers of Maslow are largely about survival. They are about having enough security to function. They are also more about taking what is needed to stabilise oneself than about giving, creating, leading, or taking risks for others. A person who is struggling with housing, income, debt, health, safety, bureaucracy, or social instability may not yet be in a strong position to build a business, lead a movement, or absorb failure.
That does not mean such a person has no potential. It means the platform should first help them understand where they currently stand and what kind of stability must be improved before larger entrepreneurial action becomes realistic.
For that reason, Phase 1 / Pillar 1 of Changepreneurship should focus heavily on personal stability. Before pushing the user into business planning, branding, outreach, funding, or execution, the platform should help investigate the user’s actual life situation. It should ask: Where is this person on the pyramid? Which needs are sufficiently met? Which needs are fragile? Which unresolved issues may later sabotage the project? What support, structure, income, documents, relationships, habits, or systems would make this person stronger before they start?
The goal is not to judge the user. The goal is to prevent false starts.
Any stability the user can create before starting the deeper Changepreneurship process is a win later in the pipeline. More stability means better decisions, more patience, more resilience, better communication, and a stronger ability to handle setbacks. A person who is less trapped in survival mode is more capable of long-term thinking, collaboration, creativity, and contribution.
So Changepreneurship should not treat entrepreneurship as merely a business process. It should treat entrepreneurship as a human development process.
The platform should be grounded in the idea that before someone can successfully change the world, build a company, or create value for others, they often need to become stable enough to carry that responsibility. In that sense, Changepreneurship starts before the business plan. It starts with the person.
A suitable guiding principle could be:
Changepreneurship begins by strengthening the person behind the project. The more stable the person is, the more realistic, resilient, and valuable their entrepreneurial action becomes.
This makes Maslow’s hierarchy especially useful as a foundation for the platform. It gives Changepreneurship a structured way to assess whether a user is ready to move from survival, to stability, to contribution, and eventually to meaningful change-making.
	 
SECTION 11   MENTORSHIP & NETWORK
 
Mentor access has been identified as a critical gate. This section defines how it fits into the platform.
11.1
Does the platform have its own mentor network, or does it point users to external sources?
Changepreneurship does not need to own the entire mentor network. Instead, it connects to accepted external platforms through the user’s own accounts and uses those accounts on the user’s behalf. For example, if a user needs a mentor, the platform may connect to MicroMentor or similar approved platforms, search for suitable mentors, prepare outreach, and initiate contact through the user’s account. The core value is not referral, but execution: Changepreneurship does the work of finding and approaching the right people while keeping the process inside its guided venture-building flow.
	 
 
11.2
At what exact point in the journey does the platform tell a user they need a mentor — and what triggers that?
The platform tells a user they need a mentor at the moment the mentor gap becomes the next blocking condition in their journey. This is not a fixed screen that appears for every user, and it is not simply a generic recommendation at the end of an assessment. It happens when the AI co-founder determines that continuing with more questions, planning, or business-building would be less useful than first bringing in a suitable external human mentor.
The first possible moment this can happen is during the early onboarding and assessment stage, after the user has connected their accounts and completed the introductory conversation. At that point, the platform has enough information from the user’s own answers, connected accounts, professional history, social/professional network, and venture idea to judge whether the user has adequate guidance around them. The platform already says users connect accounts early so the system can analyze their background before asking too many manual questions, and it explicitly says the journey should not always continue to the next question when another action is more important.
The primary trigger is simple:
The user either directly indicates that they do not have a mentor, or the platform infers from the available evidence that they lack a suitable mentor for the specific type of venture they are trying to build.
However, the trigger should be broader than only asking, “Do you have a mentor?” A user may believe they have support, while in reality that person is not suitable for the specific problem. Therefore, the platform should evaluate whether the user has access to someone who can provide relevant guidance for their current bottleneck.
For example, the mentor requirement may be triggered when:
                                                                                                                                          1. The user says they have no mentor, advisor, experienced founder, or domain expert helping them.
                                                                                                                                          2. The connected account analysis shows no credible mentor-like relationship in their professional or entrepreneurial network.
                                                                                                                                          3. The user has a promising idea but lacks experience in the target industry, meaning they are at high risk of making avoidable beginner mistakes.
                                                                                                                                          4. The user is entering a complex or regulated field, where advice from a domain expert is necessary before moving forward.
                                                                                                                                          5. The user is technically strong but commercially weak, such as a deep-tech founder, academic, engineer, or inventor who needs business translation, customer validation, or commercialization guidance.
                                                                                                                                          6. The user is personally motivated but strategically isolated, meaning they have passion and potential, but no experienced person around them who can challenge assumptions.
                                                                                                                                          7. The next phase requires real-world validation or serious decisions, and the platform concludes that the user should not continue alone before speaking with a suitable mentor.
The exact point in the journey is therefore:
Immediately before the platform would otherwise route the user into the next major phase, question, or business-building action, if the AI determines that the lack of a suitable mentor is now the highest-priority constraint.
In practice, this can happen in several places. In Phase 1: Self-Discovery, it may happen if the user’s personal environment, confidence, resilience, or support network is too weak. In Phase 2: Idea Discovery, it may happen if the user has a promising but unclear idea and needs experienced guidance to shape it. In Phase 3: Market Research, it may happen if the user is making claims that require domain expertise. In Phase 5: Product Concept Testing, it may happen before the user starts speaking to potential customers if they need help framing the test properly. The platform document already supports this logic by saying that “find a mentor before proceeding” is an included action type, triggered when the user has no mentor, after which the AI searches for a suitable mentor and reaches out on the user’s behalf.
The user should not merely see a warning. They should see a clear intervention:
“Before we continue, I recommend finding a suitable mentor. Your idea has potential, but based on your current profile and network, you do not yet appear to have someone experienced enough to challenge your assumptions and guide the next step. Continuing without that support would increase the risk of wasted time, wrong decisions, or avoidable failure. I will now help you find suitable mentors through approved external platforms connected to your account, such as MicroMentor, and prepare outreach on your behalf.”
This means the mentor trigger is not a passive advice moment. It is an execution moment. The platform should move from assessment mode into action mode: identify the type of mentor needed, search approved external platforms through the user’s connected accounts, prepare outreach, and, where permitted, contact suitable mentors on the user’s behalf.
	 
 
11.3
What kinds of mentors does the platform help users find?  Consider: domain mentor, founder mentor, functional mentor (marketing/finance/ops), investor mentor. Which are relevant?
The platform should not simply say, “You need a mentor,” and then leave the user to figure it out. Once the platform has identified that mentorship is needed, it must also determine what type of mentor is needed, why that mentor is needed, and what action should follow.
The mentor recommendation should be based on the specific gap that is blocking the user. A mentor is not a generic “business buddy.” The platform should match the user to the kind of human guidance that fits the user’s actual situation.
For example, the user may need:
                                                                                                                                          1. A founder mentor if they lack general entrepreneurial experience and need help thinking like someone who owns the outcome.
                                                                                                                                          2. A domain mentor if the idea belongs to an industry the user does not understand well enough.
                                                                                                                                          3. A commercialization mentor if the user is technical, academic, creative, or mission-driven but struggles to translate the idea into a viable product, service, or business model.
                                                                                                                                          4. A validation mentor if the user is avoiding customer conversations, market testing, or real-world feedback.
                                                                                                                                          5. A financial mentor if the user has weak pricing, runway, cost, or revenue assumptions.
                                                                                                                                          6. An operational mentor if the user is moving from idea into execution and needs help structuring processes, roles, tools, and workflows.
                                                                                                                                          7. A strategic mentor if the user has too many possible directions and needs help choosing the right sequence.
                                                                                                                                          8. A confidence and accountability mentor if the user is capable but isolated, hesitant, inconsistent, or overwhelmed.
The platform should determine this through a combination of the user’s onboarding answers, venture stage, assessment scores, connected account analysis, missing skills, weak assumptions, and current blockers. This fits the product principle that Changepreneurship must be a structured journey with memory, routing, scoring, evidence tracking, and clear next actions, not a generic AI chat tool.
The platform should then create a mentor requirement profile. This is essentially a short internal brief that explains what kind of person the user needs.
That profile should include:
                                                                                                                                          * the user’s venture stage;
                                                                                                                                          * the current blocker;
                                                                                                                                          * the missing expertise;
                                                                                                                                          * the preferred mentor background;
                                                                                                                                          * the relevant industry or domain;
                                                                                                                                          * the user’s language and country preferences;
                                                                                                                                          * whether the mentor should be short-term or long-term;
                                                                                                                                          * what the first conversation should achieve;
                                                                                                                                          * what information can be shared with the mentor;
                                                                                                                                          * what the user must approve before outreach happens.
For example:
“This user needs a mentor with early-stage SaaS and customer validation experience. The user has a promising platform idea but is still making assumptions about the target market without enough direct customer evidence. The mentor should help the user design the first validation conversations, challenge the business model assumptions, and decide whether the idea is ready to move into product concept testing.”
After the mentor requirement profile is created, the platform should move into mentor acquisition mode. That means it helps the user find and approach suitable mentors through approved external channels, such as MicroMentor, LinkedIn, incubator networks, university entrepreneurship programs, local business organizations, or sector-specific communities. The platform may support mentor matching, but it must remain within the product boundary: it may identify the type of mentor needed, but it should not become a public mentor marketplace or social network.
The platform should present the user with a small number of suitable mentor directions rather than overwhelming them with hundreds of profiles. A good structure would be:
“I recommend looking for one of the following mentor types:
                                                                                                                                          1. Early-stage SaaS mentor
                                                                                                                                          2. Customer validation mentor
                                                                                                                                          3. Legal-tech domain mentor
The highest priority is the customer validation mentor, because your next risky step is market testing.”
The user should then be asked to approve the outreach direction before messages are sent. This is important because the platform should not remove the founder from the process. The founder must still approve decisions, handle real relationships, speak with people, and accept responsibility.
The outreach itself should be practical and specific. The platform should not send a vague message like:
“Can you mentor me?”
Instead, it should prepare a focused request, such as:
“I am building an early-stage venture in [field]. I am currently trying to validate whether [target group] has a serious enough problem around [problem]. I am looking for a mentor who can help me design the first validation conversations and challenge my assumptions before I build too much. Would you be open to a 30-minute introductory conversation?”
This keeps the mentor request respectful, concrete, and more likely to receive a response.
The platform should also track the mentor-search process as part of the user’s venture journey. Mentor outreach should count as a meaningful venture-building action, because the existing platform definition already includes outreach, mentor, customer, or stakeholder tasks as meaningful activity.
The platform should track:
                                                                                                                                          * which mentors were suggested;
                                                                                                                                          * which ones the user approved;
                                                                                                                                          * which messages were sent;
                                                                                                                                          * who responded;
                                                                                                                                          * whether a meeting was scheduled;
                                                                                                                                          * what advice was received;
                                                                                                                                          * whether the advice changed the venture plan;
                                                                                                                                          * whether the mentor relationship is active, inactive, or unsuitable.
This matters because a mentor is not valuable merely because they exist. The mentor must produce useful guidance that affects the next decision.
The platform should also protect the user from poor mentor matching. A mentor should not be treated as suitable just because they are experienced. The mentor must match the actual bottleneck. A retired corporate executive may be impressive but not useful for a first-time founder validating a small local service. A technical expert may be brilliant but not useful for pricing or customer discovery. A motivational coach may be encouraging but not useful for market validation. The platform should therefore evaluate mentors against the specific decision the user is facing.
	 
 
11.4
Does the platform facilitate the mentor relationship — or simply recommend and step aside?
Robert, below is one consolidated and document-ready version for 11.4, combining the original mentor concept with the newer AI-first mentorship model.
________________


11.4 Mentorship, AI Support, and Human Validation
Changepreneurship should provide users with structured support throughout the development of their ideas, projects, ventures, or social initiatives. This support should not rely on one single form of guidance. Instead, the platform should combine AI support, human mentorship, peer feedback, expert input, and network access in a layered system.
The role of mentorship within Changepreneurship should be understood differently from traditional entrepreneurship programmes. In many older models, the mentor is treated as the first major source of guidance. The user comes with an idea, explains it to a mentor, and the mentor helps shape the next steps. However, this model is becoming outdated because AI is now capable of providing immediate, structured, and reasonably high-quality support for many of the tasks that mentors used to perform.
Many users will already have used AI before they request a human mentor. They may have asked AI to evaluate their idea, explain possible business models, identify risks, draft outreach messages, create validation plans, compare target groups, prepare pitch material, or suggest next actions. Therefore, Changepreneurship should not assume that the human mentor is the user’s first source of guidance.
Instead, the platform should treat AI as the likely first layer of mentorship and the human mentor as a second layer of validation, correction, lived experience, credibility, and network access.
This means the platform should ask early in the support process whether the user has already used AI. If so, the platform should collect a short overview of what the user asked AI, what advice was received, what documents or plans were created, what actions were taken, and what questions remain unresolved. The user should be able to paste AI conversations, upload AI-generated plans, or answer a short questionnaire about how AI has already helped.
The platform should then perform a mentor gap analysis. This analysis should identify what AI has already covered and what still requires human input. The goal is to avoid wasting human mentor time on generic advice that AI can already provide. Instead, the mentor conversation should focus on the areas where human judgment is genuinely valuable.
For example, AI may help a user define a target audience, draft a validation plan, create a simple business model, or prepare a list of assumptions. However, a human mentor may still be needed to judge whether those assumptions make sense in the real world, whether the user is interpreting the advice correctly, whether there are important blind spots, or whether the user needs introductions to people who can help test the idea.
In this model, the human mentor does not replace AI. The human mentor improves, validates, challenges, or contextualizes what the user and AI have already produced.
Human mentors should mainly be used for areas where AI has natural limitations. These include:
                                                                                                                                          * validating whether AI-generated advice makes sense in practice;
                                                                                                                                          * identifying blind spots the user or AI may have missed;
                                                                                                                                          * judging whether the user is interpreting the advice correctly;
                                                                                                                                          * providing lived experience from a specific industry, profession, community, or local market;
                                                                                                                                          * warning against mistakes that are only obvious through experience;
                                                                                                                                          * offering introductions to customers, partners, funders, suppliers, lawyers, experts, or institutions;
                                                                                                                                          * assessing the founder’s personal readiness;
                                                                                                                                          * challenging the user when they are hiding behind planning instead of taking action;
                                                                                                                                          * providing credibility, confidence, or reassurance before an important decision;
                                                                                                                                          * helping the user understand informal norms, politics, incentives, and human behaviour in a specific field.
This makes mentorship more efficient and more valuable. The human mentor receives a clearer briefing and does not need to repeat basic explanations. The user also receives better support because the conversation starts at a more advanced level.
A useful mentor briefing could look like this:
The user has already used AI to define the target audience, draft a validation plan, and identify possible pricing models. The remaining gaps are market reality, lawyer willingness to participate, legal-sector credibility, and introductions to relevant legal professionals. A human mentor should therefore not focus on basic business model explanation, but on validating the legal-market assumptions and helping the user access real-world contacts.
This approach also reduces the pressure on the platform to provide unlimited human support. Human mentors are valuable but limited. Their time should be protected. AI can handle many first-line questions, while human mentors can focus on the questions that truly require judgment, experience, or relationships.
At the same time, the platform should not force every user into an AI-first process. Some users may distrust AI, dislike using it, lack confidence with digital tools, or simply prefer speaking to a human being. Changepreneurship should therefore support different mentorship preferences.
The platform can distinguish between several user types:
                                                                                                                                          1. AI-first users
These users prefer to explore their idea with AI first and only involve humans for validation, specialist input, accountability, or network access.
                                                                                                                                          2. Human-first users
These users prefer a human mentor from the beginning because they value trust, emotional support, conversation, or personal guidance.
                                                                                                                                          3. Hybrid users
These users use both AI and human mentors throughout the process.
                                                                                                                                          4. AI-resistant users
These users may not want to use AI at all and should still be able to receive meaningful support through human guidance or peer structures.
However, the most common future pathway is likely to be AI-first. Users will often arrive with partially developed ideas, AI-generated documents, draft plans, and assumptions that already sound convincing. This creates a new risk: users may overtrust AI output before it has been tested in reality.
Changepreneurship should therefore help users distinguish between:
                                                                                                                                             * advice that sounds logical;
                                                                                                                                             * advice that has actually been validated;
                                                                                                                                             * advice that requires expert review;
                                                                                                                                             * advice that depends on local context;
                                                                                                                                             * advice that could create legal, financial, reputational, or operational risk;
                                                                                                                                             * advice that still needs customer, community, institutional, or market confirmation.
The platform should make clear that AI can support thinking, but it cannot replace real-world validation. AI can help the user prepare, structure, and reflect, but the user remains responsible for testing assumptions, making decisions, and acting in reality.
A strong platform response could be:
You have already used AI to develop your concept, target group, and first validation plan. The remaining uncertainty is not whether the plan is logically structured, but whether the market will respond as expected. A human mentor should now be used to validate the assumptions, identify missing risks, and help you access people who can provide real-world feedback.
This creates a modern and efficient support model. Changepreneurship does not ignore AI, but it also does not treat AI as a full replacement for human mentorship. Instead, the platform coordinates both.
AI provides scalable, immediate, low-cost, and structured support. Human mentors provide judgment, context, trust, lived experience, credibility, accountability, and access to real-world networks. The strength of Changepreneurship should be in combining these layers intelligently.
The platform should therefore act as a bridge between AI-generated thinking and human reality-testing. It should help users prepare better before meeting a mentor, help mentors focus on the highest-value issues, and help both sides avoid wasting time on generic advice.
In practical terms, Changepreneurship should include:
                                                                                                                                             * an intake question asking whether the user has already used AI;
                                                                                                                                             * an option to upload or summarize AI-generated advice;
                                                                                                                                             * a mentor gap analysis identifying unresolved questions;
                                                                                                                                             * a focused mentor brief before each human mentorship session;
                                                                                                                                             * a distinction between AI-answerable questions and human-judgment questions;
                                                                                                                                             * support pathways for AI-first, human-first, hybrid, and AI-resistant users;
                                                                                                                                             * safeguards against overtrusting AI output;
                                                                                                                                             * a clear emphasis on real-world testing and founder responsibility.
This positions Changepreneurship as a platform that understands the future of mentorship. The goal is not simply to connect users with mentors. The goal is to help users move from idea to action by combining the speed and structure of AI with the judgment, credibility, and networks of human beings.
	 
SECTION 12   DATA, PRIVACY & ETHICS
 
These decisions affect what is legally allowed and what users will actually trust.
12.1
What user data is the platform allowed to use to improve itself over time?
12.1 Data Use, Platform Learning, and Continuous Improvement
Yes. The platform should be allowed to use all available and relevant user data to improve itself over time, on the condition that the use of this data is clearly connected to helping users move forward and helping the company improve its ability to serve them.
In my vision, user data should not be treated as passive information that is simply stored. It should function as the learning layer of the platform. The platform should learn from the user’s goals, actions, decisions, obstacles, progress, outcomes, preferences, connected accounts, mentor interactions, AI conversations, documents, and other relevant activity. The purpose of this learning is to make the platform more accurate, more personal, more useful, and more effective over time.
The guiding principle should be:
The platform may use user data when doing so helps the user progress, improves the quality of the platform, strengthens the company’s ability to serve users, or increases the chance that users achieve meaningful outcomes.
This means the platform should continuously learn from both individual users and wider user patterns. On an individual level, it should understand what a specific user is trying to achieve, where that user gets stuck, what type of support is most effective, and what the next logical step should be. On a collective level, it should learn which parts of the journey work well, where users commonly fail, which types of guidance are effective, which mentor interactions create results, and which patterns lead to successful outcomes.
For example, if a user keeps planning but avoids taking external action, the platform should be able to recognise this pattern and respond accordingly. If a user repeatedly struggles with outreach, the platform should help improve that process. If a user reaches a stage where mentor support, legal expertise, funding, validation, or external accountability is needed, the platform should be able to identify that based on the available data.
The purpose of using data is not surveillance. The purpose is progress.
The platform should use data to create a feedback loop:
User activity creates data.
Data creates insight.
Insight improves guidance.
Better guidance improves user outcomes.
Better outcomes improve the platform.
This feedback loop should become one of the core strengths of the platform. Every user journey should help the platform become better, provided that this learning happens responsibly.
At the same time, this does not mean that data should be used without limits. The platform must use data in a way that is legal, ethical, transparent, secure, and proportionate. Users should understand that the platform learns from their activity in order to support them better. They should also be protected against misuse of their information.
The platform should therefore follow a clear ethical boundary:
We use data to move people forward, not to manipulate them, exploit them, or extract value from them without benefit to them.
This is especially important because users may share sensitive information, including personal struggles, financial problems, business ideas, legal issues, family circumstances, professional setbacks, or other vulnerable information. That kind of data can be extremely useful for helping the user, but it must be handled with care.
The platform should therefore apply appropriate safeguards, including clear user permission where required, transparent explanations of data use, strong security, internal access controls, data minimisation where possible, anonymisation or aggregation where individual identity is not needed, and user rights regarding access, correction, deletion, and control over their data.
The company should not use user data simply because it is available. It should use user data because there is a clear and legitimate reason connected to the user’s progress, the platform’s improvement, or the company’s mission.
In practical terms, the platform should use data to answer questions such as:
                                                                                                                                             * What does this user need next?
                                                                                                                                             * Where is this user stuck?
                                                                                                                                             * Is the user making real progress or only preparing?
                                                                                                                                             * What support has worked for similar users?
                                                                                                                                             * Which mentor, expert, platform, or external contact may help?
                                                                                                                                             * What risks or blockers are emerging?
                                                                                                                                             * What advice has already been given?
                                                                                                                                             * What still needs validation?
                                                                                                                                             * What stage is the user actually in?
                                                                                                                                             * What intervention is most likely to move the user forward?
This approach allows the platform to become increasingly intelligent over time. It also creates a long-term advantage for the company, because the more the platform learns from real user journeys, the better it can support future users.
Therefore, the answer is yes: the platform should be allowed to use all available and relevant user data to improve itself over time, as long as this is done responsibly and with the clear purpose of helping users and the company move forward.
Data should be the learning engine of the platform, but the moral condition is that this learning must serve the user’s development, the platform’s mission, and the company’s long-term ability to create meaningful progress.
	 
 
12.2
The platform benchmarks users against each other. What steps are required to ensure no individual user can ever be identified from those benchmarks?
(Past chat)(Past chat)(Past chat)## 12.2 User Consent, Control, and Responsible Data Boundaries
The platform should be built around a clear principle: user data may be used to help the user and improve the platform, but the user must remain protected, informed, and in control.
Because the platform may work with sensitive personal information, business ideas, legal issues, financial circumstances, personal goals, communications, documents, and connected external accounts, it must not treat user data casually. The platform should have permission to learn from data, but that permission must exist within clear legal, ethical, and practical boundaries.
The user should understand that the platform uses their information to support their journey. This means that the platform may analyse their goals, progress, actions, conversations, uploaded documents, connected accounts, mentor interactions, and other relevant information in order to provide better guidance and make better decisions on their behalf.
However, this must not be hidden from the user. The platform should explain, in plain language, what data is being used, why it is being used, and how it benefits the user.
The basic rule should be:
The user should never feel that the platform is secretly using their information against them. The user should understand that the platform uses their information to help them move forward.
This is especially important because the platform is not just a passive tool. It may actively support users, guide them, analyse their situation, identify opportunities, connect them with mentors, prepare outreach, interact with accepted external platforms, and help them take action. That makes trust essential.
The platform should therefore separate data use into clear categories.
First, there is data used directly to support the individual user. This includes information needed to understand the user’s goals, stage, problems, strengths, weaknesses, preferences, and next steps. This type of data use should be part of the normal functioning of the platform.
Second, there is data used to improve the platform. This may include learning from patterns across users, such as where people commonly get stuck, which interventions work, which mentor recommendations are useful, which journeys create better outcomes, and which parts of the platform need improvement. Where possible, this should be done through anonymised or aggregated data.
Third, there is data from connected accounts and external platforms. This is more sensitive, because the platform may act through the user’s own accounts on platforms that are accepted as legitimate. For example, if the platform connects with a user’s account on a mentor platform, communication platform, email system, document system, or similar service, the platform must only act within the user’s permission and within the rules of that external platform.
The platform should not pretend to be the user in a deceptive way. It may act on the user’s behalf, but only where the user has authorised this and where the action is consistent with the user’s intention.
For example, the platform may help draft outreach messages, suggest mentors, prepare communication, organise documents, or recommend next steps. In some cases, it may also send or execute actions on behalf of the user, but this should depend on the sensitivity of the action and the level of permission granted by the user.
A useful distinction should be made between low-risk, medium-risk, and high-risk actions.
Low-risk actions could be automated more easily. For example, organising information, summarising progress, detecting incomplete steps, or suggesting next actions.
Medium-risk actions may require user review. For example, preparing a message to a mentor, updating a profile, or selecting which external opportunity to pursue.
High-risk actions should require explicit user approval. For example, sending important legal communication, making financial commitments, submitting formal applications, contacting professionals on the user’s behalf, or sharing sensitive personal documents.
The platform should therefore not have unlimited freedom to act. It should have structured permission levels.
The user should be able to decide how much autonomy the platform has. Some users may want the platform to make suggestions only. Others may want the platform to prepare actions for approval. More advanced users may allow the platform to carry out certain actions automatically within agreed limits.
This creates a balance between automation and control.
The platform should aim to reduce the amount of effort required from the user, but not remove the user’s rights or awareness. The ideal situation is not that the user has to micromanage every step. The ideal situation is that the user can trust the platform to act intelligently within clearly defined boundaries.
The platform should also avoid using data in ways that are unrelated to the user’s progress or the company’s mission. For example, user data should not be sold casually to third parties, used for manipulative advertising, or exploited in ways that do not clearly benefit the user. If data is shared with third parties, this should only happen when there is a legitimate reason, proper protection, and clear disclosure.
The ethical boundary should be:
The platform may learn from the user, but it may not exploit the user.
The platform should also give users rights over their data. Users should be able to view important information the platform holds about them, correct inaccurate information, disconnect external accounts, withdraw certain permissions, request deletion where legally possible, and understand what consequences this may have for the functionality of the platform.
At the same time, the platform should be honest that certain data is necessary for the service to work properly. If a user refuses to share essential information, the platform may not be able to provide accurate guidance, personalised support, or automated action. That should be explained clearly and respectfully.
In practical terms, the platform should follow these rules:
                                                                                                                                             * Use data only when there is a clear purpose.
                                                                                                                                             * Explain data use in understandable language.
                                                                                                                                             * Ask for permission where permission is required.
                                                                                                                                             * Give users control over connected accounts.
                                                                                                                                             * Require approval for sensitive or high-impact actions.
                                                                                                                                             * Use anonymised or aggregated data where individual identity is not needed.
                                                                                                                                             * Protect sensitive information with strong security.
                                                                                                                                             * Avoid unnecessary data collection.
                                                                                                                                             * Do not sell or exploit user data in ways that conflict with the user’s interests.
                                                                                                                                             * Allow users to disconnect, correct, or delete data where appropriate.
The platform’s long-term success depends heavily on trust. If users feel watched, exploited, or manipulated, they will not share the information the platform needs in order to help them. But if users understand that the platform uses their data responsibly to support their progress, they are more likely to trust it, engage with it, and benefit from it.
Therefore, the answer to 12.2 should be that the platform may use user data broadly, but not without boundaries. The platform should be transparent, permission-based, secure, proportionate, and mission-driven.
The goal is not to collect as much data as possible. The goal is to use the right data, in the right way, for the right reason: helping the user move forward while improving the platform’s ability to create meaningful outcomes.
	 
 
12.3
Are there any assessment areas that are ethically sensitive — financial vulnerability, mental health indicators, personal debt? If yes, how should the platform handle those with extra care?
The platform must treat security and privacy as core product requirements, not as secondary legal details. This is especially important because the platform may connect to users’ accounts on external platforms and may act on their behalf within accepted services.
The platform is not only storing ordinary profile information. It may handle sensitive and practical information about a user’s goals, business ideas, personal situation, financial position, legal problems, documents, communications, mentor relationships, and progress. In some cases, the platform may also use the user’s connected accounts to perform actions on external platforms.
That creates a higher duty of care.
The basic principle should be:
The platform may act on behalf of the user, but it must never create unnecessary risk, confusion, or loss of control for the user.
This means that security should be built into the design from the beginning. The platform should not first focus on automation and only later think about protection. The more useful and autonomous the platform becomes, the more important security becomes.
There are several areas that must be handled carefully.
1. Connected Accounts
The platform may connect to accounts on accepted external platforms. These could include mentor platforms, email systems, document platforms, communication platforms, or other legitimate services that help the user move forward.
For example, the platform may connect to a user’s account on a platform like MicroMentor, where it can help identify relevant mentors, prepare outreach, track communication, and support follow-up.
However, this access must be permission-based.
The user should know which account is connected, what the platform can access, what actions the platform can perform, and how to disconnect the account. The user should not be surprised by what the platform can see or do.
The platform should also avoid unnecessary account access. If read-only access is enough, it should not request permission to write or send messages. If temporary access is enough, it should not request permanent access. If limited access is enough, it should not request full account control.
The rule should be:
The platform should only request the access it needs, and only use that access for the purpose the user has agreed to.
2. Acting on Behalf of the User
A major part of the platform’s value is that it can reduce the user’s workload. It should not only tell the user what to do; where appropriate, it should help prepare, organise, and execute actions.
However, acting on behalf of the user must be handled with strict boundaries.
There should be different levels of action:
Suggestion only
The platform analyses the situation and recommends what the user should do.
Prepared for approval
The platform prepares a message, application, document, mentor request, or other action, but the user must approve it before anything is sent or submitted.
Limited automation
The platform may perform certain agreed actions automatically, such as organising information, checking for updates, tracking progress, or sending low-risk follow-ups.
Explicit approval required
The platform must ask for direct permission before performing high-impact actions, such as submitting formal applications, contacting lawyers, sending legal or financial information, sharing sensitive documents, making commitments, or sending important communication.
The platform should not treat all actions the same. Sending a harmless reminder is not the same as sending a legal email, contacting a mentor, submitting a document, or sharing private information.
Therefore, the platform should classify actions by risk and require approval where needed.
3. Sensitive Information
The platform may process information that is personal, financial, strategic, emotional, legal, or otherwise sensitive.
This type of information should be protected more carefully than ordinary usage data.
Examples of sensitive information may include:
                                                                                                                                             * Legal problems or disputes.
                                                                                                                                             * Government-related issues.
                                                                                                                                             * Financial hardship or funding needs.
                                                                                                                                             * Personal identity information.
                                                                                                                                             * Private business ideas.
                                                                                                                                             * Uploaded documents.
                                                                                                                                             * Communications with mentors, lawyers, officials, or support organisations.
                                                                                                                                             * Evidence, contracts, applications, or formal letters.
                                                                                                                                             * Notes about weaknesses, personal circumstances, or obstacles.
The platform should not expose this information unnecessarily. It should not share sensitive information with mentors, professionals, external platforms, or third parties unless there is a clear reason and, where needed, user approval.
The platform should also be careful when summarising or interpreting sensitive information. A bad summary can harm the user if it creates a wrong impression, leaves out context, or presents an issue too strongly or too weakly.
For sensitive matters, the platform should prefer accuracy over speed.
4. Audit Trail and Traceability
Because the platform may act on behalf of users, every important action should be traceable.
The platform should keep a clear record of:
                                                                                                                                             * What action was taken.
                                                                                                                                             * When it was taken.
                                                                                                                                             * Which account or external platform was involved.
                                                                                                                                             * Whether the user approved it.
                                                                                                                                             * What information was shared.
                                                                                                                                             * What message or document was sent.
                                                                                                                                             * What result came back.
This is important for trust, accountability, and troubleshooting.
If a user later asks, “Why did the platform send this?” or “Where did this information go?”, the platform should be able to answer clearly.
Traceability is especially important when dealing with mentors, lawyers, government agencies, funding organisations, formal applications, or sensitive personal data.
The user should not have to guess what happened.
5. Data Minimisation
The platform should not collect data simply because it might be useful someday. It should collect what is needed to provide the service properly.
This matters because too much data creates unnecessary risk. The more information the platform stores, the greater the consequences if something goes wrong.
The platform should therefore follow a data minimisation principle:
Collect what is necessary, keep what is useful, remove what is no longer needed, and avoid storing sensitive information without purpose.
This does not mean the platform should be afraid to use data. The platform needs data to be effective. But it should be disciplined and intentional about what it collects and why.
6. User Control
The user should remain in control of their connected accounts and important data.
The user should be able to:
                                                                                                                                             * See which external accounts are connected.
                                                                                                                                             * Disconnect accounts.
                                                                                                                                             * Review important actions taken by the platform.
                                                                                                                                             * Change permission levels.
                                                                                                                                             * Delete or correct certain information.
                                                                                                                                             * Withdraw approval where possible.
                                                                                                                                             * Decide whether the platform may act automatically or only prepare suggestions.
                                                                                                                                             * See what information has been shared externally.
This is not only a legal or compliance issue. It is also a product trust issue.
If users feel they have no control, they will not trust the platform with meaningful information. If they feel protected and informed, they are more likely to allow the platform to help them deeply.
7. Platform Approval of External Services
The platform should not connect to every possible external service by default.
It should only support platforms that are considered legitimate, useful, and compatible with the platform’s purpose. For example, a mentor platform like MicroMentor may be relevant because it helps users connect with mentors. Other platforms may be useful for communication, documents, project management, education, business development, legal help, or funding.
Before allowing an integration, the platform should consider:
                                                                                                                                             * Is the external platform legitimate?
                                                                                                                                             * Does it help the user’s journey?
                                                                                                                                             * Is it safe to connect?
                                                                                                                                             * Does it allow this kind of access under its terms?
                                                                                                                                             * What risks are created by connecting?
                                                                                                                                             * What permissions are required?
                                                                                                                                             * Can the user disconnect it easily?
                                                                                                                                             * Can the platform act transparently on the user’s behalf?
The platform should not connect to suspicious, exploitative, illegal, or manipulative services.
8. Protection Against Misuse
Because the platform may automate outreach and action, it must also prevent misuse.
It should not become a spam machine. It should not allow users to mass-message mentors, professionals, or organisations in a careless way. It should not help users misrepresent themselves. It should not send misleading information. It should not create fake identities or deceptive communication.
Automation must support genuine progress, not abuse.
The rule should be:
The platform should help users act more effectively, not help them act irresponsibly.
This is important for protecting the user, the platform, mentors, professionals, and external ecosystems.
9. Human Review for High-Stakes Situations
For high-stakes matters, the platform should not rely entirely on automation.
This includes legal matters, government communication, financial commitments, formal applications, sensitive personal disclosures, and important professional outreach.
In these situations, the platform may still provide strong support. It can organise facts, draft communication, identify missing information, prepare documents, explain options, and recommend next steps. But final action should often require user approval or professional review.
The platform should be especially careful not to create the impression that it is replacing licensed professionals where professional advice is legally required.
The platform can support the user, but it should not recklessly overstep.
10. Security as a Competitive Advantage
Strong security should not be seen only as a cost. It should be part of the platform’s value.
Users are more likely to trust a platform that clearly protects them. Mentors, lawyers, public organisations, business partners, and external platforms are also more likely to cooperate with a platform that handles data responsibly.
Security can therefore become part of the platform’s credibility.
The long-term ambition should be that users can say:
“This platform helps me move forward, but it also protects me while doing so.”
That balance is essential.
Final Position
The answer to 12.3 should be that the platform must have strict security, privacy, and external account access rules because it may handle sensitive information and act on behalf of users.
The platform should be able to connect to approved external services and use those accounts to support the user’s journey, but only within clear permissions, clear boundaries, and clear traceability.
The platform should not ask for more access than it needs. It should not act secretly. It should not share sensitive information without a proper reason. It should not automate high-risk actions without approval. It should not connect to untrusted platforms. It should not exploit user data.
The platform should be powerful, but controlled.
The goal is to create a system that can genuinely reduce the burden on the user while still keeping the user protected, informed, and in control.
	 
 
12.4
Should users be able to delete all of their data? If they do, what happens to the benchmark data their responses contributed to?
12.4 Should users be able to delete all of their data? If they do, what happens to the benchmark data their responses contributed to?
Yes. Users should be able to delete all personal data connected to their account.
This should include their profile data, uploaded documents, connected-account tokens, answers, notes, drafts, case information, journey history, mentor-outreach history, internal recommendations, and any other data that can reasonably be linked back to them.
This is especially important because the platform may handle sensitive information about legal problems, finances, business ideas, personal struggles, government issues, housing problems, and communications with third parties. A user must be able to leave the platform without feeling that their personal situation remains permanently stored inside the system.
Under GDPR-style principles, users have a right to request erasure of personal data in several situations, although the right is not absolute in every case. Organisations may sometimes need to retain limited records for legal obligations, legal claims, or other permitted purposes. (European Commission)
The platform should therefore offer a clear “delete my data” option, but it should also explain exactly what deletion means.
What should be deleted?
When a user deletes their account, the platform should delete or disconnect:
                                                                                                                                             * The user account.
                                                                                                                                             * Personal profile information.
                                                                                                                                             * Uploaded files and documents.
                                                                                                                                             * Connected-account credentials, access tokens, and permissions.
                                                                                                                                             * Raw answers given by the user.
                                                                                                                                             * Raw journey data connected to that user.
                                                                                                                                             * Drafts, notes, recommendations, and case files connected to that user.
                                                                                                                                             * Personal history of mentor outreach, legal outreach, or other external platform activity stored inside this platform.
                                                                                                                                             * Any internal identifiers that still allow the platform to connect benchmark contributions back to that user.
The platform should also revoke access to connected external accounts wherever technically possible.
However, the platform should be clear that it cannot necessarily delete information that already exists on third-party platforms. For example, if a message was already sent through an external mentor platform, email account, lawyer directory, or government portal, the platform can delete its own copy and revoke its access, but the receiving party or external platform may still retain their own copy.
What happens to benchmark data?
The correct approach is:
The user’s raw personal data should be deleted, but properly anonymised benchmark data may remain.
Benchmark data should be treated differently depending on whether it is still personal data.
If the benchmark data still contains anything that can identify the user, directly or indirectly, then it should be deleted or anonymised when the user deletes their account.
If the benchmark data has been irreversibly anonymised, aggregated, and separated from the user, then it may remain because it is no longer personal data. The European Commission explains that pseudonymised data can still be personal data if re-identification is possible, while truly anonymous data is no longer personal data only when anonymisation is irreversible. (European Commission) The European Data Protection Board also distinguishes pseudonymised data, which remains personal data, from anonymised data, which is outside GDPR scope when individuals are no longer identifiable by reasonably likely means. (European Data Protection Board)
So the platform should not keep a user’s identifiable answers in the benchmark after deletion. But it may keep aggregate learning such as:
“35% of users at this stage needed help identifying the right mentor category.”
Or:
“Users with incomplete evidence files often need an extra document-collection step before lawyer outreach.”
That kind of benchmark insight can remain, provided it cannot be traced back to the individual user.
Important distinction: raw contribution vs. aggregate benchmark
The platform should separate two layers:
1. Raw user contribution
This is the actual response, file, message, note, or personal journey step submitted by the user. This should be deleted when the user deletes their data.
2. Aggregated benchmark insight
This is statistical or generalised knowledge created from many users. This may remain if it is properly anonymised and no longer possible to identify the user from it.
For example, if Robert answers a question about his legal problem, that raw answer should be deleted when he deletes his account.
